articles.title: The Semianalysis team argues that Claude Code is an inflection point for AI agents; 4% of GitHub public commits are being authored by Claude Code today, and the team projects that will rise to 20% by the end of the year.
articles.author: Doug O'Laughlin, Jeremie Eliahou Ontiveros, Jordan Nanos, Dylan Patel, et al.

articles.title: Google is planning on doubling its capital expenditure in 2026 to ~$175–185B. Google spent $91.4B on capex in 2025.
articles.author: Stephen Morris

articles.title: At ~15%, NYC has the highest combined city-state personal tax rate in the US, and the top marginal corporate income tax rate at 17.4%. The city also has the highest industrial, retail and multifamily property taxes per square foot of 10 major cities.
articles.author: Michael Cembalest

articles.title: David Weil finds that it takes 41 years after a rise in the fertility rate for the “support ratio” to rise above its initial value, allowing for increased consumption - an “unfortunate implication for pro-natalist policies” at the political level.
articles.author: David Weil

articles.title: The crude birth rate in Latin America and the Caribbean (LAC) fell by two-thirds between 1950 and 2023. A cohort analysis shows that the declines in completed fertility are due primarily to fewer children per mother rather than by rising childlessness.
articles.author: Milagros Onofri, Inés Berniell, Raquel Fernández, and Azul Menduiña

articles.title: Kedrosky notes that investors started re-rating software earnings in 2022, around the launch of ChatGPT. Still-high P/E multiples “shouldn't distract from a fundamental revaluation that's been going on for some time, despite an advancing equity market.”
articles.author: Paul Kedrosky

articles.title: A Pew analysis finds U. S. Border Patrol encounters with migrants at the southern border have fallen to their lowest level in more than 50 years. The headline decline is likely understated as it still includes Biden's last four months in office.
articles.author: John Gramlich

articles.title: Survey evidence suggests that the worsened mental health of the young in recent years is particularly concentrated among those who are less-educated and working. For the 2021–2025 cohort of 18–22 year olds, “despair” is higher for workers than non-workers.
articles.author: David Blanchflower and Alex Bryson

articles.title: After reaching a post-GFC peak of ~12.9mm in 2023, the number of US manufacturing jobs has fallen steadily to ~12.7mm, below their pre-pandemic level of ~ 12.8mm.
articles.author: David Uberti

articles.title: Post-1965, measured labor productivity in US construction averaged ~ -0.6% per year, compared to +1.6% economy-wide. GS attributes ~40% of the gap to tighter land-use rules, ~20% to weak innovation, and ~20% to mismeasurement of quality improvements.
articles.author: Elsie Peng

articles.title: Btw 1939 and 1963, the % of Americans below LBJ’s absolute poverty line (3× the cost of a minimal meal plan), fell from 48.5 to 19.5, driven by rising market income. Post-1964, most (for non-blacks, all) drops were the result of transfers, not earnings.
articles.author: Richard Burkhauser and Kevin Corinth

articles.title: US business valuations jumped post-2000 and remain elevated. The Gordon formula still works with free cash flow, but FCF has outpaced earnings as labor’s share fell and firms reinvest less. Intangible capital and/or higher markups likely play a role.
articles.author: Andrew Atkeson, Jonathan Heathcote and Fabrizio Perri

articles.title: Gold has fallen ~15% since its recent peak last week. Gold remains up 9% this year. Market participants attributed the volatility in part to retail buying.
articles.author: Ian Smith, Emily Herbert and William Sandlund

articles.title: Btw 1993 and 2010, children who moved into revitalized public housing that mixed market-rate and subsidized units, stayed a mean 5 years, and earned ~17% higher income as of their late 20s/early 30s. One key channel was exposure to higher-income peers.
articles.author: Raj Chetty, Rebecca Diamond, Thomas Foster, Lawrence Katz, et al.

articles.title: Oracle’s share price has fallen more than 50% from its September 10th peak, driven by concerns about high CAPEX on data centers, debt issuance and Oracle’s links to Open AI.
articles.author: Carmen Reinicke

articles.title: Damodaran’s “intrinsic rate,” the sum of real GDP growth and inflation, has broadly tracked the nominal 10-year Treasury rate since 1954. The 10-year yield of 4.18% at the start of 2026 is “within reach” of the 5.10% intrinsic rate.
articles.author: Aswath Damodaran

articles.title: JTC calculations suggest that properly constructed individual income tax Laffer curves are flat over wide ranges. Modeling shifting across business types and interactions btw various taxes under 2022 law, they find revenue is ~ constant for top rates btw 37% and 55%.
articles.author: Rachel Moore, Brandon Pecoraro and David Splinter

articles.title: 60% of millennials who are married and living apart from their parents at age 30 own a home, compared to 68% of comparable baby boomers. At 30, 42% of millennials are married, versus 64% of boomers.
articles.author: Aziz Sunderji

articles.title: New Census estimates suggest the overall US population grew by 1.8mm or .5% btw July 2024 and July 2025. Net migration was 2.7mm in 2024 and 1.3mm in 2025. They project net migration declining to 321,000 in 2026.
articles.author: Census Staff

articles.title: Evidence from 147 large US and international “runups” in an industry’s portfolio indicates that average post-runup returns are similar to the market average. The higher likelihood of a crash is offset by higher returns absent a crash.
articles.author: Michael Mauboussin and Dan Callahan

articles.title: The sharp rise in Japanese yields may not spark an international crisis. It is partly post-slump normalization, extreme new stimulus may not materialize, and Japan’s debt is partially mitigated by its CA surplus and positive net investment position.
articles.author: John Authers

articles.title: Since 2007, the S&P 500's operating margins have grown 4pp to 16% as a share of revenue. All of the increase has come from tech-related sectors. Operating margins for non‑tech firms have been stable at ~9%.
articles.author: Torsten Sløk

articles.title: Using nominal cash flow projections, the IRR for the S&P 500 was 8.41% on Jan 1. Subtracting the 10-year Treasury rate (4.18%) yields an ERP of 4.23%. Damodaran notes, “The current ERP is almost exactly equal to the average for the 1960–2025.”
articles.author: Aswath Damodaran

articles.title: Performance of Nazi fighter pilots rose as they neared eligibility for a medal and fell off upon receipt, prompting periodic offerings of new medals. This “hierarchical system of status-based incentives” raised net shootdowns by an estimated 5 to 10%.
articles.author: Leonardo Bursztyn, Ewan Rawcliffe and Hans-Joachim Voth

articles.title: In data from the Mexico City metro area over 2003–2009, students who just missed the exam score cutoff for admission to an elite high school have 5.3% higher wages after five years (2.5% after 10 years) than marginally admitted students.
articles.author: Francisco Cabrera-Hernández, Andrew Dustan, Daniel Osuna Gomez and María Padilla-Romo

articles.title: Bridgewater forecasts real US GDP growth of 2.8% in 2026, with AI capex contributing 1.4pp (50%) this year and 1.5pp in 2027.
articles.author: Greg Jensen, Danny DeBois and Adam Zimbler

articles.title: Citing Microsoft's Nadella’s comments on the state of frontier LLMs “at this point it’s a multi-model world” and “token pricing basically drops by a half every three months,” Kedrosky forecasts a “brutally competitive transition where models commoditize.”
articles.author: Paul Kedrosky

articles.title: Markets’ near-certainty that the FFR will not rise between now and the end of 2027 clashes with data on jobs, retail spending, inflation, and household balance sheets showing high employment and upside risks to inflation, Klein argues.
articles.author: Matt Klein

articles.title: John Authers warns that the largest rally in precious metals since the stagflationary 1970s and early 1980s “is a clear sign that something serious is amiss.”
articles.author: John Authers

articles.title: Robin Brooks warns that despite the ~30% fall in the trade-weighted Yen versus the G10 since 2020 and the 200bps rise in Japan’s relative 30-year yield since 2024, Japan’s debt overhang means the yen will continue to depreciate until yields climb further.
articles.author: Robin Brooks

articles.title: A NYT analysis finds there were likely ~230,000 US interior deportations in 2025 more than the total over Biden’s four years. The previous recent peak of interior deportations was less than 200,000 in 2012.
articles.author: Albert Sun

articles.title: Illegal immigration to the US plunged in 2024, nearing zero by year-end, while exits began to accelerate in 2025. FRBD estimates that 2025 net outflows averaged 48,000/month through July, suggesting a cumulative 576,000 exits through the end of the year.
articles.author: Daniel Wilson and Xiaoqing Zhou

articles.title: The downtick in hiring in AI-exposed occupations started 6 months prior to the release of ChatGPT, and is “perfectly” aligned with the start of Fed rate hikes in Spring 2022. Sectors in the most AI-exposed quintile tend to be interest-sensitive.
articles.author: Zanna Iscenko and Fabien Curto Millet

articles.title: Brookings estimates that in 2025, net immigration to the US was btw -10,000 and -295,000, and will be btw 185,000 and -925,000 in 2026. They estimate that sustainable monthly job growth was btw 20k and 50k last year and will be btw -20k and 50k in 2026.
articles.author: Wendy Edelberg, Stan Veuger, and Tara Watson

articles.title: Relative to the mean US worker, unionized construction workers earned a 71% premium in 1973, versus 15% in 2024. Nonunion construction workers earned a 7% premium in 1973, but only 86 cents on the dollar in 2024. 90% of construction workers are nonunion.
articles.author: Ronda Kaysen and Robert Gebeloff

articles.title: An experiment with over 500 professionals found that each year of model progress cut task time ~8%, with 56% of gains driven by increased compute and 44% by algorithmic progress, suggesting that US productivity could rise 20% over the next decade.
articles.author: Ali Merali

articles.title: Investment spending on information processing equipment, software, data centers and R&D drove 39% of American GDP growth in the first 3Q of 2025. Those same categories were responsible for 28% of GDP growth in 2000, during the dot-com boom.
articles.author: Hannah Rubinton and Bontu Ankit Patro
articles.pub: Federal Reserve Bank Of St. Louis

articles.title: In 1950, sales taxes accounted for ~60% of California’s revenue, and personal income taxes accounted for 11%. In recent years, the personal income tax share has risen to ~60%, with the top 1% of California taxpayers responsible for ~40% of the revenue.
articles.author: Michael Moritz

articles.title: Controlling for poverty and demographics, Mississippi has the best outcomes for fourth-grade reading and math, and is near the top in eighth-grade scores. In absolute terms, "eighth graders still rank 41st on national tests for reading, and 35th for math."
articles.author: Sarah Mervosh

articles.title: Productivity, or nonfarm employee output per hour, soared to a 4.9% annualized rate in Q3 - its highest pace since 2023 - back-to-back with a strong Q2, revised upward to 4.1%. Unit labor costs declined by 1.9% as efficiency gains offset wage gains.
articles.author: Vince Golle

articles.title: S&P Global’s base case is that copper demand rises 50% from today’s level by 2040 due to growth from electrification, the AI buildout and global defense spending. That level of demand implies a 10mm ton gap over the period.
articles.author: James Attwood

articles.title: There is little evidence of reshoring as measured by the US manufacturing purchasing managers’ index, which shows that new export orders and imports have been contracting since 2022, and have downticked sharply since Liberation Day.
articles.author: Economist Staff

articles.title: An absolute measure (2.5 to 5x the poverty line) shows that the share of American families in the middle class fell from 35% to 31% btw 1979 and 2024, as middle-class families moved into the upper-middle class, whose share rose from 10% to 31%.
articles.author: Stephen Rose and Scott Winship

articles.title: US blue-collar employment has fallen by 123k jobs, ½ of them in manufacturing, since early 2025 - likely an underestimate, as preliminary estimates of upcoming annual data revisions suggest 100k less jobs in manufacturing and 30k in construction.
articles.author: Joseph Politano

articles.title: Lehman makes the case for restricting sports gambling, marijuana, and pornography. The costs of vice and its regulation are not limited to individual harm. "Vice, and addiction per se, threatens our capacity to act as fully capable humans and citizens."
articles.author: Charles Fain Lehman

articles.title: The oil intensity of US GDP declined 52% btw 1990 and 2024, but remains almost twice that of Germany and France. As of 2023, 90% of energy consumption for American transport and 33% for industrial production was petroleum-based.
articles.author: Michael Cembalest

articles.title: Danish data show that the propensity to become an R&D worker or a “transformative entrepreneur” who pursues innovation is highly correlated with IQ. Productivity growth is driven by the "symbiotic relationship” between such entrepreneurs and R&D workers.
articles.author: Ufuk Akcigit, Harun Alp, Jeremy Pearce, and Marta Prato
articles.pub: Federal Reserve Bank of New York

articles.title: 1.9mm American workers have been out of work for at least half a year. Long-term unemployment at this level is an outlier, as it's generally seen late in a recession or in its aftermath.
articles.author: Tom Porcelli et. al.

articles.title: China's saving remains high, but its investment has slowed. Instead of boosting consumption, the government exports excess saving via proactive depreciation of the yuan. The real effective exchange rate has dropped 11% (19% relative to trend) since 2021.
articles.author: Paul Krugman

articles.title: Despite hyperscaler spending on capex/R&D, debt financing remained well under late 1990s levels as of Q32025. Even with the Q4 surge in AI-related debt, “free cash flow to revenue for most direct AI companies exceeds the S&P 500’s by a wide margin.”
articles.author: Michael Cembalest

articles.title: US labor productivity grew a bit faster in 2019–2024 than in 2007–2019, but fell short of its 2000–2007 rate. Post-1990, output growth has been on a mostly downward trajectory, largely due to a decline in the growth of hours worked.
articles.author: Nathan Modica

articles.title: Twenty-year time series in 87 countries show stable hours of work for prime-age adults over decades; US hours are stable over a century. Almost universally, prime-age males’ decreasing hours are closely offset by rising prime-age female participation.
articles.author: Amory Gethin and Emmanuel Saez

articles.title: Real wages are up 4% since 2019. During that period, the price level has risen 26%, outpaced by average hourly earnings, which grew 30%.
articles.author: Torsten Sløk

articles.title: Though sympathetic to the “reindustrialize America” impulse, and concerned about national security, Miller argues for more careful strategic thought. “Ships and magnets matter, while t-shirts don’t. What we disagree about is everything in between.”
articles.author: Chris Miller

articles.title: The temporary increase in GDP growth due to investment driven by the nearshoring boom has receded, while exports to the US rose. Private consumption in Mexico has completely stagnated, driving GDP growth close to zero.
articles.author: Robin Brooks

articles.title: ~85% of Mexico's exports remain tariff-free under the USMCA, allowing Mexican firms to capture ~25% of the drop in the US-China trade deficit, according to USTR. In the first 11 months of 2025, Mexican manufacturers' exports to the US rose almost 9% y/y.
articles.author: Santiago Pérez and Anthony Harrup

articles.title: According to ADP data, American firms with 1–49 employees have reduced their workforce by almost 200,000 workers since the start of the year, whereas firms with more than 50 workers have increased their workforce by ~700,000.
articles.author: Harriet Torry and Justin Lahart

articles.title: Inequality within 186 distinct US ethnic groups accounts for 96% of total income variation, while between-group inequality contributes only 4%, largely constant across time and regions. For 6 broad ethnic categories, within variation is 97% of the total.
articles.author: Oded Galor and Daniel Wainstock

articles.title: The first official release of the BEA data for Q3 shows US GDP growth of 4.3% at an annualized rate, well above Bloomberg’s read of economists’ consensus prediction of 3.2%.
articles.author: Myles McCormick and Kate Duguid

articles.title: Actual 2025 tariff rates are, to date, far less than statutory rates, but the costs of the tariffs are mostly borne by the US - estimated tariff pass-through to US prices in 2025 is 94%. Tariffs on imports act like a 1 to 2pp tax on US manufacturing.
articles.author: Gita Gopinath and Brent Neiman

articles.title: In 1979, 60th percentile American families earned 43% as much as 95th percentile families - the high end of their "reference group.” Today, they earn 31%. Krugman argues such gaps are driving the “general sense of declining social inclusion.”
articles.author: Paul Krugman

articles.title: More than half of young voters in the US, UK, France and Germany now back “zero sum” parties fueled by the beliefs such as “people only get rich by making others poor” on the left and “immigrants succeed at the expense of the native-born” on the right.
articles.author: John Burn-Murdoch

articles.title: Despite revolutionizing health care with GLP-1 drugs like Wegovy, Novo Nordisk's market cap has fallen two-thirds from its peak, and is currently only 23% that of Eli Lilly, which has now pulled ahead in sales of weight-loss injections.
articles.author: Economist Staff

articles.title: ⅓ of top 1% US income earners as indicated by tax returns fall out a year later and ⅔ are absent a decade later. Over two decades, income variability reduces the top .01% income share by 30% (20% using retained earnings in place of volatile capital gains).
articles.author: David Splinter and Jeff Larrimore

articles.title: Counting non-cash benefits as income would reduce the 19.8mm US households defined as poor by 90%. If the $1.4T in annual Federal poverty spending, including non-cash benefits, were distributed in cash to those households, each would receive $70,000.
articles.author: Phil Gramm and John Early

articles.title: The college/non-college life expectancy gap widened from 2.6–6.3 years btw 1992 and 2019, while county mortality inequality jumped 30% to the detriment of rural areas. Smoking by state predicts ~300 extra deaths per 100k for non-college grads, 0 for grads.
articles.author: Christopher Foote, Ellen Meara, Jonathan Skinner, and Luke Stewart

articles.title: Jacob Savage argues that the widespread discrimination against young white men in “high-status” sectors such as media and tech during the 2010s is a key driver of contemporary political dynamics among young men.
articles.author: Jacob Savage

articles.title: Pettis argues that unless the EU is “willing to dismantle its welfare system and force down wages relative to productivity to restore competitiveness,” it must either accept deindustrialisation, tariffs, or depreciation of the Euro.
articles.author: Michael Pettis

articles.title: The US–EU productivity divergence over the past 25+ years was largely due to the tech sector. Notably, California’s outperformance relative to non-CA USA was even greater.
articles.author: Paul Krugman

articles.title: Since the start of Trump's second term, on a trailing-12-month basis, the federal deficit has declined to ~6% of GDP from 6.4% in 2024, driven by tariff revenue and an increase in personal income receipts due to the rising stock market.
articles.author: Justin Fox

articles.title: German imports from China have risen to ~3.7% of GDP, up from ~3% in Q4 2019. Over that same period, exports to China fell from ~2.7% of GDP to ~1.9%. German manufacturing output is down 14% from its 2017 peak.
articles.author: Tom Fairless and Bertrand Benoit

articles.title: In 2025, 38% of Stanford and 21% of Harvard undergrads were registered as having a disability — up from 5% in 2009; 6% of youth from the richest US school districts, and 1% from the poorest, are now in special support programs.
articles.author: John Burn-Murdoch

articles.title: 82% of NYC emigrants are not moving to Texas or Florida; they are moving to Long Island and Westchester. Aziz Sunderji suggests that lowering housing costs could help the city retain these workers.
articles.author: Aziz Sunderji

articles.title: HUD data on 4.5mm households show a median stay in public or subsidized housing of 10–15 years, often absent disability or old age. The rent cap equal to 30% of income acts as a 30% earnings tax, disincentivising work.
articles.author: Howard Husock and Bruce Meyer

articles.title: 49% of men under the age of 40 without any college experience do not have a spouse or a short or long-term partner, compared to 27% of their female peers and 18% of college-educated women.
articles.author: Daniel Cox

articles.title: Real wages are up 4% relative to just before the pandemic, but Krugman flags three reasons why many Americans are worse off than it appears: high interest rates on consumer credit, lower job-finding rates, and the high prices of starter homes.
articles.author: Paul Krugman

articles.title: First-time undergraduate enrollment in public 4-year colleges has ~ doubled since 1990. In public 2-year and for-profit institutions, enrollment has ~ halved. Stronger labor markets explain 60% of the drop in enrollment in two-year colleges.
articles.author: Joshua Goodman and Joseph Winkelmann

articles.title: Citing Lee/Yoo’s recent research John Burn-Murdoch argues young Americans' recent upticks in “financial risk-taking, leisure spending and reduction in work effort” are responses to perceived changing economic incentives.
articles.author: John Burn-Murdoch

articles.title: Chinese total R&D spending adjusted for PPP was almost on par with the US’s in 2023.
articles.author: Edward White

articles.title: As the 2-year Treasury yield has fallen, the 10y10y forward yield has “remained stubbornly high. Markets are demanding higher risk premia for longer-term Treasuries,” Brooks infers.
articles.author: Robin Brooks

articles.title: China’s share of direct US imports fell from a peak of ~22% in 2017 to ~13% in 2024. Between Liberation Day and July 2025, China’s share fell another 4pp, returning to ~9%, its level at the time of China’s accession to the WTO.
articles.author: Laura Alfaro and Davin Chor

articles.title: The CBO has reduced its forecast of tariff revenue through FY 2035 by 25% to $3T. 2/3 of the downward revision is due to methodological refinements such as an increased estimate of the share of imports from Canada and Mexico covered by the USMCA.
articles.author: Marc Goldwein
articles.pub: Committee for a Responsible Federal Budget

articles.title: Noting the convergence of the employment rates of young college and non-college workers since 2010, Tim Taylor concludes that today, “the main labor advantage [of a college degree] is higher wages after finding a job.”
articles.author: Timothy Taylor

articles.title: Neil Shearing argues that as the global economy fractures, the US is poised to benefit; most countries will ultimately choose the US-centered bloc (currently 50% of PPP-adjusted world GDP) over a China-centered one (currently 32% of world GDP).
articles.author: Martin Wolf

articles.title: The share of native-born prime-age men (25–54) not in the labor force - 12.1% - has returned to its pre-pandemic level, but is elevated relative to prior economic expansions.
articles.author: Steven Camarota and Karen Zeigler

articles.title: De Loecker et al. note that the deleted observations in their paper are small pharmaceutical companies with ~0 sales, unsuitable for production function estimation. With these excluded, the results are invariant to the inclusion of finance and insurance.
articles.author: Jan De Loecker, Jan Eeckhout and Gabriel Unger

articles.title: The gap btw high school and college graduates in the rate at which individuals leave unemployment, either by finding a job or dropping out of the labor force, began to fall around 2000. By 2019, HS grads left unemployment sooner than college grads.
articles.author: Alexander Cline and Barış Kaymak

articles.title: De Loecker et al. found that aggregate markups surged from 18% to ~ 67% from 1980–2014. This comment argues that much of this finding is due to DEU’s deletion of observations with missing values for SG&A, and the rest is limited to Finance and Insurance.
articles.author: C. Lanier Benkard, Nathan Miller and Ali Yurukoglu

articles.title: In September, 44% of middle-income Americans reported they were financially "worse off" than a year ago, while 23% said they were "better off," ~ unchanged from last September, when 48% reported being "worse off" versus 24% who said "better off."
articles.author: Rachel Louise Ensign and Rachel Wolfe

articles.title: A data-driven method to select cost-effective policy interventions, applied to survey responses of ~1,000 people reared in poverty in 3 US cities, confirms the primacy of education, but finds an ~= role for childhood determinants of noncognitive skills.
articles.author: Oren Danieli, Tanaya Devi and Roland Fryer

articles.title: Asia's foreign-exchange reserves have risen $400B this year to ~$8T. The increase has been in part driven by this year’s decline in the dollar and rally in gold prices.
articles.author: Subhadip Sircar

articles.title: Citing Fervo’s 70% y/y reduction in drilling times, a Stanford analysis suggests “the power costs of geothermal will be competitive with rival fuels by 2027.” A Princeton analysis suggests geothermal output could triple US nuclear power by 2050.
articles.author: Economist Staff

articles.title: Tsinghua students and faculty are creating more patents related to machine learning and AI than MIT, Stanford, Princeton and Harvard combined. However, American patents, as measured by citations, are rising in relevance compared to Tsinghua’s.
articles.author: Saritha Rai

articles.title: GS estimates that for every 1pp export-driven increase in Chinese GDP, advanced economies experience a 0.1–0.3pp drag. “China Shock 2.0 is likely to crowd out tech-intensive, high-value-added manufacturing activity in the rest of the world.”
articles.author: Andrew Tilton, Hui Shan, Lisheng Wang, Xinquan Chen, et al.

articles.title: To counter Chinese style “persistent, policy-engineered surpluses—designed, e.g., to boost a country’s manufacturing share by suppressing wage growth,” Pettis proposes a “global customs union open to all countries that commit to balanced trade.”
articles.author: Michael Pettis

articles.title: Btw 1993 and 2023, the share of American female high school seniors who reported wanting to get married “someday” fell from 83% to 61%. Their male counterparts saw just a 2pp decline to 74%.
articles.author: Dana Braga

articles.title: A GS analysis suggests 5–15% of the American population engages in “gig work,” but only 2–4% of the population participates in platform-based gig work like Uber. Overall, platform gig workers work 14 hours a week and earn ~$18 an hour.
articles.author: Jessica Rindels

articles.title: China’s annual generation of electrical power is 2.5x that of the US. China is self-sufficient in electricity via coal and renewables, whereas the US is self-sufficient in terms of oil and gas but faces capacity constraints in generating electricity.
articles.author: Karen Karniol-Tambour, Ian Singer, Daniel Hochman and Jeremy Ng

articles.title: A sharp and steady decline in the GCT scores of Marine Corps officers occurred btw 1980 and 2014. 96% of this is explained by the widening (and weakening) of the applicant pool due to increased college participation. Affirmative action played no role.
articles.author: Matthew Cancian and Michael Klein

articles.title: Purchases of Treasurys in 2025 have been “solid.” Foreign buyers bought a net $375B through July and Treasury’s adjustment of the duration mix by shifting towards bills and maturities in the belly of the curve (~2–10 years) has supported demand.
articles.author: Joseph Wang

articles.title: A Pew study finds that only 15% of Americans trust the Federal government to do what is right “most of the time,” down from ~75% in 1960. A secular drop occurred during the Johnson and Nixon years; economic fluctuations account for cyclical variation.
articles.author: Michael Boskin, Alexander Kleiner and Ian Whiton

articles.title: About 10% of Americans aged 20–24 are not working or seeking work, nor are they in school or raising children, about twice the fraction during the 1990s. This group spends seven hours per day alone, up from five a decade ago.
articles.author: John Burn-Murdoch

articles.title: Sub-Saharan Africa’s population, currently ~1.3B, is forecast to reach ~3.5B in 75 years. ~60% of the population is < 25. Worryingly, a Bloomberg analysis finds that between 2005 and 2020, for every two new working-age persons only one job was created.
articles.author: Michael Cohen, Nduka Orjinmo, Yvonne Mhango, Tom Fevrier, et al.

articles.title: The sum of global exports and global imports relative to global GDP has been stable over the past decade. Robin Brooks argues that despite the ongoing trade wars, “we’re essentially in a holding pattern at peak globalization.”
articles.author: Robin Brooks

articles.title: China's surplus in manufactured goods is now over 2% of global GDP; greater than the combined surpluses of Germany and Japan at their peaks. China's overall goods surplus is $1.2T, $800B higher than it was in 2020.
articles.author: Brad Setser

articles.title: German industrial production is only at its 2005 level, even after a partial rebound in September. Since the start of the year, Germany has run a deficit in capital goods with China, the first since 2008.
articles.author: Olaf Storbeck, Sebastien Ash and Florian Müller

articles.title: JPM estimates that to meet an IRR hurdle of 10% on their forecast AI investment through 2030 would require ~$650B in annual revenue in perpetuity. That would be 58bps of global GDP, or $34.72/month from every current iPhone user.
articles.author: Tarek Hamid, C. Stephen Tusa, Jr., Harlan Sur, et al.

articles.title: Global electricity demand is forecast to rise by 40–50% by 2035. Only 10% of this growth is due to AI. Coal demand, driven by power generation in India and China, is 2x its 2000 level, but will likely peak before 2035 as cleaner energy comes online.
articles.author: Stéphanie Bouckaert, Christophe McGlade, Brent Wanner et al.

articles.title: The debt of several blue-chip firms, including Microsoft in the US, Siemens in Germany and LVHM in France, trades at lower yields than the underlying government benchmark, as “tough fiscal compromises” look unlikely.
articles.author: Finbarr Flynn, Tasos Vossos, and Greg Ritchie

articles.title: Japan has so far avoided inflation and debt crises despite decades of slow growth and deficits that left gov debt at > twice GDP by “running a risky sovereign wealth fund” with maturity and currency mismatch, financed at below-market interest rates.
articles.author: Yili Chien, Wenxin Du, and Hanno Lustig

articles.title: BAC internal data show the share of households devoting more than 95% of household income to “necessity spending” increasing 0.3pp to 24% in the first 3 quarters of 2025 compared to the same period in 2024 - ~⅓ of the previous year’s pp rise.
articles.author: Joe Wadford

articles.title: Voters under the age of 45, 46% of registered voters in New York City, made up ~43% of voters in the mayor’s race. In neighborhoods where the nonwhite population is shrinking, Mamdani won by more than 50pp.
articles.author: Maya King, Jeff Adelson and Alex Lemonides

articles.title: Baker, et al found that a shift to universal child care led to a rise in aggression, anxiety and hyperactivity among Quebecer children. James Heckman, who found very high returns to a small, high quality pilot program, warns against low-cost imitations.
articles.author: Economist Staff

articles.title: The American corporate sector has been substantially self-financing since 2000. Net equity issuance is negative even after taking stock-based compensation expenses into consideration.
articles.author: Michael Mauboussin and Dan Callahan

articles.title: Since 1996 cable news's incentive to attract viewers with exciting cultural news rather than economic news has shifted the political discourse and is in turn responsible for ⅓ of the increased cultural conflict since 2000, Noy and Rao estimate.
articles.author: Shakhed Noy and Aakaash Rao

articles.title: QT, increased t-bill issuance, and the govt shutdown are all draining reserves from the banking system, putting upward pressure on money market spreads. Sløk suggests we may be “nearing the point” of switching from an ample to a scarce reserves regime.
articles.author: Torsten Sløk

articles.title: In the mid-1990s, individuals became more likely to cohabit and less likely to marry. After the financial crisis, remaining single became more common and fertility declined sharply. Dissolution rates for both marriage and cohabitation have declined.
articles.author: Sean Elliott

articles.title: A Bloomberg analysis finds that prices dropped on 51 of 67 products and services in China over the past two years. 34% of Chinese firms are unable to cover interest on their debt - a 25-year high - as China’s deflationary spiral worsens.
articles.author: Bloomberg

articles.title: Btw 1988 and 2023, the US IT sector’s TFP rose by 178%, while TFP in all the non-IT sectors combined rose by only 12%. Taking account of sectoral linkages, the IT sector contributed ~17pp to TFP growth since 1988, and the non-IT sectors about 20pp.
articles.author: Joel David, Gustavo de Souza, Adalyn Schommer

articles.title: The median age of first-time homebuyers in the US has climbed to a record of 40, up from 33 in 2021.
articles.author: Michael Sasso

articles.title: Data from a British birth cohort followed since 1958 show that cognitive skills early in life predict wages at age 50 better than cognitive skills at age 50. Early cognitive skills likely allow for higher educational attainment or on-the-job learning.
articles.author: Uta Bolt

articles.title: Given that deflation is becoming entrenched in China, Robin Brooks argues that the US has the upper hand in the trade war. He cautions that easing up on China's exporters while China's hold on rare earths is undiminished, undermines the US position going forward.
articles.author: Robin Brooks

articles.title: Since January 2020, private sector real hourly earnings have fallen 9% in New York City, while increasing 3% nationally, as large firms based in NYC move jobs to lower-cost states. JP Morgan now has more workers in Texas than in New York.
articles.author: The Economist

articles.title: “Even a few billionaires or marquee employers packing up their bags” could put a significant hole in NYC’s budget, given that the city’s top 1% earners pay 40% of the city's income tax and a significant portion of property taxes.
articles.author: Ben Steverman and Emily Flitter

articles.title: Both the Social Security retirement and Medicare HI trust funds face insolvency in 7 years. Goldwein proposes an Employer Compensation Tax that would “generate enough revenue to close two-thirds of Social Security’s funding gap and half of Medicare’s.”
articles.author: Marc Goldwein
articles.pub: Committee for a Responsible Federal Budget

articles.title: A Bank of America analysis suggests Treasury's anticipated decision to issue more short-term bills may drive the 10-year yield lower by more than 25bps. That decision would likely result in bills accounting for 25% of outstanding debt, up from 20% today.
articles.author: Elizabeth Stanton

articles.title: Product-level data on prices and effective tariff rates show pass-through of tariffs to retail prices increasing gradually, reaching 20% after 6 months. As of August, CPI had risen 2.9%, compared to an estimated 2.2% in the absence of tariffs.
articles.author: Alberto Cavallo, Paola Llamas, and Franco Vazquez

articles.title: John Authers notes that Shiller's CAPE (cyclically adjusted price-earnings multiple) hit 40 for the first time since the dot-com bubble burst in 2000. “It never got close to that level over the preceding century, even on the eve of the 1929 Great Crash.”
articles.author: John Authers

articles.title: Since Powell’s Jackson Hole speech, currencies issued by countries with low debt burdens are outperforming the currencies of high-debt countries. Brooks projects that Sweden's “undervalued” Krona will emerge as a key safe-haven currency going forward.
articles.author: Robin Brooks

articles.title: Noting that over the past decade global CO2 emissions projected by the IEA have fallen by more than 40%, Bill Gates argues innovation will likely “give us the tools we need to meet the growing demand for energy without increasing carbon emissions.”
articles.author: Bill Gates

articles.title: Meta, Microsoft, and Alphabet’s capex as a percent of revenue (21–35%) are now on par with utilities’ global mean and AT&T’s at the height of the telecom bubble. Kai Wu notes the “telecom index still remains -60% below its dot-com high 25 years later.”
articles.author: Kai Wu

articles.title: “Educationally similar” monozygotic (MZ) twins raised apart [TRA] have a mean IQ delta of 5.8 points, ~ the same as those raised together. “Educationally dissimilar” MZ TRA have a delta of 12.8 points, closer to non-twin siblings than twins reared together.
articles.author: Jared Horvath and Katie Fabricant

articles.title: External renminbi loans, deposits, and bond investments have quadrupled over the past five years to $484B, approaching a third of China’s $1.5T of overseas fixed-income assets. Much of the expansion is in the form of trade finance.
articles.author: William Sandlund and Haohsiang Ko

articles.title: In 2024, solar and wind generated 15% of global electrical production and 63% of the growth of electrical output since 2019.
articles.author: Paul Krugman

articles.title: As AI-related real private fixed investment has surged, non-AI-related investment’s contribution to US real private fixed investment has turned negative.
articles.author: Shawn Donnan, Brooke Sutherland, Mark Niquette, and Alexandre Tanzi

articles.title: A 30-year-old immigrant without a high school degree yields net revenue losses of $20k to the federal government over 10 years and >$130k over 30 years, while his college-educated peer yields fiscal gains of $200k over 10 years and $1.6mm over 30 years.
articles.author: Daniel Di Martino

articles.title: The cost per watt of the newest nuclear plants in the US is $15, relative to $1.67-$2.58 per watt for Chinese nuclear plants under construction. Since 2013, the US has built two new nuclear reactors while China has built 13 and has 33 underway.
articles.author: Brad Plumer and Harry Stevens

articles.title: The global stock of investable assets is valued at $261T, or ~ 200% of world GDP, up from ~75% in the 1990s. The weight of equity relative to bonds is higher than it was post-GFC, but below 1990s levels.
articles.author: Christian Mueller-Glissmann, Andrea Ferrario, Alessandro Giglio and Peter Oppenheimer

articles.title: Among religious Americans aged 18–39, 57% and 66% of women and men, respectively, report wanting children someday, compared to 33% and 31% of their non-religious peers.
articles.author: Daniel Cox

articles.title: A Goldman analysis suggests restoring housing affordability to late 1990s levels will require construction of 3–4mm new units, equivalent to 2–2.6% of the current housing stock.
articles.author: Elsie Peng and Pierfrancesco Mei

articles.title: Weak labor demand is not keeping pace with “modest” labor supply growth. Outside of the healthcare sector, job growth is now net negative. Mericle and Mei note that the impact of AI on the labor market may “not become apparent until a recession hits.”
articles.author: David Mericle and Pierfrancesco Mei

articles.title: Between 2017 and 2021, only 17% of $150B of Chinese trade financing was denominated in RMB. Since 2022, $100B in trade financing has been RMB denominated, opening up an avenue for China to de-dollarize.
articles.author: Martin Lynge Rasmussen

articles.title: At current 5-year yields, stabilizing debt/GDP would require a reduction in the primary deficit of 2.3% of GDP in the UK and the US, and 3% in France. Maturing American debt will be refinanced at rates ~200bps higher than today’s.
articles.author: Henry Curr

articles.title: In 2025H2, BoJ was a net seller of JGB’s to the tune of 2.6% of GDP, as maturing debt exceeded mean gross monthly purchases of ¥4.7T. A planned reduction in purchases to a mean of ¥2T “will likely send longer-term yields even higher.”
articles.author: Robin Brooks

articles.title: 43% of American adults say widespread betting on sports is "a bad thing for society," up from 34% in 2022. 47% of men under 30 say legal sports betting is bad for society, up from 22% in 2022.
articles.author: John Gramlich

articles.title: The US built 888 miles of new high-voltage transmission lines in 2024, less than the 2015–2019 mean of 900 miles and the 2009–2014 mean of 1,700 miles.
articles.author: Jennifer Hiller

articles.title: The US imports 80 to 100% of critical minerals, of which 50 to 100% come from China. If new Chinese controls on mineral exports remain in place, Cembalest argues, “the eventual impact on US military, industrial and renewable energy output would be huge.”
articles.author: Michael Cembalest

articles.title: Adjusting earnings with the relatively standard PCEPI deflator, Winship finds that real earnings of year-round male workers aged 15+ were flat from 1973–79, but grew 13% from 1989–2023. With his experimental MACPI deflator, they grew 40% over 1973–2023.
articles.author: Scott Winship

articles.title: The rate of violent crime in urban areas increased 61% between 2019 and 2024, compared to increases of just 4% in suburban areas and 2% in rural areas over that period, according to the National Crime Victimization Survey.
articles.author: Jeffrey Anderson

articles.title: China’s DeepSeek costs 20–40x less per million “input tokens” than OpenAI’s ChatGPT.
articles.author: Derek Chollet, Terah Lyons, Lisa Sawyer and Kip Wainscott

articles.title: Gold’s spot price has risen ~59% since the start of the year. Since Powell’s Jackson speech, gold has risen ~25% while the dollar has remained stable against G10 currencies in a broad debasement of advanced economies’ currencies relative to gold.
articles.author: Robin Brooks

articles.title: According to an IMF analysis, global imbalances grew in 2025 but are poised to narrow as the unwinding of pull-forward behavior associated with the implementation of tariffs dissipates.
articles.author: IMF Staff

articles.title: Robin Brooks argues China's record good surplus overstates the strength of its export sector. Brooks suggests that the effect of tariffs has been to suppress Chinese margins, as exporters cut prices to sell to the non-US market.
articles.author: Robin Brooks

articles.title: Over 2019–2024, 2nd-generation immigrant black female workers had earnings as high, or higher, than (non-hispanic) white women; 2nd-generation black male workers earned 10% < these white workers at the median, but this gap disappears at the top decile.
articles.author: Rong Fu, Neeraj Kaushal, and Felix Muchomba

articles.title: The CBO estimates that the deficit in FY 2025 was (-)1,809B, ~unchanged from the previous year. Both receipts and outlays rose by ~$300B. Customs duties (including tariffs) rose by $118B (153%). Net interest on the debt surpassed $1T for the first time.
articles.author: Kathleen Burke and Aldo Prosperi

articles.title: Unemployment for recent American college graduates is up 1.3pp since 2022. Unemployment for the generally younger group of non-college workers, measured at labor market entrance rather than at ages 22-27, has seen a 2.4pp rise.
articles.author: John Burn-Murdoch

articles.title: Paul Kedrosky argues the AI buildout may be a bubble, as unlike the railroad and dot-com booms, "capital destruction isn’t merely tolerated; it is sanctified in advance...the line between necessary excess and outright mania disappears."
articles.author: Paul Kedrosky

articles.title: "Encounters" by Border Patrol agents on the southwest land border are at the lowest level in decades and "almost no one is travelling north through the Darién Gap."
articles.author: Economist Staff

articles.title: The value of foreign central banks' gold holdings, currently $3.83T, will likely soon eclipse the value of their Treasury holdings, now $3.92. Gold has already overtaken the euro as central banks’ second-largest asset.
articles.author: Jack Ryan and Mark Burton

articles.title: Citing a median 24m fwd P/E ratio across the Mag7 of 27x (26x net Tesla) relative to their comps 52x during the Tech Bubble, Oppenheimer argues valuations are “not at levels that are as high as are typically seen at the height of a financial bubble."
articles.author: Peter Oppenheimer, Sharon Bell, Guillaume Jaisson and Giovanni Ferrannini

articles.title: 10-year gilt yields are the highest across the G7. Goldman estimates the UK needs a fiscal consolidation of ~ 3% of GDP to attain a primary balance of ~1% to stabilize debt.
articles.author: Sven Jari Stehn and Fausto Martin

articles.title: A new combined cycle-gas plant cost ~$800 a kilowatt in 2021, but is ~$2,600–2,800 today due to supply constraints. Siemens, GE Vernova and Mitsubishi Heavy account for 70% of global gas turbine production capacity and are struggling to increase capacity.
articles.author: Stephen Stapczynski, Akshat Rathi, and Josh Saul

articles.title: French 10-Year OATS now trade at a higher spread than Italy’s equivalent BTPs. John Authers warns that the relative calm is only due to investors' belief that France is too big to fail and the ECB will intervene, implying "a long and steady debasement."
articles.author: John Authers

articles.title: Long Japanese yields spiked, and the Yen fell in response to the likely new prime minister’s “reputation as a fiscal dove.” Robin Brooks describes the divergence btw 10y20y fwd and simple 10-year yields as “scary.”
articles.author: Robin Brooks

articles.title: Tariffs generated $30B in August 2025; $27B of that was new revenue, and the current regime could raise ~$2T over the next decade. Clausing/Obstfeld’s analysis finds that the welfare losses associated with the tariffs offset over a third of the revenue.
articles.author: Kimberly Clausing and Maurice Obstfeld

articles.title: The market-implied 10-year real yield expected ten years from now is ~ 3%, its highest level since 2010, and ~2x what it was in early 2023. The real 20-year yield expected in 20 years is even higher, signalling high real yields for many years to come.
articles.author: Jesper Rangvid

articles.title: Goldman estimates overall American power demand is accelerating to a 2.5% CAGR (a growth rate not seen since the 1990s), ~ 40% of which is driven by datacenters. They estimate that 60% of the new generation capacity will be sourced from natural gas.
articles.author: Eric Sheridan, James Schneider, Kash Rangan, Gabriela Borges, et al.

articles.title: Since Powell’s Jackson speech, gold has risen 16% while the dollar has remained stable against G10 currencies in a broad debasement of advanced economies’ currencies relative to gold.
articles.author: Robin Brooks

articles.title: The recent rise in a dissimilarity index of the occupational mix between college graduates aged 20 to 24 and those aged 25 to 34 suggests a possible impact of AI on early career workers.
articles.author: Martha Gimbel, Molly Kinder, Joshua Kendall, and Maddie Lee

articles.title: Linking the decline in the share of US output devoted to goods rather than services to the twin budget and trade deficits, Warshawsky stresses the role of the budget deficit in financing the expansion of the low-productivity-growth healthcare sector.
articles.author: Mark Warshawsky

articles.title: Paul Kedrosky notes that, since August, GPU rental rates in the US have declined 4%. He writes that a flood of liquidity from a robust securitization market has driven “a flywheel of data center development disconnected from true demand.”
articles.author: Paul Kedrosky

articles.title: Bain notes that anticipated capital investment in data centers is $500B annually.  Bain’s analysis of sustainable ratios of capex to revenue for cloud service providers suggests that $500B of annual capex corresponds to $2T in annual revenue and cost savings, a number well short of their most optimistic scenario.
articles.author: David Crawford, Anne Hoecker, and Dana Aulanier

articles.title: FDI flows into China have decreased, while flows into other emerging markets remain steady. Robin Brooks suggests "China’s status as an export hub for the world looks increasingly challenged over the medium term."
articles.author: Robin Brooks

articles.title: The fraction of a county’s adults on SSDI in 1990 predicts growth in its drug death rate after 2000 because it is a measure of the county’s pain level. Had opioid prescribing practices stayed at 1995 levels, drug deaths in 2015 would have been 43% lower.
articles.author: William Evans and Ethan Lieber

articles.title: John Burn-Murdoch argues Guenther’s "representation gap" theory is “visible in the US,” where the mean voter’s preferences on immigration are “far more conservative than those of Democratic party elites," and closer to those of Republican politicians.
articles.author: John Burn-Murdoch

articles.title: An Australian analysis of the top 10% of high-quality scientific publications found Chinese researchers have surpassed American researchers across 8 critical technology domains.
articles.author: Justin Riggi

articles.title: Since the launch of ChatGPT in November 2022, AI related stocks have accounted for 75% of S&P 500 returns, 79% of earnings growth, and 90% of capital spending (Capex and R&D) growth.
articles.author: Michael Cembalest

articles.title: Looking fwd 50 years, Mankiw forecasts “heavy handed" state intervention in competitive US markets (such as industrial policy, tariffs and minimum wages) will decline, arguing “The arc of the economic universe is long, but it bends toward efficiency.”
articles.author: Greg Mankiw

articles.title: Fewer exits from buyout funds have “slowed fund distributions so dramatically that, at the current rate, it would take about nine years” for US buyout fund LP’s to collect their money from the more than 12,000 companies held by US buyout funds.
articles.author: Allison McNeely, Preeti Singh, and Laura Benitez

articles.title: In the US, prices of private vehicles have dropped 40% (quality adjusted) since 1995, but costs of US-produced transit buses have remained flat, exceeding foreign prices. A duopoly in manufacturing, customization, and “Buy America” rules keep costs high.
articles.author: Léa Bou Sleiman, Edward Glaeser, Julia Shephard, and Samantha Silverberg

articles.title: So far this year, China has installed 277 gigawatts of wind and solar capacity, 4x the scale of "utility-scale additions," via all power sources the US will bring online in 2025. Chinese firms now have a ~75% market share in clean energy manufacturing.
articles.author: David Uberti, Ed Ballard, and Brian Spegele

articles.title: Bloomberg estimates that China's trade surplus will reach a record $1.2T in 2025, ~ 20% above its 2024 surplus of just under ~$1T.
articles.author: Bloomberg Staff

articles.title: I debate @JasonFurman—Pres. Obama’s Chair of the Council of Economic Advisors—at Harvard over the effect of tax increases on the expected value of innovative risk-taking and the magnitude of changes needed to equilibrate America’s fiscal policy.
articles.author: Edward Conard

articles.title: 11% of American men 25–54 are neither employed nor looking for work. The destruction of low-skilled, “male-coded” jobs, Krugman argues, has led these men to look for support from parents and female partners.
articles.author: Paul Krugman

articles.title: Americans aged 22–45 are seeking financial stability before marrying, shifting from marriage as a starting point to an achievement after career and wealth building. This change delays marriage, increases selectivity, and lowers the overall marriage rate.
articles.author: Rachel Wolfe

articles.title: Efforts by advanced economies to offset demographic decline via immigration have been met with widespread public opposition. Slower growth associated with lower immigration likely means “ever greater demands on the purse of the native-born population.”
articles.author: Stephen Bush and John Burn-Murdoch

articles.title: In county survey data of 12,316 female respondents born btw 1985 and 1989, those who identify as conservative have a completed fertility rate of 2.1, relative to moderates’ rates of 1.9, and liberals’ 1.7.
articles.author: Scott Yenor and Lyman Stone

articles.title: Robin Brooks argues that prior to the pandemic, US debt service was “manageable” even as debt soared, as older, higher-coupon debt was rolled over into lower-coupon debt. That dynamic has now reversed.
articles.author: Robin Brooks

articles.title: A 10-percentile increase in a person's polygenic index (PGI) raises that person's income by 0.9 percentiles and that of their child by 0.7 percentiles; ~1/2 is direct genetic transmission, and ~ 1/2 the effect of parent’s genes on nurturing capacity.
articles.author: Sjoerd van Alten, Silvia Barcellos, Leandro Carvalho, Titus Galama, et al.

articles.title: Btw 2010 and 2023, enrollment at US colleges declined from 17.3mm to 14.1mm. 90% of the decline was driven by falling enrollment in the bottom two quintiles of schools. The top two quintile schools gained enrollment by 3% and 8%, respectively.
articles.author: Preston Cooper

articles.title: Mark Zandi finds Americans in the top 10% of the income distribution accounted for 49.2% of consumer spending in Q2, the highest level since 1989.
articles.author: Jonnelle Marte

articles.title: Chang et al find that in the PSID, higher parental income and living in an intact family during middle childhood and adolescence are more predictive of favorable child outcomes than income and family structure during early childhood.
articles.author: Yoosoon Chang, Steven Durlauf, Bo Hu, and Joon Park

articles.title: Price wars left 8 Chinese carmakers profitable in the first half of the year. A senior executive at BYD expects 80% of China’s 120 carmakers to be “pushed out” of the market as the government’s “involution” campaign takes hold.
articles.author: Edward White, Gloria Li, and Kana Inagaki

articles.title: A comparison of German and Swiss bond yields suggests the dramatic extent to which Germany has suffered a loss of both its safe haven status and its prestige, as its ability to confront its fiscal deterioration and that of its neighbors has declined.
articles.author: Robin Brooks

articles.title: Apart from initial volatility in gold and pharma imports, US tariffs have yet to impact net trade flows. Soaring imports from Taiwan and SE Asia led to growth in total imports from Asia to the US in spite of reduced trade with China.
articles.author: Brad Setser

articles.title: If Japan sticks with low interest rates, it faces a further drop in the yen and worsening inflation. Rising yields put Japan’s debt sustainability at risk. “This catch-22 means a debt crisis is much closer than people think,” Brooks argues.
articles.author: Robin Brooks

articles.title: GS data show that at least 10 French firms are trading at a negative spread to the French sovereign. “Sovereign borrowing costs typically act as a baseline for yields on corporate bonds.”
articles.author: Ian Smith, Emily Herbert, and Olaf Storbeck

articles.title: Since 1970, cumulative real income growth for pensioners in France and the UK has outpaced that of the workers who support them. French pensioners over the age of 65 have higher incomes than working-age adults.
articles.author: John Burn-Murdoch

articles.title: Brooks expresses alarm at the “unprecedented” sharp rise in market-implied Japanese and European forward yields at the long end, “as the debt crisis in the Eurozone still festers.” The “less worrying” picture for the US is likely a safe haven effect.
articles.author: Robin Brooks

articles.title: CBO forecasts that the US population will increase by only 17mm over the next 25 years. Deaths will exceed births starting in 2031, after which point net population growth will be entirely driven by immigration.
articles.author: Daniel Crown and Katherine Keisler-Starkey

articles.title: Btw 2005 and 2018, both the highest levels and the sharpest increases in scores for Depressive Affect (DA) were among female adolescents who self-identified as “liberal” rather than “conservative,” particularly in the presence of low parental education. Flagged by Tyler Cowen.
articles.author: Catherine Gimbrone, Lisa Bates, Seth Prins and Katherine Keyes

articles.title: High frequency data show that many US workers - especially lower-income, hourly workers - face month-to-month, hours-induced, and within-firm pay fluctuations. Contrary to the Permanent Income Hypothesis, this affects consumption stability and utility.
articles.author: Peter Ganong, Pascal Noel, Christina Patterson, Joseph Vavra, et al.

articles.title: Real median household income was $83,730 in 2024, effectively flat from 2019's $83,260.
articles.author: Melissa Kollar and Zach Scherer

articles.title: As expected, French Prime Minister François Bayrou lost a no-confidence vote this Monday on his proposed budget-cutting measures. French 10-year yields, at 3.47%, are now above those of Greece, and closing in on Italy's 3.51%.
articles.author: Ian Smith, Emily Herbert and Leila Abboud

articles.title: New National Assessment of Educational Progress results show the share of American high school seniors who scored basic or above on national tests fell to the lowest level on record: 68% in reading and 55% in math.
articles.author: Matt Barnum

articles.title: Surveys in 27 European countries show that MPs’ policy views generally match those of their voters on economic issues, but are systematically to the left on cultural issues. This “representation gap” is filled by right-wing populist parties.
articles.author: Laurenz Guenther

articles.title: Since the beginning of 2021, the top 10 stocks in the S&P 500 by market cap accounted for 54% of the index’s increase in market capitalization.
articles.author: Torsten Slok, Rajvi Shah, and Shruti Galwankar

articles.title: In 2022, the present value of Social Security was ~50% of the wealth of American families at the median of the wealth distribution, ~34% at the 75th percentile, and 20% at the 90th. The wealth shares of the top 1% and top 5% both rose by 4pp since 1989.
articles.author: Nadia Karamcheva, Xiaotong Niu, and Julie Topoleski

articles.title: Labor demand and employment remain strong; 80.7% of US residents aged 25–54 have a job - near the recent peak. Hiring, however, has slowed; unemployment for those <25 has risen enough to affect the aggregate unemployment rate.
articles.author: Matt Klein

articles.title: An AEI analysis finds immigrants with a high school degree or less have a negative net fiscal impact. These immigrants are less of a burden than their native peers as they don’t qualify for as much public assistance.
articles.author: Pia Orrenius, Alan Viard, and Madeline Zavodny

articles.title: John Burn-Murdoch hypothesizes that rising populism reflects “an unveiling of the electorate’s unfiltered inclinations,” with extreme views, both left and right, better reflected in social, as opposed to traditional, media.
articles.author: John Burn-Murdoch

articles.title: Heckman argues that contrary to Chetty’s research, local geography has little causal effect on children’s adult outcomes in the US. Studies that find otherwise reflect parental self-selection.
articles.author: James Heckman and Sadegh Eshaghnia

articles.title: Brooks argues that upward pressure on global long bond yields is due to budget deficits and the fading of QE. Noting QE’s strong downward pressure on Japanese yields, he argues “there’s still lots of room for yields to rise from artificially low levels."
articles.author: Robin Brooks

articles.title: An outsized share of the top manufacturing firms in the Fortune 1000 as of 2018 originated circa 1900, apparently the persistent echo of an “industrial revolution,” while the less technology-based retail and wholesale sectors lack a “special generation.”
articles.author: Yueran Ma, Benjamin Pugsley, Haomin Qin and Kaspar Zimmermann

articles.title: Capital spending by the tech sector drove 35–45% of US GDP growth over the past three quarters. Without this capex, Mike Cembalest notes, “US growth might already have converged to ~1% due to weakness in consumption.”
articles.author: Michael Cembalest

articles.title: According to the General Social Survey, the share of Americans (18–64) reporting they had sex weekly dropped from 55% in 1990 to 37% in 2024. 24% of young adults 18–29 report not having sex within the last year.
articles.author: Grant Bailey and Brad Wilcox

articles.title: Amid a global sell-off of long bonds, the 30-year Treasury yield hit 5% until disappointing data on US job openings caused it to retreat to 4.9%, and Japan’s 30-year yield briefly hit a record high.
articles.author: Ian Smith, Leo Lewis and Arjun Neil Alim

articles.title: Deming argues that the drop in headcount of software developers aged 22–25 may merely be a reflection of the experience of a particular cohort constituting only 4 years of observations, not the “canary in the coal mine” suggested by Brynjofsson et al.
articles.author: David Deming

articles.title: A WSJ-NORC poll finds the % of Americans who say they have “a good chance of improving their standard of living” fell to 25%, a record low since 1987. The survey results are more negative than predicted by macro conditions, suggesting unmeasured fears.
articles.author: Lindsay Ellis and Aaron Zitner

articles.title: Jonathon Sine argues China “is moving beyond its breakneck industrial prime, facing similar dilemmas to those America confronted in the 1960s and 70s.” The ratio of science/engineering to humanities undergraduate majors is 2:1 in both the PRC and US.
articles.author: Jonathon Sine

articles.title: Over 2021 - 2024, sectors that were more exposed to LLMs saw significant overall increases in wages and employment following LLMs’ release, mainly for young, educated workers, reflecting cases where AI augments, rather than substitutes for, human effort.
articles.author: Andrew Johnston and Christos Makridis

articles.title: Btw 2019 and 2023, automation helped China increase its export share in labor-intensive sectors. During that period, Chinese manufacturers added ~280,000 industrial robots every year, 50% of the global total.
articles.author: Ryan McMorrow, Haohsiang Ko and William Langley

articles.title: If half of the Nvidia chips sold btw February 2024 and February 2026 are employed in the US, that alone will increase power demand 25 GW. The US added 27GW of energy production in 2023.
articles.author: Economist Staff

articles.title: The 10-year French OATS spread over German bunds yields is nearing the highs sparked by last year’s political crises. France’s finance minister warned that the country may need an IMF bailout like that of the UK in 1976.
articles.author: John Authers

articles.title: Deutsche Bank notes that the German economy has grown 1% since 2017 relative to US growth of 19%. Since 2000, the US share of global market capitalization has held steady at 48% while the EU/UK shares fell from 18% to 10%, and 8.3% to 2.6%, respectively.
articles.author: David Luhnow and Tom Fairless

articles.title: U.S. gross migration rates have fallen modestly over the past 50 years, but net migration has hardly budged. "On average, only 10–12 out of 100 moves reflect a net flow from one location to another."
articles.author: Andrea Foschi, Christopher House, Christian Proebsting, and Linda Tesar
articles.pub: Federal Reserve Bank of Kansas City

articles.title: In the 20% of occupations most exposed to AI, employment of 22- to 25-year-olds declined 6% since late 2022, driving this cohort’s “tepid overall job growth,” while employment in each of the >25 age groups rose between 6% and 8%.
articles.author: Erik Brynjolfsson, Bharat Chandar and Ruyu Chen

articles.title: American steel manufacturers have gained 7pp of market share since 2015 and are expanding domestic capacity by ~25% of 2024’s domestic production, though American steel prices are the highest globally.
articles.author: Bob Tita and Andrew Mollica

articles.title: U.S. asset supply as a % of GDP doubled since 1982, but asset demand – driven by aging, rising inequality, increasing foreign desire for U.S. assets, and falling productivity growth – grew by 4x GDP. “Asset demand won the race, and interest rates fell.”
articles.author: Adrien Auclert, Hannes Malmberg, Matthew Rognlie, and Ludwig Straub
articles.pub: Federal Reserve Bank of Kansas City

articles.title: An ~18pp rise in female labor force participation between 2000 and 2025, an ~7pp rise in participation of seniors since 2012, and a 30-year low in youth unemployment have temporarily mitigated the decline in Japan’s labor force, which peaked in 1995.
articles.author: Kazuo Ueda

articles.title: Pew estimates the unauthorized immigrant population was 14mm in 2023, or 4.1% of the population. They also estimate that in January 2025, 53mm immigrants comprised a record 15.8% of the US population; there was a likely 1mm drop btw then and June.
articles.author: Jeffrey Passel and Jens Manuel Krogstad

articles.title: Addressing fertility decline in response to rapid increases in women's opportunities, Goldin argues “the more men can credibly signal that they will be dependable dads…the higher will be the birthrate in the face of greater female agency.”
articles.author: Claudia Goldin
articles.pub: Federal Reserve Bank of Kansas City

articles.title: Inker and Pease argue that 80% of the S&P’s outperformance relative to advanced economies since 2010 arose from two sources that are unlikely to repeat: dollar strengthening and near all-time high valuation ratios, which “always beggar future returns.”
articles.author: Ben Inker and John Pease

articles.title: Examining official data and company financials, Matt Klein estimates “U.S. GDP would be about 0.4% higher than currently reported…if the [capex] of Amazon, Google, Meta, Microsoft, and Oracle were fully incorporated into the official data.”
articles.author: Matt Klein

articles.title: If US developers’ plans are realized, they will bring online a record 64 GW of new power in 2025, with solar accounting for 50%. That would surpass the previous record of 58 GW of new energy brought online in 2002, of which 98% was fueled by natural gas.
articles.author: EIA Staff

articles.title: On a rolling one-year basis, foreign inflows into American assets hit a record high in June, driven by equity inflows, which offset directionally weaker foreign demand for Treasury notes and bonds.
articles.author: Robin Brooks

articles.title: Technological rivalry raises obsolescence risk, reducing the time over which adjustment costs can be recovered and thus reducing firm growth in response to innovation, reconciling an increase in the rate of innovation with a slowdown in aggregate TFP.
articles.author: Yoshiki Ando, James Bessen, and Xiupeng Wang

articles.title: Jason Thomas argues that high rates driven by the AI buildout and US fiscal policy are “crowding out interest-sensitive sectors like for-sale housing.”
articles.author: Jason Thomas

articles.title: New voters are breaking Republican. In 2018, 34% of new voter registrations were Democrats and 20% GOP. By 2024, the GOP’s share of new voters rose 9pp relative to 2018 and the Democrats share declined 8pp, giving the GOP an edge among new registrants.
articles.author: Shane Goldmacher and Jonah Smith

articles.title: Electricity supplies nearly 30% of China’s energy use today and is growing at an annual rate of 6%. In the US, electricity accounts for 22% of energy use and is growing at an annual rate of 0.6%.
articles.author: Dan Wang and Arthur Kroeber

articles.title: Alberta Central, a Canadian bank, estimates Canada's unemployment rate for 16–24 year olds is 15%. The overall unemployment rate according to Statistics Canada is 6.9%.
articles.author: Ari Altstedter

articles.title: Brad DeLong suggests that AI investment is largely defensive in nature, “with fear of AI‑driven disruption defining the moment,” and is skeptical regarding its prospects. ”What, concretely, are the ‘new-and-improved’ services that justify the AI boom?”
articles.author: Brad DeLong

articles.title: Since the release of ChatGPT in November of 2022, earnings per share of the MAG 7 have risen 125% (through 8/18), while earnings per share of the other 493 companies comprising the S&P 500 have risen just 4%.
articles.author: John Authers

articles.title: As AI-related investment has risen since 2023, residential and nonresidential investment have declined or flatlined. This may suggest that a relatively rate-insensitive AI buildout is crowding out more interest-sensitive forms of investment.
articles.author: Economist Staff

articles.title: Torsten Sløk attributes the steepening of the US yield curve to accelerating Treasury issuance, an expectation of politically motivated cuts in the short rate, and a rise in expected inflation.
articles.author: Torsten Sløk, Rajvi Shah, and Shruti Galwankar

articles.title: Due to stronger growth, the underlying real short-term interest rate in the US is ~ 1pp higher than in the euro area. US inflation is also ~1pp higher. Jesper Rangvid concludes the current FFR is appropriate.
articles.author: Jesper Rangvid

articles.title: The bulk of the decline in the gender gap among full-time college educated workers born btw the 1931 and 1984 birth cohorts is within occupation. Changes in fields of study contribute on net only ~5.5pp out of a more than 40pp drop.
articles.author: Joseph Altonji, John Eric Humphries, Yagmur Yuksel and Ling Zhong

articles.title: Following tepid reactions to the release of GTP-5, Joe Wang observes, "It is looking more like companies are spending hundreds of billions on rapidly depreciating GPUs that produce a commoditized product that most clients only modestly benefit from."
articles.author: Joseph Wang

articles.title: The rate of performance improvement of large language models may be slowing, as models face both data availability and compute constraints. GPT-5’s launch underwhelmed many in the sector.
articles.author: Melissa Heikkilä, Tim Bradshaw, Cristina Criddle and George Hammond

articles.title: A St. Louis Fed analysis finds that US manufacturing employment hit bottom just after the trough of the Great Recession. Half of the rise in the sector's employment btw 2014 and 2024 was in food manufacturing.
articles.author: Ricardo Marto and Hoang Le
articles.pub: Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis

articles.title: "There are still more criminals than you might expect." 6.2% of white American men, 14.4% of hispanic men, and 26% of black men born in 1991 will have been imprisoned by the time they turn 50.
articles.author: Inquisitive Bird

articles.title: Shelter inflation lags changes in overall CPI due to the delayed adjustment of market rents. This dynamic explains “the lagged and dampened responses of shelter inflation to the post-pandemic surge and then retreat of market-rent inflation.”
articles.author: Laurence Ball and Kyung Woong Koh

articles.title: According to a long-running survey, the number of high school seniors reporting they were "very happy" fell from 24.8% in 2007 to 17.9% in 2019 and 12.7% in 2023. Jean Twenge cautions, "The constant pressure of social media [is] a trap of unhappiness."
articles.author: Jean Twenge

articles.title: In contrast to the 19th century, when trade deficits increased US investment, Pettis argues, in the 21st century, trade deficits “cause the debt to rise, [the share of] productive sectors like manufacturing to decline, [and] income inequality to rise.”
articles.author: Michael Pettis

articles.title: Btw 1989 and 2019, US healthcare returns tracked the US tech sector’s returns, albeit with lower volatility. Since 2020, healthcare returns have stagnated as overall US healthcare spending has flatlined at ~17% of GDP since 2008.
articles.author: Michael Cembalest

articles.title: The current tariff regime will raise ~$2.8T net new revenue through 2034, conventionally scored, according to a CRFB estimate. “Those who wish to reduce or reverse the tariffs should put forward alternative sources of deficit reduction to replace them.”
articles.author: Marc Goldwein
articles.pub: Committee For A Responsible Federal Budget

articles.title: Wage growth of the bottom quartile of American workers slowed to a 3.7% annual rate in June, below that of the top quartile, whose wages grew 4.7%. Since 2015, wage growth for the bottom quartile has generally outpaced that of their higher-earning peers.
articles.author: Justin Lahart

articles.title: Low productivity growth in the US manufacturing sector btw 1976 and 2018 was due to “rising technological rivalry” that “render[ed] rivals’ technologies obsolete and innovations more transient.”
articles.author: Yoshiki Ando, James Bessen and Xiupeng Wang

articles.title: 98% of manufacturers in the US are small-to-medium enterprises that are relatively underpenetrated by advanced technologies, including robots, additive manufacturing, and embodied AI, posing headwinds to rapid increases in US manufacturing productivity.
articles.author: Danny DeBois, Aidan Madigan-Curtis, Kate Dunbar and Kenza Wilks

articles.title: Bridgewater estimates that closing the ~$3T gap btw US manufacturing output and goods consumption will be an uphill battle. Largely due to manufacturing wages 7x that of China, the “US’s biggest comparative disadvantage is its cost of production.”
articles.author: Danny DeBois, Aidan Madigan-Curtis, Kate Dunbar and Kenza Wilks

articles.title: Markets have priced in five 25bp cuts for the Fed but no cuts for the ECB through the end of 2026. This may reflect the 200bp ECB cuts that have already occurred or concerns about a US slowdown.
articles.author: Robin Brooks

articles.title: The unemployment rate for the quintile of workers most exposed to AI rose 0.30pp between 2022 and early 2025, but for the least-exposed quintile it climbed 0.94pp. “By the most obvious measures, the effect of AI on jobs is invisible.”
articles.author: Sarah Eckhardt and Nathan Goldschlag

articles.title: Paul Kedrosky estimates given datacenter’s high deprecation rates - the replacement cycle for GPUs is 3 to 4 years - to be profitable, the return “must exceed the risk-adjusted, weighted average cost of capital, which is 12–14%.”
articles.author: Paul Kedrosky

articles.title: Btw 2014 and 2024, there was a "sustained erosion of conscientiousness" in young Americans (ages 16–39); they report "feeling increasingly easily distracted and careless, less tenacious and less likely to make and deliver on commitments."
articles.author: John Burn-Murdoch

articles.title: China's goods exports to the US are down 22% y/y but have been offset by a 12% increase in goods exports to the rest of the world. Robin Brooks notes that transshipping or selling outside the US will compress exporters' margins, both acting as deflationary shocks.
articles.author: Robin Brooks

articles.title: Employment of college graduates in AI-intensive and non-intensive sectors both grew 5% on net btw the rollout of ChatGPT in Nov. 2022 and the most recent data. Unemployment is up in computer science, but down in a number of other AI-exposed majors.
articles.author: Will Raderman

articles.title: After a 20-year decline, the share of novel patents produced by “mega firms” began a sharp rebound in 2001, reaching a peak of 18% in 2016. A relative increase in cash holdings since the early 2000s likely facilitated mega firms’ funding of risky R&D.
articles.author: Joonkyu Choi, Serguey Braguinsky, Yuheng Ding, Karam Jo, et al.

articles.title: US Census data show that btw 2005 and 2023, the fraction of Americans aged 25–34 who completed their education rose from 74% to 83%, but the % “ever married” fell from 62% to 44%, and the % with “a child in the household” fell from 55% to 39%.
articles.author: Paul Hemez and Jonathan Vespa

articles.title: The Economist highlights research that suggests high-performing employees who switched to a new firm underperformed relative to colleagues who stayed put.
articles.author: Economist Staff

articles.title: Bloomberg projects that the long-term real natural interest rate will rise to 2.8% by 2030, the highest since 2000. Demographics, debt, reduced Asian buying, and spending on defense, AI, and climate change may lead the 10-year nominal rate to exceed 6%.
articles.author: Jamie Rush, Tom Orlik and Stephanie Flanders

articles.title: Greater bill issuance and a shorter debt maturity structure will amplify the fiscal impact of Fed policy, "As the level of short-term debt increases, the government’s interest expense becomes more closely tied to the Fed’s policy rate."
articles.author: Joseph Wang

articles.title: Tariffs have generated $152B through July, ~ 2x the revenue collected over the same period last year. Trump’s tariffs may persist through a change of government due to the scale of the revenue and the difficulty of offsetting it via new taxes.
articles.author: Andrew Duehren

articles.title: Btw 2016 and 2023, Alphabet, Amazon, Meta and Microsoft's free cash flow and net earnings ~ tracked each other; since 2023, net earnings have risen 73% and free cash flow has declined by 30% due to the AI buildout.
articles.author: Greg Ip

articles.title: Wage growth for the bottom quartile of American workers slowed to a 3.7% annual rate in June; under that of the top quartile, whose wages grew 4.7%. Since 2017, wage growth for the bottom quartile has generally outpaced that of their higher-earning peers.
articles.author: Delphine Strauss, Claire Jones and Steff Chávez

articles.title: China's PISA test-takers reportedly outscored the mean Western test taker in math and science by over 100 points. However, 85% of the population, and a higher share of students, reside in regions for which Beijing does not disclose PISA scores.
articles.author: Nicholas Eberstadt
