articles.title: Bank of America forecasts US electrical demand will increase at a 2.5% CAGR btw 2024 and 2035 relative to its 0.5% CAGR btw 2014 and 2024. Only ⅓ of the utilities’ ~$100B capex spend in 2024 was for expanding grid capacity.
articles.author: Vanessa Cook

articles.title: Setser argues the IMF is likely to underestimate China's 2025 BOP surplus. “The customs goods surplus increased by $150B in the first half of 2025, and a conservative forecast of the current account surplus through Q2 2025 puts it at just over $600B."
articles.author: Brad Setser

articles.title: Net foreign inflows into US assets were near a record high in May. Robin Brooks argues that U.S. exceptionalism is “alive and well,” with the fall in the Dollar likely driven by temporary liquidity issues “at a time of heightened risk aversion.”
articles.author: Robin Brooks

articles.title: The dollar’s share in official currency reserves, FX transaction volume, and foreign currency debt instruments and related measures suggest little threat to its international dominance as store of value, unit of account, and means of payment.
articles.author: Carol Bertaut, Bastian von Beschwitz, and Stephanie Curcuru

articles.title: Enormous government subsidies for EVs are reducing Chinese oil demand, which is expected to peak in 2027.
articles.author: Brian Spegele

articles.title: Heckman et al, using two longitudinal data sets with a rich set of controls, find that participation in varsity athletics raises rates of high school and college attendance and graduation, as well as earnings, especially for disadvantaged students.
articles.author: James Heckman, Colleen Loughlin and Haihan Tian

articles.title: A randomized experiment giving 1,000 parents an unconditional $1K/month over 3 years found essentially no differences in family outcomes; treated children showed no significant gains in well-being or education.
articles.author: Patrick Krause, Elizabeth Rhodes, Sarah Miller, Alexander Bartik, et al.

articles.title: Jonathan Sine argues China’s global manufacturing share is likely near its peak as growth has slowed from 1.5% to 0.5% btw 2010 and today due to deglobalization and intensified countermeasures against Chinese industrial policy.
articles.author: Jonathan Sine

articles.title: Kedrosky estimates capex related to the datacenter buildout is ~20% of the railroad buildout in the 1880s and rising, though he cautions AI datacenter depreciation is high: “we aren’t building century-long infrastructure…datacenters are short-lived.”
articles.author: Paul Kedrosky

articles.title: The unemployment rate for recent male college graduates (22–27) has risen from <5% to 7%. Recent male graduates “are now unemployed at the same rate as their non-graduate counterparts,” while the rate for their female peers is unchanged.
articles.author: John Burn-Murdoch

articles.title: “Many countries now have vast ‘contingent liabilities’ - off-balance-sheet commitments that nonetheless represent an enormous potential outlay.” The U.S. government “is on the hook for contingent liabilities worth more than 5X the country’s GDP.”
articles.author: Economist Staff

articles.title: The 2024 rise in CA’s minimum wage in fast food restaurants from $16 to $20 raised the sector’s wages ~8% and lowered its employment by 2 to 4% relative to the US sector overall, with an estimated loss of 18,000 CA fast food jobs. @jeffreypclemens
articles.author: Jeffrey Clemens, Olivia Edwards and Jonathan Meer

articles.title: Other than China and Canada, America's trading partners have largely avoided retaliation against US tariffs, allowing Trump to raise almost $50B in excess customs revenues without a significant impact on US exports.
articles.author: Peter Foster, Amy Borrett, Andy Bounds and Ilya Gridneff

articles.title: Researchers from the @DallasFed forecast that the 56pp projected increase in the US debt-to-GDP ratio over the next 30 years will boost long-term interest rates by about 170bps, implying yields of about 6% in 2055. Higher term premia would account for 120 of the 170 bps.
articles.author: Michael Plante, Alexander Richter and Sarah Zubairy

articles.title: During the pandemic era “Great Retirement Boom,” >2.4mm people retired in excess of trend, @mfariacastro finds. As of 2024, retirements had reverted to their prepandemic trend.
articles.author: Miguel Faria-e-Castro, Serdar Birinci, Kurt See and Gus Gerlach
articles.pub: Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis

articles.title: Autor and Hanson warn the US faces a second "China Shock" that tariffs are ill-equipped to counter. According to an Australian analysis, btw 2003 and 2007, the US led China in 60 of 64 cutting-edge sectors; by 2023, China led the US in 57 of the 64.
articles.author: David Autor and Gordon Hanson

articles.title: Almost all measured productivity growth in US manufacturing since 1987 was in Computer and Electronic Products, but true TFP growth may be underestimated by 1.7pp in durables overall (and by 0.4pp in nondurables) due to quality improvements.
articles.author: Enghin Atalay, Ali Hortacsu, Nicole Kimmel and Chad Syverson

articles.title: China's DJI can manufacture millions of drones a year; about 500 American drone manufacturers are collectively building fewer than 100,000 drones annually.
articles.author: Farah Stockman

articles.title: Chinese exports hit $1.8T through 2Q2025, driving a record surplus of $586B. Bloomberg notes: "The pickup in China’s export growth in June was led by a rebound in shipments to the US after a temporary thaw in the trade war. The blip may not last long."
articles.author: Bloomberg Staff

articles.title: Investors are still very long dollar assets, even though some are starting to hedge or modestly reduce their exposure. Joseph Wang argues the dollar may have “much more to fall” if fiscal deficits are not reduced and a dovish Fed chair is appointed.
articles.author: Joseph Wang

articles.title: Persistent trade deficits at current levels would push our net international investment position beyond levels sustained in any advanced economy. Stabilization will require an adjustment of at least 2% of GDP, implying real exchange depreciation of ~15–20%.
articles.author: Tamim Bayoumi and Joseph Gagnon

articles.title: The rise in long-term Treasury yields likely reflects the global rise in rates rather than a US-specific "Trump risk premium," Robin Brooks argues, citing the stability of the US trade-weighted yield differential relative to other advanced economies.
articles.author: Robin Brooks

articles.title: If US business investment is not “saving-constrained,” Michael Pettis argues, “the American economy will not adjust to net foreign inflows with higher domestic investment, and therefore it must adjust with lower domestic saving.”
articles.author: Michael Pettis

articles.title: Brad Setser argues that China’s BoP surplus, which now ~ equals Europe’s, misrepresents its true surplus. China masks exports through foreign subsidiaries and investments, as evidenced by its customs surplus growth.
articles.author: Brad Setser

articles.title: The AI buildout is being funded by special purpose vehicles (SPVs). Paul Kedrosky warns "if the returns aren't there quickly, the thin equity cushion above datacenter SPV debt holders will be speedily wiped out."
articles.author: Paul Kedrosky

articles.title: Zero-sum thinking, in terms of political and policy views is strongly associated with lower levels of intergenerational upward mobility. @S_Stantcheva notes, “Younger generations today are much more zero-sum than older ones.”
articles.author: Stefanie Stantcheva

articles.title: Variation in construction costs has had relatively low predictive power for variation in house prices, with increased decoupling over time suggesting “increasingly binding restrictions that prevent housing supply expansion in high-demand areas.”
articles.author: Brian Potter and Chad Syverson

articles.title: CCP officials have attacked "neijuan" or “involution,” meaning low-priced, disorderly price competition. Overcapacity, driven by industrial investment, is fueling, and potentially entrenching, deflation.
articles.author: Joe Leahy and Haohsiang Ko

articles.title: China's precisely targeted rare earth export controls were "probably the key factor in forcing Washington to reverse its tariff rises on the country." The American auto industry would effectively shut down if the controls were not lifted. @crmiller1
articles.author: Chris Miller

articles.title: As of July 7, the US has an effective tariff rate of 17.6%, now reflecting a 20% tariff on Vietnam and 25% tariffs on Japan and South Korea. Dynamically scored, this regime will likely raise $2.2T over 2026–2035.
articles.author: Ernie Tedeschi

articles.title: The pandemic-era flight of families with young children from non-sunbelt cities ended in 2024. Sunbelt cities have seen growth over the entire period. Rural counties are now losing their aged 0–4 populations at a faster rate (-.5%) than cities (-.2%).
articles.author: Connor O'Brien

articles.title: Between 2004–2024, an average of 2.1mm people annually became first-time home buyers. Last year there were only 1.1mm first-time buyers, relative to 1.9mm in 2008; 46mm American households are renting, a record high.
articles.author: Carol Ryan

articles.title: Over the past 50 years, federal spending has averaged 21.1% of GDP, rising to 24.4% post-pandemic. Revenues, at 18.3% of GDP, remain above the 17.3% 50-year average, but not enough to cover interest costs and “relentlessly” rising Medicare and Medicaid.
articles.author: Steven Rattner

articles.title: “Shifting priorities.” In a 2023 Pew study, only 22% of those 18–34 say having children is extremely or very important. For comparison, 68% say the same about having a job or career they enjoy, and 62% say this about having close friends.
articles.author: Melissa Kearney and Phillip Levine

articles.title: The dollar is down 11% since the start of the year. Robin Brooks argues that the decline is not a function of an increased risk premium but "maps almost entirely into interest differentials."
articles.author: Robin Brooks

articles.title: A large dataset on preferences of workers and capability assessments from AI experts suggests a mismatch between actual AI usage by task and a Human Agency Scale that rates its suitability to the task, taking into account both tastes and capabilities.
articles.author: Erik Brynjolfsson, Yijia Shao, Humishka Zope, Yucheng Jiang, et al.

articles.title: Between 2008–2023, the US household and corporate sectors ran a mean surplus of 3.5% and 1.6% of GDP, respectively. "On a net basis, the US private sector does not need foreign savings," Martin Wolf argues.
articles.author: Martin Wolf

articles.title: A 4-generation dataset linking 2.5+ mm adults to their ancestors finds that btw 1850 and 1940, a white American’s socioeconomic status was largely independent of his grandparent’s status after corrections for measurement error and father’s status.
articles.author: Zachary Ward, Kasey Buckles and Joseph Price

articles.title: The American manufacturing trade deficit in 2024 was $1.2T. Robert Lawrence estimates that closing it would increase the share of manufacturing employment from 7.9% to 9.7% an increase of 1.7pp.
articles.author: Robert Lawrence

articles.title: An ECB analysis finds Euro Area affiliates of US multinationals account for >5% of EA value added and are responsible for ~ 30% of the EA 2024 goods surplus vis-a-vis the US. Overall, the EA-US current account was ~ at par in 2024.
articles.author: Lorenz Emter, Michael Fidora, Fausto Pastoris, Martin Schmitz, et al.

articles.title: Since February 2020, real Federal spending has risen 25%. Interest payments account for ⅓ of the increase; that spending along with increases in Social Security, Medicare, Medicaid and veterans benefits account for 77% of the increase.
articles.author: Justin Fox

articles.title: Citing a number of examples from medicine and meteorology, @cremieuxrecueil argues that “hard-to-explain trend[s]” are often artifacts of shifts in measurement methodology resulting from institutional changes and, in some cases, economic incentives.
articles.author: Cremieux

articles.title: Foreign holdings of Treasuries at $9.01T held close to a record high in April, down just $36B from March. Private selling accounted for all of the decline; official entities were net buyers.
articles.author: Chris Anstey and Ye Xie

articles.title: The CBO's dynamic analysis of the reconciliation bill forecasts an increase in the deficit of $2.8T over 2025–2034; increased borrowing raises interest rates on the 10-year by a mean of 14bps and real GDP increases by .5% over that period.
articles.author: Phillip Swagel, Devrim Demirel, John McClelland, Chad Chirico, et al.

articles.title: After years of near-zero yields, 10-year Bund yields are trading around 2.5% down from March's 2.9%. The Bund market is "normalizing" with bond yields moving above interest rate swaps of the same duration.
articles.author: Ian Smith and Sam Fleming

articles.title: Robin Brooks argues the “unprecedented” immigration wave under Biden “collided with a segmented labor market that was leaving more and more people on the sidelines.” He implies the marginal native male worker is worse off due to increased competition.
articles.author: Robin Brooks

articles.title: The US prime-age employment-to-population ratio and the job-finding rate for those in the labor force are ~at the European mean. But the US participation rate has fallen below 90%, on par with Italy at the bottom of the pack, driven by men ages 25–34.
articles.author: Robin Brooks

articles.title: Illegal immigrants constitute ~ 4.4% of the overall American workforce according to a GS analysis, but make up 13% of construction workers, 16% of animal slaughtering and processing workers, and 17% of farm workers.
articles.author: Arian Campo-Flores, Rebecca Picciotto, Patrick Thomas and Tarini Parti

articles.title: Investment funds’ participation in Treasury auctions rose from 1.7% to 67.8% btw Jan. 2008 and Oct. 2023, largely replacing broker-dealers. Commensurate extension of Fed open market operations to non-bank institutions could improve market liquidity.
articles.author: Nina Boyarchenko and Or Shachar

articles.title: Between 1992 and 2025, the US economy significantly decarbonized; the ratio of carbon dioxide emissions to GDP fell from ~ .44 to ~.19, largely being driven by the replacement of coal by natural gas.
articles.author: Roger Pielke Jr.

articles.title: Tariffs will not return highly labor-intensive production to the US, and thus will have little effect on inequality of market income. But, per the Budget Lab, “they’re part of a policy mix that will greatly increase inequality after taxes and transfers.”
articles.author: Paul Krugman

articles.title: The decline in labor force participation by white men 25–54 accelerated post-2008, leading @robin_j_brooks to speculate that it is fundamentally driven by "a lack of opportunity." During that same time period, LFP for white women 25–54 rose.
articles.author: Robin Brooks

articles.title: The 6.6% unemployment rate for new college graduates aged 20 - 24 is at its highest level in a decade, well above the national rate of ~ 4%. HS graduates ages 18 - 19 with no college averaged a rate of 14.5%, up from 13.3% over the prior 12-month period.
articles.author: Justin Lahart and Te-Ping Chen

articles.title: In 1990, Sub-Saharan Africa's GDP per capita was 30% of the world average. Btw 2014 and 2022, GDP per capita declined to 22% of the world’s mean. 50% of the world’s population growth over the next 15 years will occur in Africa.
articles.author: Cesar Calderon and Ayan Qu

articles.title: According to a Dutch analysis, the volume of China’s imports hasn’t grown since 2022, while exports have risen 33% as its import substitution campaign gains speed. @Brad_Setser notes, "China’s vision of trade is exporting without importing.”
articles.author: Jason Douglas and Clarence Leong

articles.title: The 2.1M year-round, full-time workers in the United States who first came to the country on a student visa earn a median salary of $115,000, 32% higher than native-born counterparts, and are twice as likely to work in R&D or to become entrepreneurs.
articles.author: Connor O’Brien

articles.title: The Yale Budget Lab projects that the combination of OBBBA and the tariffs in place as of June 1 results in a net income reduction of ~1% for the median household, a 6.5% reduction for the bottom 10% of households, and a 1.5% increase for the top 10%.
articles.author: Ernie Tedeschi

articles.title: Regions with high cancer mortality in 1996 saw increased drug-related mortality in non-cancer patients and reliance on public transfers, and a rise in the GOP vote share in elections for the House, president, and governor from the mid-2000s to 2022.
articles.author: Carolina Arteaga and Victoria Barone

articles.title: Supply curves in four key sunbelt areas have become less elastic. “High prices in the suburban parts of these areas no longer stimulate much new building, possibly because the residents of these areas have made it difficult to permit new projects”.
articles.author: Edward Glaeser and Joseph Gyourko

articles.title: The surge in alternative lending sources suggests that the financial system “rewired itself” around the regulatory constraints imposed on large institutions. Less regulation may restore large bank lending at the expense of small banks and private credit.
articles.author: Joseph Wang

articles.title: In 2024, the US produced a record amount of energy, a 1% increase from the previous record set in 2023. Natural gas, crude oil, natural gas plant liquids, biofuels, solar, and wind all set domestic production records.
articles.author: Mickey Francis

articles.title: East Asian “reserves tracked the current account surplus almost perfectly until 2014,” but in the past 10 years outflows were driven by private investors seeking yield, “not state investors who are forced to hold safe assets.” @Brad_Setser
articles.author: Brad Setser

articles.title: Sløk notes that the stock of US Treasuries outstanding rose by $1.7T from 2023:Q4 through the end of 2024, exceeding the rise in corporates+mortgages, which finance private expenditure on cars, homes, and business investment.
articles.author: Torsten Sløk

articles.title: Tens of millions of highly educated young Chinese men face unprecedented barriers to launching their careers, as both domestic and global economic headwinds lead Chinese employers to slash hiring for entry-level roles.
articles.author: Luna Sun

articles.title: Globally, there are ~105 male births for every 100 female births. The bias in favor of boys is shrinking in developing countries even as a preference for girls is emerging in the rich world.
articles.author: Economist Staff

articles.title: Concurring with the CBO and JCT, four leading fiscal models “all show a tax cut of around $4.0T over ten years, partially offset by spending cuts of about $1.5T over the same time span.”
articles.author: James Capretta

articles.title: About ¾ of firms facing tariff-induced cost increases reported passing on costs to consumers: ~⅓ of mfg and ~45% of service firms fully passed on all cost increases, while 45% of mfrs and ⅓ of service firms passed on a portion of the cost increase.
articles.author: Jaison Abel, Richard Deitz, Sebastian Heise et al
articles.pub: Federal Reserve Bank of New York

articles.title: “The EU is styling itself as the big alternative to the US,” Brooks writes, but “the mess EU has made on Russia sanctions [via Kyrgyzstan]” belies its fitness. “You can do that, but you can’t then also pretend to be an alternative to Dollar hegemony.”
articles.author: Robin Brooks

articles.title: Aging populations and anti-immigration rhetoric in rich countries are forcing companies building electricity infrastructure to find creative solutions to meet surging demand. The US is unable to fill a third of its 400k+ new annual engineering roles.
articles.author: Akshat Rathi, Olivia Rudgard and Josh Saul

articles.title: The CBO released an updated cost estimate for the OBBBA projecting that the bill would add over $2.4T to primary deficits over 10 years and increase debt from 100% of GDP to 124% by 2034, 133% if interest rates remain high.
articles.author: Marc Goldwein
articles.pub: Committee for a Responsible Federal Budget

articles.title: China's household consumption plummeted from 53% of GDP in 1983 to 39% today (compared to ~70% for the US), driven by a sharp decline in household sizes and incomes as “decades of fertility-control policies have left China with relatively few children.”
articles.author: Yi Fuxian

articles.title: The 10-yr TIPS break-even, at 2.3%, is only 20 bps above its 20-year mean of 2.1%: expected inflation is anchored, but the cyclically adjusted E/P ratio minus the 10-yr TIPS yield, a measure of the equity premium, is at worryingly low June 2007 levels.
articles.author: Martin Wolf

articles.title: Only 37% of international graduates earning BA’s, master’s, or PhD’s btw 2012 and 2021 remain in the US. Roughly 90% of Chinese STEM PhD graduates do remain in the U.S. long-term, though that is subject to changes contemplated by the Dept of State.
articles.author: Connor O'Brien

articles.title: The US foreign-born population reached 51.4mm, or 15.4% of the population in 2024, Nicholas Eberstad notes, surpassing the prior peak of 14.8% set in 1890.
articles.author: Nicholas Eberstadt and Patrick Norrick

articles.title: Since 2022, Saudi Arabia’s budget has switched from surplus to a deficit, now 4% of GDP, which would require a $92bbl oil price -  ~50% above its current level - to eliminate without fiscal reforms. Debt/GDP is 26%, well below KSA’s 40% ceiling.
articles.author: Andrew England and Ahmed Al Omran

articles.title: In the SCF, the wealth share of the top 0.1% of the wealth distribution rose from 10.6% in 1989 to 14.7% in 2022. Adding the Forbes 400 to the sample yields an increase from 12.2% to 17.1%. The Forbes 400 share rose from 1.8% to 2.8%.
articles.author: Moritz Kuhn and José-Víctor Ríos-Rull

articles.title: Only 34% of the 35mm US borrowers with federally managed student loans, and not enrolled in school, were making repayments as of March. “If delinquent borrowers do not start making payments, there could be 12 million borrowers in default by year’s end.”
articles.author: Preston Cooper

articles.title: Denmark is raising its retirement age to 70, the highest in Europe. The change will impact all persons born after 1970.
articles.author: Laura Gozzi

articles.title: Real house prices are now 1% below the 2022 peak and 12% above the 2006 peak. With house prices up 34% over four years, affordability remains at levels last seen in the early 1980s when 30-year mortgage rates were over 14%. @calculatedrisk
articles.author: Bill McBride

articles.title: Workers 22–27 with at least a college degree contributed 7 bps, or 12%, of the 60 bp rise in the US unemployment rate since mid-2023, 2x their weight in the labor force. Math and computer jobs for this group fell 8% since 2022.
articles.author: Matthew Martin

articles.title: Asia’s 11 largest economies ran a surplus of more than $900B in 2024, and hold $7.5T of dollar assets. Their net capital flows into the US fell to $68B in 2024, and there is concern that outflows could “hit like an avalanche” to avoid FX losses.
articles.author: Ruth Carson, Masaki Kondo, Rebecca Choong Wilkins, and Diana Li

articles.title: Interest as a share of Federal revenues will exceed its 1980's peak shortly. If the yield curve remains at current levels rather than reverting towards the CBO benchmark, interest-to-revenues will exceed 25% by 2032 and 30% by 2035. @GeneralTheorist
articles.author: Chris Marsh

articles.title: JPM expects the Senate to pass the House bill without major changes; “adding $2.8 to $3.4T to deficits over the next ten years when including interest on the incremental debt” relative to CBO’s baseline.
articles.author: Michael Cembalest

articles.title: Matt Klein notes that the House Bill contains unexpectedly large spending cuts. Adding tariff revenue, "it is possible that the new budget could lead to almost no incremental increase in federal debt issuance compared to the 'current law' baseline!"
articles.author: Matt Klein

articles.title: Despite Japan's external assets increasing 13% y/y to $3.7T in 2024, an all-time high, Germany assumed Japan’s position as the largest creditor nation due to its current account surplus of ~€250B and the euro's appreciation.
articles.author: Erica Yokoyama

articles.title: Since 2023, foreign investors have bought a net $1.2T of coupon Treasuries, or 1/3 of the net private coupon supply. The falling dollar and rising yields abroad will likely reduce foreign demand for Treasuries and “ultimately force lower fiscal spending.”
articles.author: Joseph Wang

articles.title: Since 2000, median life expectancy in developed economies has increased 5% to 82 years, but has been outpaced by a 12% rise in the median effective working life from 34 to 38 years.
articles.author: Kevin Daly, Mambuna Njie and Johan Allen

articles.title: In 2016–2024, 1,433 of ~3,100 counties moved towards Trump in all three presidential elections. Of those counties, only 3 had a median income above $100,000, while 18 of the 57 counties that moved towards the Democrats had a median income > $100,000.
articles.author: Shane Goldmacher

articles.title: Nicholas Eberstadt argues China’s “knowledge capital” as measured by PISA test scores, may be overstated, as testing is limited to four high income provinces/municipalities, excluding 85% of the population.
articles.author: Nicholas Eberstadt

articles.title: Since Xi took office in 2012, China's R&D spending has tripled. Chinese R&D spending in 2023, adjusted for PPP, was only ~5 percent less than that of the US.
articles.author: Brian Spegele

articles.title: Krugman speculates that loss of confidence in the dollar might cause a “sudden stop” in capital flows from abroad and sharply higher interest rates, resulting in a “severe” housing crash as in the domestically triggered credit crisis of 2008.
articles.author: Paul Krugman

articles.title: The Japanese yield curve is now the steepest in the developing world, reflecting worsening concern about high debt levels along with the BOJ’s switch from quantitative easing to tightening.
articles.author: Ajay Rajadhyaksha

articles.title: Long-dated bonds are under pressure globally as 10-year term premia hit a decadal high. US 30-year bond yields exceeded 5% for the first time since 2007, as a 100bps rise in Japanese yields further reduced the appeal of Treasurys.
articles.author: Alice Gledhill and Mia Glass

articles.title: Enrollment in teaching colleges instead of increasingly valued research universities and moves out of computer science and engineering led students with parental incomes in the bottom ⅓ to now "earn less than ½ the enrollment wage premium" of the top ⅓.
articles.author: Zachary Bleemer and Sarah Quincy

articles.title: In 2024, investment was 21.7% of US GDP, while national saving (private saving minus the government deficit) was only 17.3%. The counterpart of this excess of spending over income is the current account deficit.
articles.author: Thomas Klitgaard
articles.pub: Federal Reserve Bank of New York

articles.title: The rise in Japan’s 30-year yield to more than 3%, compared to 1% in 2023, is working to keep Japanese savings at home, compounding the impact of loose fiscal policy in the US. @robin_j_brooks
articles.author: Robin Brooks

articles.title: If the reconciliation bill passes as written and 10-year yields stay at ~4.5%, the deficit will rise to ~ 7% of GDP in 2027 - up from 6.4% in 2024, @MarcGoldwein cautions.
articles.author: Marc Goldwein
articles.pub: Committee for a Responsible Federal Budget

articles.title: 107 privately held European firms worth >$1B represent just 13% ($333.38B) of the American cohort of 90 such firms worth $2.53T, despite Europe having a larger population and similar education levels to the U.S.
articles.author: Tom Fairless and David Luhnow

articles.title: Saudi Arabia will likely be running sustained current account deficits to help finance almost $2T of domestic investment. Spending at this level means the kingdom has not been a net provider of overseas capital since 2024.
articles.author: Erik Schatzker

articles.title: A growing divergence between real Treasury yields and EuroZone yields in the face of weakening American economic activity suggests investors may be pricing in a "fiscal risk premium" for American debt. @robin_j_brooks
articles.author: Robin Brooks

articles.title: Exploiting state-level variation in the timing and adoption of Medicaid expansions, Wyse and Meyer infer that the mortality hazard of new enrollees (the “treated”) fell by an average of 21%. Younger adults accounted for almost 1/2 of life-years saved.
articles.author: Angela Wyse and Bruce Meyer

articles.title: “Two-way” (i.e. after netting our minor candidates and undervotes) support for Democrats among voters under the age of 30 dropped 6pp btw 2020 (61%) and 2024 (55%) nationally. Harris lost 16pp of support from 18–29 year old Latino men.
articles.author: Yair Ghitza, Haris Aqeel and Josh Yazman

articles.title: Pending legislation will raise the “anticipated $2T annual fiscal deficit by a few $100bn in the near term” and “test the market’s appetite for dollar assets” in a period of fragile investor confidence, making fiscal stimulus risk negative for stocks.
articles.author: Joseph Wang

articles.title: Btw 1996 and 2024, geographic mobility as measured by interstate migration declined for all age groups, particularly the 25–34 cohort. A FRBR note argues the decline is driven by the rise of dual earning couples and aging.
articles.author: Urvi Neelakantan, John Bailey Jones and Yue Li

articles.title: Citing McKinsey, @JimPethokoukis notes that reallocation of workers from less to more productive firms accounted for 0.9pp of the 2.1% US productivity growth between 2011 and 2019, relative to .2% in Germany and almost zero in the UK.
articles.author: James Pethokoukis

articles.title: China’s goods trade surplus with the EU reached a record $90B for the first four months of the year, driven in part by rapidly rising EV exports which are up 17x since 2019.
articles.author: Malcolm Scott, James Mayger, and William Horobin

articles.title: The US obesity rate is almost double the average of peer nations, rising 126% since 1990. A third of US adults have multiple chronic conditions, the highest among peer nations, and the share of adults with diabetes increased by 78% over that period.
articles.author: Brianna Abbott and Josh Ulick

articles.title: Germany will aim to raise spending on defense to 5% of GDP by 2032 by increasing “hard military spending” from this year’s 2% to 3.5%, while adding 1.5% of GDP of “related spending, including infrastructure and cyber security.”
articles.author: Anne-Sylvaine Chassany and John Paul Rathbone

articles.title: The gap in employment rates between workers 25–54 who have completed college and workers who have not is 13pp, up from 12.2pp pre-pandemic.
articles.author: Rajashri Chakrabarti, Thu Pham, Beckett Pierce, and Maxim Pinkovskiy
articles.pub: Federal Reserve Bank of New York

articles.title: Danish data show that the probability of becoming either an R&D worker or a “transformative entrepreneur” who hires R&D workers and pursues innovation is highly correlated with IQ. Non-transformative entrepreneurship often reflects family background.
articles.author: Ufuk Akcigit, Harun Alp, Jeremy Pearce and Marta Prato

articles.title: Between 2015 and 2021, EU households saved 15% of their income, holding 32% of their financial assets in cash and deposits, while Americans saved 5% of their income, keeping 13% of their financial assets in cash and deposits.
articles.author: Paola Tamma

articles.title: Just 5% of young men (25–40) in the US were not in the labor force in 1976. That number rose to 11% in 2024. More than 1 in 4 men in this cohort are not working full-time, and 40% of them received some cash or cash-equivalent government benefit.
articles.author: Brad Wilcox and Grant Bailey

articles.title: The $1.134tn current account deficit of the US absorbs the bulk of the surplus saving of China, Germany, Japan, and a host of other countries. The “domestic counterpart … is not funding of the private sector but borrowing by the government.”
articles.author: Martin Wolf

articles.title: Firms that are deemed “well managed” in a US Census Bureau survey that measures monitoring and HR practices are more dynamic; they have higher propensities to acquire new plants and dispose of old ones, as well as a higher net acquisition rate.
articles.author: Raffaella Sadun, Rachel Schuh, Jonathan Hartley, John Van Reenen, et al.

articles.title: The four major “hyperscalers” continue to have a large gap btw AI spending ($440B in 2024 and $596B in 2025) and AI revenues. The four firms now account for more than half of all capex growth of the entire US equity market. Michael Cembalest notes an uptick in revenue from cloud services, which "likely includes most AI activity."
articles.author: Michael Cembalest

articles.title: A McKinsey analysis finds btw 2011–2019, 5% of American firms drove ~80% of real gross value added per worker gains. The US sample had 3x the number of leading firms relative to “stragglers” than did Germany and the UK who were almost at par.
articles.author: Jan Mischke, Chris Bradley, Olivia White, Guillaume Dagorret, et al.

articles.title: China is outpacing both the US and Europe in electrification. The US and Europe’s electrical consumption as a final share of energy seemingly plateaued at ~ 22% in 2015; since then China’s has surged to 30%.
articles.author: Nassos Stylianou, Jana Tauschinski and Edward White

articles.title: The de-escalation of the Trade War saw the $ gain 1.5% relative to its G10 peers. @robin_j_brooks cautions, "US tariffs are a negative shock on other countries, in response to which the Dollar should rise as a market offset. The opposite has happened.”
articles.author: Robin Brooks

articles.title: Torsten Sløk shows that banks are playing a smaller role as a provider of credit. Bank lending declined from ~27% of total non-financial corporate debt prior to the crisis to ~20% today.
articles.author: Torsten Sløk

articles.title: The top AI firms had been hiring software engineers at the rate of ~ 3k a month but their "net monthly hiring rate has dropped to zero.” @johnthornhillft speculates that firms believe they are close to AI that “could write software as well as any human.”
articles.author: John Thornhill

articles.title: After rising 10pp from 1993–2019, the Labor Force Participation Rate of the US 55+ population has fallen 2pp since 2019. The LFPR of the US 55–64 cohort is now below that of the EU, down from a 20pp lead in 2000.
articles.author: Nicholas Eberstadt

articles.title: The center of gravity of the American economy has shifted west; 70% of the top 50 public companies' market cap growth occurred on the west coast btw 2000 and 2024 as well as 62% of the net market cap growth of all US companies. @amcafee
articles.author: Andrew McAfee

articles.title: Americans ages 44–59 (Gen X) saw income gains at a slower pace than both Millennials and Gen Zers. They also lag in home ownership. By age 31, Millennials/Gen Z had 2x the wealth that Gen X experienced at the same age.
articles.author: The Economist

articles.title: Real median earnings for a full-time worker with a high school degree are at an all-time high and have risen 11% since 2000, prior to the "China Shock." @jmhorp notes “Every single state had positive real median wage growth from 2001 to 2024.”
articles.author: Jeremy Horpedahl

articles.title: Dollar repatriations by Chinese exporters through state banks are propping up the RMB, @martin_lynge infers. "Goods traders are being asked/required to repatriate FX earnings to help increase onshore FX liquidity and thereby ease depreciation pressure.”
articles.author: Martin Lynge Rasmussen

articles.title: CBO’s current forecast has the 10-year yield falling to 3.8% over the next 6 years. If the 10-year Treasury yield settles at ~4.4%, it would add $1.8T to the debt over the next decade. @MarcGoldwein.
articles.author: Marc Goldwein
articles.pub: Committee For A Responsible Federal Budget

articles.title: Martin Wolf notes that chronic Federal deficits have offset surpluses run by both the American domestic business and household sectors as well as foreign investment in the US.
articles.author: Martin Wolf

articles.title: CBO’s upward revisions in projected Federal debt since 2007 reflect two major unforecasted recessions and tax cuts, not unexpected cost increases due to health and aging. Yields are near those of 2007 when debt-to-GDP was ⅓ of today’s level.
articles.author: Karen Dynan and Douglas Elmendorf

articles.title: Despite its recent slide, the dollar "is still quite strong by almost all measures," @Brad_Setser notes. Aside from recent American policy, mean reversion would suggest a weaker dollar.
articles.author: Brad Setser

articles.title: Between 1996 and 2018, pay growth in higher wage industries outpaced growth in lower wage industries; 5% of fields accounted for more than half the difference.
articles.author: Joe Pinsker, Paul Overberg and Stephanie Stamm

articles.title: The increase in unemployment of new college graduates relative to overall unemployment, @DKThomp speculates, ”could be an early sign that artificial intelligence is starting to transform the economy."
articles.author: Derek Thompson

articles.title: Rogoff argues that the unsustainability of US debt has not yet “come to roost” due to the low real interest rates of 2007-2021, but that over the next 5-7 years cumulative above-target inflation of 20-25% will erase a significant portion of the debt.
articles.author: Ken Rogoff and Tyler Cowen

articles.title: Scott Winship revisits the “China Shock” analysis and finds that its extension to conclusions about national declines in manufacturing employment may be overstated. His paper provides a useful survey and summary of the related literature.
articles.author: Scott Winship

articles.title: Since 2019, aging has continued to put modest downward pressure on US real rates, but has been offset by fiscal policy, leaving real rates elevated relative to their pre-pandemic level.
articles.author: Carlos Carvalho, Andrea Ferrero, Felipe Mazin and Fernanda Nechio
articles.pub: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco

articles.title: Bridgewater's Karen Karniol-Tambour argues that changes in equity prices and bond yields do not reflect the "magnitude of underlying tectonic shifts that are underway" that imply "all US assets are under exceptional risks" from lower foreign inflows.
articles.author: Karen Karniol-Tambour and Jim Haskel

articles.title: The share of prime-age men not in the labor force rose from 2.9% to 10.9% btw 1950 to 2023. The decline is due to an increase in time spent in education and childcare as well as “push factors” such as increased disability and fewer available manual jobs.
articles.author: Leila Bengali, Mary Daly, Evgeniya Duzhak and Cindy Zhao
articles.pub: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco

articles.title: American export controls are forcing China to build less efficient AI clusters. @ohlennart argues this will lead to an enduring American edge in broad compute capacity which in turn will “compound into a broader economic advantage over time.”
articles.author: Lennart Heim

articles.title: The sub-Saharan African working age population will rise by ~ 700mm over the next 25 years. Currently, the region creates only 3mm jobs annually for the 15mm people entering the labor market.
articles.author: Economist Staff

articles.title: "China possesses scale, and the [US] does not.” @RushDoshi argues the US can balance China only by “transforming its alliance architecture” to gain scale by pooling the collective capacity of the EU, India, and Japan, which “China cannot match alone.”
articles.author: Kurt Campbell and Rush Doshi

articles.title: In Danish data, assortative mating on “ambition types” proxied by the wage paths attached to detailed educational programs rose ~20% over 1980-2018. Sorting on educational ambition rather than level explains >40% of the rise in HH inequality since 1980.
articles.author: Frederik Almar, Benjamin Friedrich, Ana Reynoso, Bastian Schulz, et al.

articles.title: Swaps and the basis trade now hold as much US government debt as the Bank of Japan; demand from hedge funds is an important driver of Treasury's current clearing level, which may “ make Treasuries vulnerable if foreign investors pull back.”
articles.author: Robin Wigglesworth, Kate Duguid, Costas Mourselas and Ian Smith

articles.title: The dollar’s value on a broad trade-weighted basis is almost 2SD above its historical mean. Two periods with similar valuations, the mid-1980s and early 2000s, saw depreciation of 25-30%, compared to today’s 5% thus far.
articles.author: Jan Hatzius

articles.title: Capital flows suggest a strategic shift out of dollar assets is underway. "A long list of different types of investors will be looking to reduce USD exposure if they can find a way to do so." @EtraAlex @jnordvig
articles.author: Jens Nordvig and Alex Etra

articles.title: AI’s share of US GDP increased from 2.4pp in 2013 to 3.5pp in 2023. Data processing nearly doubled its share, while that of manufacturing declined by 1.5pp. Labor productivity in the AI sector, driven by CAPEX, grew 4x the rate of the economy as a whole.
articles.author: IMF Staff

articles.title: According to JPM data, the number of US nominal billionaires rose 45% between 2021 and the end of 2024 to 1,990. A @gabriel_zucman analysis claims the top 19 US households control 1.81% of US wealth, up from .9% in 2000.
articles.author: Juliet Chung

articles.title: Forbes data on billionaires from 57 countries over 1986-2011 show that a 1 standard deviation in founders' aggregate wealth/GDP yields a 1.87 ppt rise in TFP growth over the next 7 yrs, vs. 1.28-ppt lower TFP growth for a 1 standard deviation rise in billionaire heirs' wealth/GDP. Via Jim Pethokoukis.
articles.author: Bordin Bordeerath

articles.title: Crossings at the MX border have now dropped to their lowest level since the 1960s. The change seemingly implies the surge in illegal immigration via the southern border during Biden’s first three years was a policy choice.
articles.author: Kejal Vyas, Elizabeth Findell and Santiago Pérez

articles.title: The median college graduate earns $80k annually relative to $47k for the median high school graduate, leaving the college wage premium close to its all-time high.
articles.author: Jaison Abel and Richard Deitz
articles.pub: Federal Reserve Bank of New York

articles.title: Students who take longer to graduate, attend expensive schools, or choose “soft” majors experience a lower return on investment in college education. In recent decades, a college degree hasn’t paid off for 25% of students.
articles.author: Jaison Abel and Richard Deitz
articles.pub: Federal Reserve Bank of New York

articles.title: Analysis of a General Mental Ability (GMA) test given to Norwegian soldiers since 1954 suggest that the upward trend in global IQ scores btw 1942-2008 and its subsequent reversal is a testing artifact, not a reflection of real changes in GMA.
articles.author: Morten Nordmo, Tore Nøttestad Norrøne and Ole Christian Lang-Ree

articles.title: The spreads between inflation-indexed and non-indexed Treasury bonds do not suggest long-term inflationary expectations have risen. To @krogoff that implies "a shift in demand away from long-dated US debt" is underway.
articles.author: Ken Rogoff

articles.title: The dollar’s weakness risks driving a flight from US equities, “US stocks, particularly Big Tech, have been a kind of piggy bank for the world. The risk now is that foreign investors will smash the piggy bank.” @johnauthers
articles.author: John Authers

articles.title: Bridgewater notes that a shift from climate leadership to energy pragmatism is driving capital to “the most economically viable energy sources.” In 2024, high-cost emerging technologies like hydrogen, CCS, and clean industry saw 40–50% investment drops.
articles.author: Karen Karniol-Tambour, Carsten Stendevad, Daniel Hochman and Jeremy Ng

articles.title: Obstfeld argues without reducing America’s spending relative to its income via fiscal consolidation, efforts “to eliminate all bilateral deficits [are] doomed to failure" and will result in a game of "whack-a-mole" and a sharp loss of efficiency gains.
articles.author: Maurice Obstfeld

articles.title: Global energy capex remains below 2010 levels in real terms. Per Carlyle’s Jeff Currie, supply investment is “inadequate” relative to demand growth, which is likely to exceed the 2.5% annual pace seen over the past 60 years.
articles.author: Jeff Currie and James Gutman

articles.title: Paul Krugman notes that since 1970 US manufacturing employment has declined from 25% of the workforce to 8% at the end of 2024. He calculates that closing the US trade deficit would likely increase US manufacturing share by only 2.5pp.
articles.author: Paul Krugman

articles.title: For 25 yrs, trade deficits funded debt-fueled consumption, not business investment, addicting voters to government spending without higher taxes (or inflation). If tariffs reduce trade deficits, it could raise interest rates, worsening this imbalance.
articles.author: Edward Conard

articles.title: In response to labor demand shocks, ~80% of initial job gains come from lower unemployment. But after 5 years, >60% is driven by net migration, per Foschi et al. Migration’s share of adjustment has held steady since the 1950s.
articles.author: Andrea Foschi, Christopher House, Christian Proebsting, and Linda Tesar

articles.title: Why are constant-quality house prices 15% above their pre-2007 peak? Ed Glaeser notes that US housing grew just 0.6% annually in the 2010s, down from 4% in the 1950s. Sun Belt metros now mirror coastal markets: supply-constrained and high-priced.
articles.author: Edward Glaeser and Joseph Gyourko

articles.title: European investors held ~$9T in US equities at the end of 2024, ~17% of US market cap, on par with the market cap of all European equities. “Trillions of dollars can leave the US if the rest of the world chooses to get back towards a neutral position.”
articles.author: Katie Martin

articles.title: ​​Torsten Sløk notes that US household and banking sector debt has fallen to its lowest level in decades as a % of GDP, while corporate leverage has moved sideways. “The bottom line is that the private sector in the US is in incredibly good shape.”
articles.author: Torsten Sløk

articles.title: As WTO members reeled from China’s ~ $1T surplus, China became the subject of 198 trade investigations in 2024, 2x that of 2023.
articles.author: Ryan McMorrow and Wenjie Ding

articles.title: Krugman challenges the notion that US trade deficits result from the dollar’s reserve currency role. “Most annual purchases of U.S. securities are of long-term bonds, not the short-term securities usually used as reserve assets.”
articles.author: Paul Krugman

articles.title: Debt as a % of GDP will hit an all-time high of 107% in 2029 up from 98% of GDP in 2024, @USCBO forecasts. The forecast assumes 10-year Treasuries will yield 4.1% this year and fall to 3.8% by 2035, close to their 1995-2024 mean of 3.7%.
articles.author: Molly Dahl, Robert Arnold, Devrim Demirel, et al.

articles.title: Obstfeld @PIIE argues that current account deficits have not been forcibly “imposed” on the US from abroad since 2002. Rejecting Pettis’ tax on capital flows and a Fed-induced contraction, he argues for fiscal adjustment to increase national savings.
articles.author: Maurice Obstfeld

articles.title: In 2023, US fixed asset investment for electrical generation, storage, and distribution was $208.5B relative to $138B invested in oil, gas, and coal extraction. Grid Strategies estimates electric power investment growth at 3%/yr through 2030.
articles.author: Greg Ip

articles.title: In 2022 53% of US counties derived at least 25% of total personal income from federal transfer payments, up from 10% in 2000; the share of federal transfers has grown fastest in rural counties that tend to support the GOP.
articles.author: Guy Chazan

articles.title: In a 776-person field experiment at P&G on real product innovation tasks, individuals using AI delivered solutions with higher average quality than teams without AI, and performed almost as well as teams using AI.
articles.author: Fabrizio Dell’Acqua, Charles Ayoubi, Hila Lifshitz, Raffaella Sadun, Ethan Mollick, et al.

articles.title: A @SFFed analysis finds sustained economic expansions raise “real household income by about 1/2 to 2/3 of a percentage point each year that an expansion continues, with larger effects evident at lower income percentiles.”
articles.author: Deepika Baskar Prabhakar and Robert Valletta
articles.pub: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco

articles.title: Sweden is planning on increasing defense spending from 2.4% to 3.5% of GDP over the next five years, financed by borrowing, citing Russia’s war in Ukraine and waning US commitments.
articles.author: Richard Milne

articles.title: Federal Reserve data through Q4 2024 reports that the top 0.1%’s wealth share increased to 13.8% from 13% at the end of 2020 contrasted with the broadly affluent 90-99% whose wealth share declined 2.4pp to 36.4% over that period.
articles.author: Alexandre Tanzi

articles.title: Between 1975 and 2015, an increasingly inelastic supply of specialized talent encouraged incumbents to “overhire to deter entrant innovation.” A pro-innovation policy would tax incumbents and subsidize entrants. Jesús Fernández-Villaverde @AEIecon
articles.author: Jesús Fernández-Villaverde, Yang Yu and Francesco Zanetti

articles.title: China is facing its own "China Shock," losing at least 7.4mm jobs in labor-intensive manufacturing industries between 2011 and 2023, due to automation and migration to lower-wage manufacturing hubs like Vietnam and Indonesia.
articles.author: William Langley and Haohsiang Ko

articles.title: Americans earning over $250k/year now represent ~50% of US consumer spending, up from ~33% in the 1990s, supported in part by stock market gains. Recent equity market declines may have a disproportionate impact on spending relative to previous selloffs.
articles.author: Jonnelle Marte

articles.title: The US foreign-born population rose by 8.3mm between 2021–2025 per Census Bureau data—the largest 4-year gain on record. The total reached 53.3mm, or 15.8% of the population, surpassing the prior peak of 14.8% set in 1890.
articles.author: Steven Camarota and Karen Zeigler

articles.title: Between 2000 and 2024, NATO's defense spending averaged 1.72% of GDP, a cumulative $2T short of the 2% target, “funds that should have been invested…in force structure, modernization, and readiness but were instead deferred or cut.” @MEaglen
articles.author: Mackenzie Eaglen and Cole Spiller

articles.title: .@michaelxpettis argues that China’s low consumption isn’t a policy failure—it’s the flip side of its manufacturing edge. When Japan tried to raise the consumption share of GDP in the 1980s, it took 17 years and cost global manufacturing market share.
articles.author: Carol Yang

articles.title: 51.4% of US women aged 18-40 were single in 2023 up from 41.8% in 2000 according to the Aspen Economic Strategy Group. Ben Goldman notes “There simply aren’t enough [men] with steady jobs and earnings for non-college women to date.”
articles.author: Rachel Wolfe

articles.title: Market reaction to CBO scoring of legislative proposals on “large negative proposal days” implies the 10-year rises by 0.75 bps for a one-time 1 ppt increase in deficits/GDP, cumulating to 6.75 bps for a persistent deficit shock. @HannoLustig
articles.author: Roberto Gomez Cram, Howard Kung, and Hanno Lustig

articles.title: .@Brad_Setser argues “the broad increase in U.S. imports actually precedes the rush to front-run tariffs. A global economy with this large a Chinese surplus can be sustained only with a large U.S. deficit.”
articles.author: Brad Setser

articles.title: China's efforts to maintain its share of global exports despite a drop in its share of American imports is creating an emerging markets backlash as they lose manufacturing employment.
articles.author: Katia Dmitrieva, Philip Heijmans, and Prima Wirayani

articles.title: American boys raised outside of an intact family with two biological parents are "more likely to go to prison or jail than they are to graduate from college." @BradWilcoxIFS
articles.author: Brad Wilcox and Maria Baer

articles.title: Between 2019 and 2023, the mean US distance from work rose from 15 to 26 miles; 12% of employees hired after March 2020 live at least 50 miles from their employers, 3x the % in 2019. High taxes and house prices predicted relocation. @I_Am_NickBloom
articles.author: Mert Akan, Jose Maria Barrero, Nicholas Bloom, et al.

articles.title: Between 1980 and 2023, home ownership for the US 25-35 cohort has fallen from 50% to 25-30%. @lymanstoneky attributes this to high prices, noting that in 1969 the median home cost ~ five years of a young adult’s income vs. nine years today.
articles.author: Wendell Cox and Lyman Stone

articles.title: Net foreign assets of China's commercial banks have been rising at around $400B annually. @Brad_Setser argues these flows are likely “a big part” of how “Chinese surpluses fund [chronic] deficits in the US."
articles.author: Brad Setser

articles.title: .@davidshor notes a 23pp gap between 18-year-old men’s and 18-year-old women’s support for Trump in 2024. He argues that overall young people are “potentially the most conservative generation that we’ve experienced in 50 to 60 years.”
articles.author: Ezra Klein and David Shor

articles.title: Chinese car exports surged almost 20% in 2024 to 4.9mm, up from 1mm in 2020; 80% of exports were gas-powered.
articles.author: Chester Dawson, Rachel Gamarski, Mpho Hlakudi, and Patpicha Tanakasempipat

articles.title: Students at Ivy-plus colleges whose SAT/ACT scores were at the 99th percentile had 1st-year college GPAs 0.43 points higher than those who had scored at the 75th percentile, while high school GPAs had little predictive power. @John_N_Friedman
articles.author: John Friedman, Bruce Sacerdote, Douglas Staiger, and Michele Tine

articles.title: In 2003, the BLS added a new category of “software developer” with responsibilities for design, planning, and requirements gathering. While computer programmer employment is at its lowest since 1980, software developer jobs are down 0.3% from 2019.
articles.author: Andrew Van Dam

articles.title: “Good jobs” in industries that pay wage premiums became increasingly dispersed across US commuting zones over 1980-2021, find Hanson and Moretti, moving towards the South and the West, particularly benefiting Blacks, Hispanics, and immigrants.
articles.author: Gordon Hanson and Enrico Moretti

articles.title: As of 2023, the amount of time Americans spend socializing with others remains 20% below 2015 levels.
articles.author: Aatish Bhatia and Irineo Cabreros

articles.title: .@AzizSunderji argues that US “population growth is increasingly concentrated in suburban counties surrounding major metropolitan areas, not in the urban cores themselves,” particularly Sunbelt suburbs.
articles.author: Aziz Sunderji

articles.title: Between 1990 and 2020, colleges raised tuitions and “captured increases in external financial aid, such as Pell Grants, instead of passing those increases through to students.” @PrestonCooper93
articles.author: Preston Cooper

articles.title: South Korean shipbuilders' stocks have surged on indications the US may outsource components of naval maintenance and shipbuilding while Trump “seeks to revive his country’s moribund shipbuilding industry.”
articles.author: Christian Davies, Song Jung-a in and Harry Dempsey

articles.title: Citing PISA results @jburnmurdoch notes the share of adults in high income countries who can not "use mathematical reasoning when reviewing and evaluating the validity of statements" has risen from 20% in 2004 to 25% in 2023, and to 35% in the US.
articles.author: John Burn-Murdoch

articles.title: The recent surge in immigration masked internal migration away from urban counties. @JedKolko notes educated young adults are "still more likely to live in urban neighborhoods than other people are, but by a smaller margin than before the pandemic."
articles.author: Jed Kolko

articles.title: The ongoing wave of immigration to OECD countries has likely put upward pressure on housing costs. @TheEconomist reports on studies finding that a 1% increase in the migrant population of a region raises housing costs from 1-3%.
articles.author: Economist Staff

articles.title: .@leah_boustan et al. study children born in 1979-83 in 15 countries, and find that absolute economic mobility is strongest for sons and daughters of immigrants in destinations that are historically “immigrant-receiving.”
articles.author: Leah Boustan, Mathias Fjællegaard Jensen, Ran Abramitzky et al.

articles.title: While higher US labor costs create a greater incentive to automate, @dylan522p argues China is the only country currently positioned to achieve full-scale factory automation. Building a similar robotic arm costs ~2.2x more in the US than in China.
articles.author: Dylan Patel, Reyk Knuhtsen, Niko Ciminelli, Jeremie Eliahou Ontiveros, Joe Ryu and Robert Ghilduta

articles.title: .@AzizSunderji argues that deporting undocumented workers will make housing more expensive and reduce housing supply, noting that labor typically accounts for 20-40% of total construction costs, and that ~25% of construction laborers are undocumented.
articles.author: Aziz Sunderji

articles.title: The 2024 NAEP results show that 40% of fourth graders are “below basic” in reading proficiency, relative to 30% who are “at or above proficient.” 39% of American 8th graders are “below basic” in math, vs. 28% who are “at or above.”
articles.author: Mark Schneider

articles.title: US VCs invested $80B in Q4 ‘24, the highest since 2021, with 40% of total funding going to six large firms including OpenAI, xAI, and Databricks. Q1 ‘25 follows a similar pattern, with $30B raised so far and OpenAI in talks to raise $40B.
articles.author: George Hammond

articles.title: As the riskiest borrowers have been absorbed by the $2T private credit market, high-yield spreads have fallen to a historically low level since 2022. @GregObenshain
articles.author: Greg Obenshain

articles.title: In 2024, American firms returned more than $1.7T to investors in buybacks and dividends. @AswathDamodaran
articles.author: Aswath Damodaran

articles.title: The authors reestimate Williams’ models using vintage data. From 1995-2011, estimated trend growth explains most of the drift in this “real-time” R-star. From 2012-2019, the rise in trend growth was offset by a falling trend in other factors.
articles.author: Sophia Cho and John Williams
articles.pub: Federal Reserve Bank of New York

articles.title: Adjudicating the debate between @DanielDiMartino and @David_J_Bier, @stanveuger argues the likely fiscal impact of a 22-year-old high school dropout immigrant is "a small negative number."
articles.author: Stan Veuger

articles.title: Controlling for student quality, Altonji et al. estimate school rank has a moderate, statistically significant effect on returns to Texas MBA and JD degrees, but little impact on earnings from graduate nursing, social work, and pharmacy degrees.
articles.author: Joseph Altonji and Zhengren Zhu

articles.title: Over the past 10 years, $9T has been spent globally on electrification, however Michael Cembalest points out that renewables' share of final energy consumption is still advancing at only 0.3-0.6% per year.
articles.author: Michael Cembalest

articles.title: As baby boomers die, inheritances as a share of US output are over 10% which is just off a post-WW II high. For every $100 paid in wages, the dead leave behind $20. Given fewer children per family, even this underestimates the “shift to an inheritocracy.”
articles.author: Economist Staff

articles.title: Even as the jobless rate has fallen to near-record lows, the share of the US population receiving SNAP benefits is stuck at an elevated 12.3%, “more than double what economic conditions alone would have projected over the past 20 years.”
articles.author: Matt Weidinger and Angela Rachidi

articles.title: .@Brad_Setser argues that after a 2022 change in China’s balance of payments methodology, China’s official numbers substantially underreport its surplus. For 2024, he estimates a $800B surplus vs. China’s official figure of $400B.
articles.author: Brad Setser

articles.title: German manufacturing fell ~ 10% over the past 2 years, and “its companies, squeezed between rising costs and falling exports, are shedding thousands of jobs a month.” Patents are ½ of Japan’s and Korea's, and CO2 emissions/capita are just below China’s.
articles.author: Bertrand Benoit

articles.title: In 2024, the US spent 3.1% of GDP on interest and 2.9% on defense. @nfergus argues, “History suggests that any sustained period when a great power spends more on interest …than on military is likely to see its strategic rivals challenge its position.”
articles.author: Niall Ferguson

articles.title: Card et al, find that the 25-30 percentage point college enrollment gap between disadvantaged boys and girls with IQs of ~116 closed among students who qualified for a “gifted” program. @EricChyn
articles.author: David Card, Eric Chyn, and Laura Giuliano

articles.title: Mark Warshawsky and Giorgi Bokhua @AEIecon project the deficit will be 6.9% of GDP in 2033, up from 6.1% in their 2022 baseline model. Debt is forecasted to reach 256% of GDP in 2052, “likely resulting in a crisis even before 2053.”
articles.author: Mark Warshawsky and Giorgi Bokhua

articles.title: Analysis by @TheEconomist finds that by 2027, there will be 22.5mm more men, ages 23-37, than women, ages 22-36, due to decades of policies leading to sex-selective abortions. One in six young Chinese men won’t find a marriage partner.
articles.author: Economist Staff

articles.title: .@swinshi argues that 72% of 40-year-olds exceed their parents' family income excluding government benefits, using his preferred inflation measure and adjusting for family size.  That matches responses to the GSS survey data.
articles.author: Scott Winship

articles.title: Prices for transformers have surged 70–100% since 2020, and US domestic supply meets just 20% of demand. New tariffs could further inflate transformer prices, worsening grid bottlenecks as AI data centers push demand higher.
articles.author: Jinjoo Lee

articles.title: .@cartainc finds venture valuations have undergone a reset in the last two years with the rate of “down rounds” running ~ 20% in the last two years up from ~ 10% between 2019 and mid-2022.
articles.author: Ashley Neville and Kevin Dowd

articles.title: The IMF estimates that Europe’s internal barriers act like tariffs of 45% on manufacturing and 110% on services, “a sector that makes up around 70% of EU GDP;” such barriers limit trade across EU countries to less than half that across US states.
articles.author: Mario Draghi

articles.title: .@Katie_Roof describes “the era of the zombie unicorn,” noting that of 354 VC-backed firms newly valued over $1B in 2021, only 10 went public via IPOs or SPACs. Many are now facing “down rounds” at lower valuations or worse—becoming “unicorpses.”
articles.author: Katie Roof

articles.title: .@jean_twenge and David Blanchflower cite 11 studies confirming a decline in life satisfaction particularly among the unmarried young. “The U-shape in wellbeing by age that used to exist…is now gone, replaced by a crisis in wellbeing among the young.”
articles.author: Jean Twenge and David Blanchflower

articles.title: .@Brad_Setser notes that China’s exports of manufactured goods have grown over $150B/year over the past six years, while its imports have grown only $15B/year, and argues “Mr. Xi has a one-way vision of trade.”
articles.author: Brad Setser

articles.title: .@IEA expects US electricity demand growth of 2% annually from 2025-2027, up from a prior 1% forecast, largely driven by data centers. Currently, data centers consume 4% of US electricity and could reach between 6.7-12% of total demand by 2028.
articles.author: Eren Çam, Marc Casanovas and John Moloney

articles.title: Mogstad et al find that over 70% of the difference in earnings variability between the Nordic countries and the US/UK is due to a lower skill premium rather than skill variability, likely the result of wage compression due to collective bargaining.
articles.author: Magne Mogstad, Kjell Salvanes, and Gaute Torsvik

articles.title: In 2024, China added a record 356GW of wind and solar capacity, but also started building 94.5GW of coal power capacity - the most since 2015. Last year, 93% of new global coal power construction was in China.
articles.author: Joe Leahy, Wenjie Ding, and Attracta Mooney

articles.title: The US is preparing reciprocal tariffs that treat value-added taxes as barriers to US exports. @Brad_Setser notes that this "basically forecloses negotiation, as the EU and others aren’t in a fiscal position to negotiate away its tax base."
articles.author: Shawn Donnan

articles.title: .@kearney_melissa finds higher fertility in states with greater FHA/VHA loan issuance among women aged <= 20 when FHA loans began in 1935. She argues FHA/VHA led to 3mm births over 1935-1957, 10% of the baby boom's rebound from low interwar birth rates.
articles.author: Lisa Dettling and Melissa Schettini Kearney

articles.title: Valerie Ramey examines case studies from the US, Singapore, and Australia and finds that temporary transfers like tax rebates “likely provided little or no macroeconomic stimulus in advanced economies,” contradicting influential micro studies.
articles.author: Valerie Ramey

articles.title: Jesús Fernández-Villaverde @AEIecon argues that AI is unlikely to deliver viable solutions for old-age care, and proposes that "the US should restructure its entire economic policy toward a family-oriented approach that facilitates marriage."
articles.author: Jesús Fernández-Villaverde

articles.title: Labor force participation for prime-age (25-54) females rose from 74.7% in Jan. 2021 to 78.2% in Sept. 2024. Hispanic women represented only 17% of the prime-age female population but accounted for ⅓ of the increase. @sffed
articles.author: Sabrina Considine, Brandon Miskanic, Nicolas Petrosky-Nadeau, and Deepika Baskar Prabhakar
articles.pub: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco

articles.title: .@DLeonhardt reports a sharp partisan gap in learning loss: 8 of the 10 states with the biggest math declines since 2019 - likely due to prolonged school closures - voted Democratic, while 8 of the 10 smallest shortfalls voted Republican.
articles.author: David Leonhardt

articles.title: Jason Furman wrote a "Dear John" letter to the Biden Administration’s economic policy this Valentine's week noting the policy "failed to seriously reckon with budget constraints and 'crowding out.'"
articles.author: Jason Furman

articles.title: Jason Furman (Obama's former CEA chair) claims that real infrastructure spending astonishingly fell 17% during the Biden administration despite large budget increases which failed to anticipate capacity constraints that drove prices up more than spending.
articles.author: Jason Furman

articles.title: At the start of 2025, the median cost of capital for an American firm is 8.5%. @AswathDamodaran argues that investments should be driven by “growth and profit prospects,” and that “hurdle rates are not where business/valuation battles are won or lost.”
articles.author: Aswath Damodaran

articles.title: “US stocks on average trade at 22 times earnings, a near 60% premium over Europe.” As EU firms choose to list in the US, “headquarters, management and hiring can be pulled away too” and Europe “loses out on the next generation of ideas and companies.”
articles.author: Sagarika Jaisinghani, Pablo Mayo Cerqueiro, Jan-Patrick Barnert, and Neil Campling

articles.title: The capex of Meta, Google, Microsoft, and Amazon is forecast to grow to at least $320B this year, up 30% y/y, “to remain at the forefront of AI large language model research.”
articles.author: Stephen Morris and Rafe Uddin

articles.title: The House preliminary reconciliation bill would allow up to $5.5T of net primary deficit. @MarcGoldwein estimates that if passed, the bill would increase interest costs by ~ $1.3T over the decade, rising to $2T or 4.7% of GDP by 2035.
articles.author: CRFB Staff
articles.pub: Committee for a Responsible Federal Budget

articles.title: Karlyn Bowman cites a new Economist/YouGov survey finding that 45% of Americans favor ending DEI programs in schools and government and 40% oppose ending the programs. A Reuters poll showed 44% in favor, 51% opposed.
articles.author: Karlyn Bowman

articles.title: .@ernietedeschi estimates that the 10% China tariffs would raise prices by 0.1%, costing the average household $223/year. The policy is expected to raise $360 billion net of a projected 0.1% decline in GDP.
articles.author: Ernie Tedeschi

articles.title: Russia currently provides 8% of natural gas consumed in Europe down from 35% in 2021. Natural gas prices in Europe are ~ 4x the price in the US, which is driving deindustrialization as industrial production shifts to countries with lower-cost energy.
articles.author: Stanley Reed and Liz Alderman

articles.title: Who is paying for Biden's blowout spending that exceeds 23% of GDP w/ 6% deficits that suck down savings, drive mortgage rates to 7%, and sales of existing homes down to their trough at the depths of the financial crisis—20% below their pre-COVID level.
articles.author: Edward Conard

articles.title: DoD found over 75% of Americans between 17 and 24 are ineligible for military service due to being "overweight, unable to pass the aptitude test, afflicted by physical or mental health issues, or disqualified by such factors as a criminal record."
articles.author: Dexter Filkins

articles.title: US regions hit by China trade shocks rebounded in jobs but not in employment rates: recovery came from young workers and immigrants entering low-wage roles in health care, education, retail, and hospitality while manufacturing jobs kept shrinking.
articles.author: David Autor, David Dorn, Gordon Hanson, Maggie Jones and Bradley Setzler

articles.title: Since the end of 2019, the US economy has grown by 12% vs. 5% for the EU. @greg_ip notes that seven US stocks are worth more than the combined market capitalization of major European stock markets.
articles.author: Greg Ip

articles.title: Globally the rise in women's expectations and a decline in lower-income men’s fortunes is "subverting the traditional role of straight marriage, in which men are seen as necessary for the economic insurance of their family." @DKThomp
articles.author: Derek Thompson

articles.title: The rise in female college enrollment from 1930-80 hasn’t reduced marriage rates for college women. “The overall decline in marriage rates is driven almost entirely by a sharp drop in marriages between non-college women and non-college men.”
articles.author: Clara Chambers, Benjamin Goldman, and Joseph Winkelmann

articles.title: Canada and Mexico are highly dependent on the US market: in 2023, 78% of Canadian goods exports and 80% of Mexican goods exports went to the US. 14% of US goods imports came from Canada, 15% from Mexico, and 14% from China. @shannonkoneil
articles.author: Shannon O'Neil and Julia Huesa

articles.title: Aswath Damodaran defines an “intrinsic risk-free rate,” the sum of real GDP growth and inflation, which he finds is more predictive of 10-year yields than the FFR. He argues, “Interest rates ultimately are driven by macro fundamentals.”
articles.author: Aswath Damodaran

articles.title: Once at par with Europe's American equity markets pulled away since ~ 2015.  One dollar invested in the MSCI US index in 1970 would be worth $268 in 2025, vs. $141 for a dollar invested in the 1970 MSCI Europe index.
articles.author: Jesper Rangvid

articles.title: German industrial output has fallen by 15% since 2018 and manufacturing employment is down 3%, as German exports slow amid increasing global competition from Chinese manufacturers.
articles.author: Tom Fairless and Bertrand Benoit

articles.title: Between 2014 and 2024, the Mag 7 added $15.8T in market cap, approximately 40% of the increase in market capitalization across all US equities over the decade. @AswathDamodaran
articles.author: Aswath Damodaran

articles.title: .@USCBO projects a deficit of 6.2% of GDP in 2025, down from 6.6% in 2024. Outlays at 23.3% of GDP would exceed the long-term mean by 2.2pp; revenue of 17.1% is just 0.2pp under its long-term mean. Projections beyond 2025 assume the expiration of TCJA.
articles.author: CBO Staff

articles.title: From 1984 to 2003, Congress offset ~ 1/3 of the changes in the projected 5-year deficit within a year. Post-2004 this policy feedback is absent. Auerbach @dannyyagan believe annual deficits over the next decade should be lowered by 0.5-1.2% of GDP.
articles.author: Alan Auerbach and Danny Yagan

articles.title: Michael Cembalest notes streamlining permits for energy infrastructure will likely have only a limited impact on speeding construction without Federal eminent domain powers. Still a 7% increase in oil and gas output by 2029 is “a very achievable goal.”
articles.author: Michael Cembalest

articles.title: .@dannyyagan notes that in CBO’s baseline, nominal GDP growth exceeds the nominal interest rate through 2040, but the historically high debt/GDP ratio implies greater vulnerability to the risk that interest rates will exceed GDP growth before 2040.
articles.author: Danny Yagan

articles.title: Kevin Hassett @Jon_Hartley_ @joshrauh estimate that following the 2017 Tax Cuts and Jobs Act, a 1pp drop in the cost of capital raised the investment rate in 2022 by 2.33 pp relative to 2016, 3x the CBO’s 0.7 pp estimate.
articles.author: Jonathan Hartley, Kevin Hassett and Joshua Rauh

articles.title: .@AswathDamodaran calculates an implied expected return of 8.91% for the S&P 500 in 2025 and an implied equity risk premium of 4.33% which is “close to historic norms,” though he notes “there is an 80% chance that stocks are overvalued.“
articles.author: Aswath Damodaran

articles.title: US inflation is still running ~ 1pp above its pre-pandemic level despite a 4% reduction in prices of imports from China since April 2022. @M_C_Klein argues higher Chinese growth could reaccelerate inflation.
articles.author: Matt Klein

articles.title: A @PIIE report argues it is “unlikely that the magnitude of Korean and Taiwanese semiconductor capex on American territory will be sufficient to meet the [CHIPS Act] target of 20% of global capacity for advanced chips made on US soil by 2030”.
articles.author: Gary Clyde Hufbauer, Megan Hogan and Mingsong Chen

articles.title: .@swinshi argues, “Whenever you read that 80% of Americans are dissatisfied with the way things are going in the US, you should remember that 80% are satisfied with the way things are going in their own life.”
articles.author: Scott Winship

articles.title: .@JHaltiwanger_UM finds rising US earnings inequality over 1996-2018 reflected both a rising gap in industry-specific wage premia and that “high-wage workers are increasingly sorted to high-wage industries with rising industry premia” and vice versa.
articles.author: John Haltiwanger, Henry Hyatt, and James Spletzer

articles.title: .@Brad_Setser @SanderTordoir note that the EU has 30mm manufacturing workers, 2x what the US had before the 1st China Shock, and argue that for the EU a 2nd China Shock “will be associated with the loss of export markets, not just a surge in imports.”
articles.author: Sander Tordoir and Brad Setser

articles.title: A McKinsey analysis argues that given the demographic headwinds facing advanced economies, US productivity growth will have to rise at 2.3x the trend rate of 2012-2023 to maintain the growth in GDP per capita over the last 25 years.
articles.author: Anu Madgavkar, Marc Canal Noguer, Chris Bradley, Olivia White, Sven Smit, and TJ Radigan

articles.title: Jiang, et al. find that despite a shortened maturity structure, fiscal expansion over the last 2 decades has reduced the convenience yields of US Treasuries, which are now negative at the long end, undermining the “reserve currency status” of the dollar.
articles.author: Zhengyang Jiang, Robert Richmond and Tony Zhang

articles.title: .@BjornLomborg argues "real energy costs of solar and wind are far higher than what green campaigners claim," citing heavy reliance on coal for new electrical generation capacity in price-sensitive developing countries like China, India, and Bangladesh.
articles.author: Bjorn Lomborg

articles.title: Dynastic persistence of ultra-high wealth in the US was limited even in periods of high inequality like 1850-1940. “Over 90% of the grandchildren of top 1% wealth grandfathers did not achieve that level”. @EconZach
articles.author: Priti Kalsi and Zachary Ward

articles.title: US data since 1919 shows that volatility in building permits foreshadows financial market volatility. “Cities and states with more elastic housing supply consistently predict financial market downturns at 12-month horizons.” @cslapoint @CortesGustavoS
articles.author: Gustavo Cortes and Cameron LaPoint

articles.title: .@jburnmurdoch argues that recent declines in fertility are due more to a decline in marriage and cohabitation than couples’ decisions to have smaller families. "The central demographic story of modern times is the rising rates of singledom."
articles.author: John Burn-Murdoch

articles.title: Auclert, et al claim to refute the "great demographic reversal" hypothesis, finding that aging in the developed world will “increase wealth-to-GDP ratios [W/Y], lower asset returns [r], and widen global imbalances [NFA/GDP] through the twenty-first century.”
articles.author: Adrien Auclert, Hannes Malmberg, Frédéric Martenet and Matthew Rognlie

articles.title: .@M_C_Klein argues that the recent rise in Treasury yields reflects “correcting bets on growth that were overly pessimistic.” “After being deeply inverted for years, the yield curve is finally starting to look normal again.”
articles.author: Matt Klein

articles.title: The ongoing Los Angeles wildfires will cost somewhere between $52-57B, according to a preliminary estimate by AccuWeather.
articles.author: Brian Sullivan and Yasufumi Saito

articles.title: Over the next 25 years, China's working-age population will fall by over 20%, while the US’s will continue to grow. “Exponential decay of population numbers over a long period of time can transform the international stage and shift the global balance.”
articles.author: Nicholas Eberstadt
