articles.title: .@DKThomp reports on an “anti-social century:“ between 2003 and 2021 18-30-year-old Americans' daily time spent alone increased 60%. He argues this raises “concerns about the impact on democracy, mental health, and societal cohesion.”
articles.author: Derek Thompson

articles.title: Real house prices are 1.3% below the 2022 peak and 11% above the 2006 peak. Affordability remains at levels last seen in the early 1980s when 30-year mortgage rates were over 14%. @calculatedrisk
articles.author: Bill McBride

articles.title: Noting the rise in 10-year yields @paulkrugman argues bond investors might be pricing in a more inflationary policy mix from the incoming administration "rising interest rates even as the Fed cuts may be an early sign of things to come."
articles.author: Paul Krugman

articles.title: .@jburnmurdoch notes in 2024 the incumbent party lost vote share in every advanced economy, by an average of 7pp. The populist right was the largest beneficiary, while leftwing parties gained in countries with center-right incumbents like the UK.
articles.author: John Burn-Murdoch

articles.title: Chinese investors are targeting the personal assets of failed entrepreneurs to recover losses, deepening the startup funding crisis, with entrepreneurship becoming a “game of unlimited liability.”
articles.author: Ryan McMorrow, Wenjie Ding, and Nian Liu

articles.title: In Q3 of 2024, expenditures for software, technology hardware, industrial equipment, and data center structures were almost 6% of GDP. @IrvingSwisher argues that the AI boom may be larger than the tech-industrial-telecom boom of the late 1990s.
articles.author: Skanda Amarnath

articles.title: A 1% per year rise in a city’s manufacturing TFP in revenue terms between 1980-1990 boosted annual earnings growth by 1.5% per year over 1980-2000. Renters' purchasing power grew by 0.6%/year, and homeowners' by 1.6%/year.
articles.author: Richard Hornbeck and Enrico Moretti

articles.title: Citing Howard Silverblatt, @RobinWigg notes that only 26 stocks now account for half the entire value of the S&P 500 index, the lowest number since at least 1980.
articles.author: Robin Wigglesworth

articles.title: .@FedGuy12 argues that a trade war cutting US trade deficits may push yields higher as overseas investors have fewer dollars to invest in fixed-income assets.
articles.author: Joseph Wang

articles.title: A @NewYorkFed analysis finds over 2010 to 2022 the US manufacturing sector's R&D expenditure as a % of gross output increased by 2.2%/year, but labor productivity fell 0.5%/year.
articles.author: Danial Lashkari and Jeremy Pearce
articles.pub: Federal Reserve Bank of New York

articles.title: Citing 25% growth in the STEM share of jobs and 50% drop in retail share since 2010, @ProfDavidDeming and @LHSummers suggest “AI could be a general-purpose technology on the scale of prior disruptive innovations” like steam power and electricity.
articles.author: David Deming, Christopher Ong, and Lawrence Summers

articles.title: A single parent with one child in Washington DC, earning $11K, receives $68K of government benefits net of taxes for a total after-tax income of $79K. A counterpart, earning $65K, receives $10K of government benefits net of taxes for an after-tax income of $75K. This structure creates little, if any, financial incentive for a low-income parent to work.
articles.author: Elias Ilin and Alvaro Sanchez

articles.title: In 2024, over half of the $71B raised by VC firms went to just 9 firms. By the end of 2024, the number of active VCs had dropped 25% since peaking at 8,315 in 2021.
articles.author: George Hammond

articles.title: Michael Cembalest argues that “hyperscalers” like Google, Microsoft, and Meta would need to generate an additional $400-500B in revenue to earn their traditional 50% gross margins on $250B in projected 2025 data center spending.
articles.author: Michael Cembalest

articles.title: Nicholas Eberstadt @AEIecon argues that given its relatively high fertility rate relative to both East Asia and Europe, the US is primed to be the world's youthful powerhouse by 2050. But to maintain this edge, the US must continue welcoming newcomers.
articles.author: Nicholas Eberstadt

articles.title: .@paulkrugman argues the extension of the 2017 tax cuts and tariffs suggests that interest rates will be “much higher for longer than most people were expecting a few months ago, which will translate into a stronger dollar and a bigger trade deficit.”
articles.author: Paul Krugman

articles.title: .@michaelxpettis argues that tariffs would raise GDP: “By taxing consumption to subsidize production, modern-day tariffs would redirect a portion of US demand toward increasing the total amount of goods and services produced at home.”
articles.author: Michael Pettis

articles.title: The mean tenure of Boeing engineers has fallen from 16.4 to 12.6 years over the last decade as job cuts and competition from space firms increase; the brain drain is threatening the institutional knowledge needed to design and build a new plane.
articles.author: Claire Bushey

articles.title: .@PikaGold notes countries with birth rates now below 1.3 saw “rapid growth in GDP per capita after a long period of stagnation or decline” as women’s new labor market opportunities demanded more than the traditional male role in child-rearing.
articles.author: Claudia Goldin

articles.title: For non-college Americans, high local prices mean lower living standards. “A high school drop-out household moving from the least expensive commuting zone to the most expensive would experience an 18.5% decline in market consumption.” @rebeccardiamond
articles.author: Rebecca Diamond and Enrico Moretti

articles.title: In 2021 migrants made up 18.8% of the German population, compared to 15.3% for the US. Sixty percent of people in Germany who "depend on government benefits for income" are either foreign-born or second-generation migrants.
articles.author: Bertrand Benoit

articles.title: An @IEA analysis finds that global coal demand hit a record high in 2024, and projects that demand will likely rise through at least 2027. @JavierBlas notes that China consumes 30% more coal than the rest of the world combined.
articles.author: Javier Blas

articles.title: Chinese export volumes through November are up 13% y/y while imports are only up 2%. A 10 pp gap between growth in exports vs. imports “implies a net exports contribution to Chinese growth of close to 2% of GDP.” @Brad_Setser @Mike_Weilandt
articles.author: Brad Setser and Michael Weilandt

articles.title: My WSJ OpEd argues that mining the technological frontier motivates America’s talent to take more risk by increasing expected after-tax payoffs, producing a 70X difference in the value of newly created companies—laughably missed by tax elasticity studies.
articles.author: Edward Conard

articles.title: .@Brian_Riedl notes that “the US has the most progressive income and payroll taxes of any OECD nation.” Across all federal taxes, the top-earning quintile paid $3.2T, while the bottom 20% paid negative $20B. #incometax
articles.author: Brian Riedl

articles.title: .@paulkrugman argues that the productivity gap between the EU and the US is largely driven by technology and finance hubs, which, regardless of their origin, persist based on positive spillovers created by the concentration of talent.
articles.author: Paul Krugman

articles.title: A NYT analysis finds the net inflow of 8mm immigrants since 2021, 60% of whom were illegal, was the largest in US history. The foreign-born percentage of the population is now at a new high of 15.2%, above the previous record of 14.8% in the 1890’s.
articles.author: David Leonhardt

articles.title: Angel investments in Norway from 2004-18 had highly skewed results: ⅓ were a total loss, the top 1% returned 50X invested capital, and the average investment returned 2X. Top angels tended to be connected to board members and founders before investing.
articles.author: Johan Karlsen, Katja Kisseleva, Aksel Mjøs and David Robinson

articles.title: The 2023 OECD Survey of Adult Skills reveals the US has ~ 3 low-scorers for every high-scorer. Germany has nearly 3X as many high-scorers per low-scorer as the US; Scandinavia has more than 5X and Japan more than 7X.
articles.author: OECD Staff
articles.pub: Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development

articles.title: Ekerdt and Wu argue that the diminishing returns to research since the 1960s is a result of the 3X increase in the share of the labor force employed in research, which resulted in a transitional decline in the “average ability among researchers.”
articles.author: Lorenz Ekerdt and Kai-Jie Wu

articles.title: Taking a demand side view, Jesper Rangvid argues that if US debt/GDP had increased by Europe’s 21pp instead of 60pp since 2007, with a conservative multiplier of 0.3 US GDP growth would have exceeded Europe’s by only 5pp vs. 23pp.
articles.author: Rangvid's Blog

articles.title: From 1930-90, the market capitalization of the top 50 US companies was dominated by firms founded in the 19th century. Since 1990, the market cap from companies founded after WWII has risen from 8% to 77% in 2024. @amcafee
articles.author: Andrew McAfee

articles.title: Draghi noted that excluding the tech sector, "EU productivity growth over the past 20 years would be broadly at par with the US.” The US dominates global R&D, and per data from Preqin accounts for 83% of VC funding in G7 economies over the past decade.
articles.author: Valentina Romei, William Crofton, and Colby Smith

articles.title: There are only 13 EU-based firms less than 50 years old with a market cap of > $10B, with a combined market cap of $400B. The comparable US cohort is worth $30T, 70x that of its EU equivalent. @amcafee
articles.author: Andrew McAfee

articles.title: Since 2021 Danish GDP has grown 3.6%, but excluding Novo Nordisk's GLP-1 drugs Ozempic and Wegovy growth would have been flat. Danish corporate income tax receipts are up 50% from pre-Covid levels. @JosephPolitano
articles.author: Joseph Politano

articles.title: An experiment in which a non-profit gave low-income Americans a tax-free $1k/month for 3 years showed little effect on political engagement, conservatism, or trust in democratic governance, but some increased belief in the value of work. @AlexBartik
articles.author: David Broockman, Elizabeth Rhodes, Alexander Bartik, et al.

articles.title: The breakeven price per barrel in the Permian Basin has fallen from $90 in 2012 to $40. AI tools could generate a further drop, helping the industry reach its “holy grail” a recovery rate of over 10%.
articles.author: Avi Salzman

articles.title: .@GautiEggertsson and @SEgiev note the difficulty of achieving negative nominal rates and argue that big downturns have resulted from a negative natural real rate - driven either by a crisis or secular stagnation - combined with stubbornly low inflation.
articles.author: Gauti Eggertsson and Sergey Egiev

articles.title: Deportations from the interior of the US peaked in 2009 at 243,000. Restrictions on immigration and deportations at scale could have a major labor market impact in segments like construction and hospitality with large numbers of foreign-born workers.
articles.author: Paul Kiernan

articles.title: .@paulkrugman offers a novel mechanism by which Trump’s tariffs may reduce US trade deficits. “A tariff-ridden world would also be a world with greatly reduced capital flows,” and "net capital flows are the counterpart of imbalances in physical trade”.
articles.author: Paul Krugman

articles.title: .@MTabarrok argues that “zero improvement in the population average score” in math and reading since the 1970s implies that growth accounting has overestimated growth in human capital, understating 2023 TFP relative to its post-1973 trend by up to ⅓.
articles.author: Maxwell Tabarrok

articles.title: China's cumulative carbon emissions since 1850 have outpaced Europe's but are still 60% of the US's level. China has been the world’s #1 carbon emitter since 2006.
articles.author: Brad Plumer and Mira Rojanasakul

articles.title: .@ikuziemko et al. find that less-educated US voters have consistently favored “predistribution” policies such as protectionism and strong labor unions, while more-educated Democratic elites have reduced support for “predistribution” since the 1970s.
articles.author: Ilyana Kuziemko, Nicolas Longuet-Marx and Suresh Naidu

articles.title: A New Zealand-based startup claims to have created a 300,000°C plasma cloud for 20 seconds using an "inside-out" tokamak, which contains superheated plasma using a high-temperature superconducting magnet inside the reactor.
articles.author: Malcolm Moore

articles.title: US utilities annual spending increased 12% in real terms between 2003 and 2023 according to FERC data. Between 2022 and 2023, capex rose 11% y/y on electrical transmission and 14% on distribution.
articles.author: Lori Aniti

articles.title: Limiting global warming to the 2° target might be more affordable than projected due to slower economic growth and the ongoing decline in the cost of solar power, wind power, and lithium batteries as production volumes increase.
articles.author: Economist Staff

articles.title: Challenging @DanielDiMartino’s estimates of the federal fiscal impact of low-skilled immigrants, @David_J_Bier finds that all education groups have a positive impact. Instead of costing $315,000, a 22-year-old high school dropout contributes $45,000.
articles.author: David Bier

articles.title: .@SteveMiran cites the 2018-19 experience with tariffs on China and $/Yuan exchange rates and argues that increased tariffs will be largely offset by $/Yuan moves, resulting in little net change in after-tariff dollar import prices.
articles.author: Stephen Miran

articles.title: Carol Shiue and Wolfgang Keller find that descendants of elites that lost status after the fall of the Ming Dynasty in 1644 regained elite status in subsequent generations by achievement in civil service exams, “the ultimate gateway to power.”
articles.author: Carol Shiue and Wolfgang Keller

articles.title: Zach Mazlish argues that households suffered a 1.7% median annualized decline in post-tax real income during Biden’s term, far more negative than alternative metrics of the real income trends, helping to explain the degree of discontent with Biden.
articles.author: J. Zachary Mazlish

articles.title: .@verdadcap cites evidence from Shleifer and argues “Although the short-term forecasts, outside of recessions, are decent, the longer-term forecasts are so bad as to be a contrarian indicator.”
articles.author: Luke Burke and Daniel Rasmussen

articles.title: US net international investment income is "finally" heading below zero, and is already negative when income earned by US corporations in low-tax jurisdictions is removed. @Brad_Setser
articles.author: Brad Setser and Michael Weilandt

articles.title: .@MacrostrategyP argues that the EU has “traded growth for ideology” with “renewable electricity price 5x that of conventional electricity.” In March 2024 German businesses’ electricity prices were 1.7x prices in the US and 2.8x in China.
articles.author: Andrew Lees

articles.title: According to a Bloomberg analysis, China's trade surplus is set to reach almost $1T this year, with the goods surplus rising 16% y/y. FDI may be on track for the first annual net outflow since 1990.
articles.author: Bloomberg Staff

articles.title: Torsten Sløk notes that despite a "solid cyclical position," the  2.9% primary US deficit (excluding interest payments) is the largest among advanced economies.  Overall the US deficit is ~ 6% of GDP.
articles.author: Torsten Sløk

articles.title: .@swinshi proposes a new price index to remove biases that overstate cost of living increases and finds that from 1979 to 2021 real market income for a middle-quintile household with children increased 59.9% vs. 35.3% under PCEPI and 12.4% under CPI-U.
articles.author: Scott Winship

articles.title: Ruy Teixeira notes that according to AP VoteCast data, the 17pp overall gender gap was unchanged from 2024. "The gender gap did widen among young voters, but it was because young men moved more sharply toward Trump than young women did."
articles.author: Ruy Teixeira

articles.title: The median wealth of unmarried women without children is $87,200, on par with the wealth of unmarried men at $82,100. 50% of unmarried women without children own a home, vs. 47% of unmarried men. @PewResearch
articles.author: Richard Fry

articles.title: .@I_Am_NickBloom et al find that areas with low human capital and high initial manufacturing share saw less services job growth to offset China shock manufacturing losses, explaining 50% of the 1997-2007 job growth gap vs. high human capital regions.
articles.author: Nicholas Bloom, Kyle Handley, André Kurmann, and Philip Luck

articles.title: US firms that both export and import account for 84% of the total value of exports and 91% of the total value of imports. Broad-based tariffs impacting intermediate goods would hurt US export competitiveness. @farihakamal2011
articles.author: Fariha Kamal

articles.title: By equalizing average neighborhood quality for black and white families, Kawano et al estimate that the Army's quasi-random locational assignment reduces the black-white earnings gap among the children of Army personnel at age 25 by 23%.
articles.author: Laura Kawano, Bruce Sacerdote, William Skimmyhorn and Michael Stevens

articles.title: A study of MA community college cohorts between 2005-16 estimates that degree attainment in STEM within 6 years rose for males in all racial categories, largely driven by an increase in male enrollment in STEM from 15.4% in 2005 to 24.6% in 2016.
articles.author: Richard Murnane, John Willett, Aubrey McDonough, et al.

articles.title: Rural counties saw population growth of ~ 1mm persons between 2020-23 largely from urban areas which likely will depolarize the countries politically. As of 2022, 32% of new residents in Trump rural counties came from blue counties. @AugustBenzow
articles.author: August Benzow

articles.title: .@RogerPielkeJr argues that more realistic projections of global population and GDP growth suggest that “even partial future policy successes could more easily bring into view future emissions consistent with a 2C temperature target.”
articles.author: Roger Pielke Jr.

articles.title: The International Energy Agency has raised their 2030 forecast for global coal consumption by 6% vs. last year’s forecast. Fossil fuels represented ⅔ of the increase in energy demand in 2023 vs. 2022. @JavierBlas
articles.author: Javier Blas

articles.title: Unlike short recent time series, three centuries of data on private short-term rates in the U.S. and U.K. show that real r* reverts to a smoothly declining trend following shocks and term premia have risen as inflation volatility fell. @paul_schmelzing
articles.author: Kenneth Rogoff, Barbara Rossi and Paul Schmelzing

articles.title: .@GregObenshain cites a @mjmauboussin analysis that finds profitability varies more within industry than across industries, and argues "Industry is not destiny. Great companies can emerge from mediocre industries."
articles.author: Greg Obenshain

articles.title: In 1980 white non-college men employed full-time earned 7% more than the average full-time US worker. In 2022, their inflation-adjusted income has remained relatively flat, and they earned less than women with a college degree.
articles.author: Emily Badger, Robert Gebeloff and Aatish Bhatia

articles.title: List and Uchida find that the “fade-out effect” of early childhood education programs “is critically-linked to the share of classroom peers assigned to preschool access—with enough treated peers the classic fade-out effect is muted.” @Econ_4_Everyone @haruka__uchida
articles.author: John List and Haruka Uchida

articles.title: .@ModeledBehavior @cojobrien finds that the current expansion is the first time in 50 years that manufacturing jobs have fully recovered from a recession, but the recovery is not reaching areas hit by the “China Shock.”
articles.author: Connor O'Brien and Adam Ozimek

articles.title: Over the past 5 years, the US has won more science Nobels than the rest of the world combined. The US science workforce is highly reliant on non-native talent: 43% of doctorate-holders in the US STEM workforce are foreign-born.
articles.author: Jeff Tollefson and Richard Van Noorden

articles.title: .@GoldmanSachs estimates that the S&P 500 will deliver an annualized nominal total return of 3% over the next decade, roughly 1% on a real basis. The 13% annualized return over the last decade exceeded the long-term mean of 11%.
articles.author: David Kostin, Ben Snider, Ryan Hammond, et al.

articles.title: According to the IMF, US gross fixed capital formation will rise 4.5% y/y in 2024 - well above the 2006-15 US mean of 1.2% and more than 3 times the rate for all advanced economies, several of which are expected to experience a fall.
articles.author: Josh Mitchell

articles.title: Andrew Lees @MacrostrategyP argues that the US current account deficit is being funded in part by the flight of capital from Europe, which cannot continue indefinitely.
articles.author: Andrew Lees

articles.title: CPS disposable income data indicate a large poverty decline in 2021 and a large increase in 2022, while consumption poverty steadily declined over 2021-2022, partly explained by savings from 2021 unemployment and COVID-era cash transfers. @AEIecon
articles.author: Bruce Meyer, Jeehoon Han, and James Sullivan

articles.title: A survey of abortion providers finds in the 13 states that have enacted near-total abortion bans, the number of women receiving abortions increased in all but three. Nationwide, there were 587,000 abortions in the first half of 2024, up 12% y/y.
articles.author: Claire Cain Miller and Margot Sanger-Katz

articles.title: At ~ 3.5% of GDP, US R&D spending is at an all-time high, driven by a surge in business spending on R&D starting around 2016. US R&D spending is higher in absolute and relative terms than that of the EU and China. @TimothyTTaylor
articles.author: Timothy Taylor

articles.title: The US is spending 3.06% of GDP or 18% of federal revenue on debt service, the highest level since 1996. Interest payments are higher than defense spending for the first time.
articles.author: Viktoria Dendrinou

articles.title: Kenneth Austin and @hillelnadler argue that tax and other preferences for foreigners holding U.S. assets are a driving force behind the large U.S. current account deficits and negative net asset positions, and recommend reducing their generosity.
articles.author: Kenneth Austin and Hillel Nadler

articles.title: Net foreign claims on US assets are now 80% of US GDP, the most negative in history. @GeneralTheorist notes that this is partly a result of elevated U.S. equity prices, and may be resolved by a stock market correction rather than a fall in the dollar.
articles.author: Chris Marsh

articles.title: Rumors of deglobalization are overstated, @Brad_Setser argues, citing China’s expanded exports. Since the pandemic, China's exports are up 3pp relative to GDP, and its manufacturing trade surplus is at a record high.
articles.author: Brad Setser

articles.title: Btw 2017 and 2023, major crimes in NYC committed by perpetrators < 18 surged 37%. Kevin O’Connor, a retired assistant commissioner warns, "These are the future adults. What are these kids going to be like when they’re 20 or 25 years old?"
articles.author: Maria Cramer

articles.title: In 2000, there were 27 Americans aged 65+ for every 100 prime-age workers (20-49). In 15 years, there will be 54. Acemoglu argues that investment in human capital is needed to take on new tasks, including technical and advanced precision work.
articles.author: Daron Acemoglu

articles.title: Bloomberg finds that the price of the American Dream has risen sharply. In 2016 childcare cost the mean family ~ 25% of weekly income; it's risen to over 33%. A 20% down payment on a median-priced home cost 65% of annual income in 2016, vs. 83% now.
articles.author: Shawn Donnan and Claire Ballentine

articles.title: .@jburnmurdoch shows that Silicon Valley’s politics recently became deeply polarized “into an increasingly outspoken conservative venture capital industry and a still left-leaning broader tech sector.”
articles.author: John Burn-Murdoch

articles.title: Using 2000-15 IRS data on 108mm self-employed Americans, Anmol Bhandari and team find the self-employed "have significantly higher average income and steeper, more persistent income growth profiles" than paid-employed peers with similar characteristics.
articles.author: Anmol Bhandari, Tobey Kass, Thomas May, Ellen McGrattan and Evan Schulz

articles.title: On average, 35% of degrees at liberal arts colleges have a negative ROI vs. 23% for all other colleges. Engineering, computer science, nursing, and economics degrees have the highest ROI. @PrestonCooper93 @AEIecon
articles.author: Preston Cooper

articles.title: .@ernietedeschi analyzes Trump's proposed tariffs, both with and without retaliation, and projects $1.2-$4.4T in revenue over 10 years, a 0.5-1.4% decline in real GDP, and a $1.9-7.6k decline in average real disposable income per household.
articles.author: Ernie Tedeschi

articles.title: .@mjmauboussin reviews ROIC from 1963-2023 including adjustment for intangibles, and finds that “the variance within industries is greater than the variance across industries. The industry is important but does not dictate a firm’s destiny.”
articles.author: Michael Mauboussin and Dan Callahan

articles.title: An @IEA global analysis finds air conditioning will require an additional 697 terawatt hours of electricity by 2030, 3x the forecast growth from new data centers.
articles.author: Malcolm Moore

articles.title: Between 2011-23 electrical demand in the US increased by just 4.4%; @IEA forecasts that electrical demand will rise by 25% between 2023-35, with 60% of the growth driven by the uptake of EVs.
articles.author: IEA Staff

articles.title: Since 1990 labor productivity per hour in the US has increased 73%, outpacing Japan's 55% increase and the Euro Area's 39% gain. @S_Rabinovitch and @currhenry speculate this outperformance is driven by higher investment and business dynamism.
articles.author: Simon Rabinovitch and Henry Curr

articles.title: Between 1990 and today, the US share of G7 GDP increased from 40% to ~ 50%. Mean wages in Mississippi, the poorest state in the US, are higher than mean wages in the UK, Canada, and Germany. @S_Rabinovitch @currhenry
articles.author: Simon Rabinovitch and Henry Curr

articles.title: The US nuclear modernization program will cost ~ $1.7T over 30 years, including new bomber jets, land-based missiles, and thermonuclear warheads. Overall 2024 defense spending was 2.7% of GDP in 2024, vs. 5.6% at the end of the Cold War.
articles.author: W.J. Hennigan

articles.title: The IMF projects that global public debt levels will approach 100% of global GDP by 2030, up from ~65% in 2007, primarily driven by the US and China. They argue that “much larger fiscal adjustments than currently planned are required to stabilize debt.”
articles.author: IMF Staff

articles.title: Parental benefits have failed to raise birth rates in advanced economies. Both Hungary and Norway spend more than 3% of GDP on efforts to raise fertility. Yet their birth rates remain under the US rate of 1.6 and the replacement rate of 2.1.
articles.author: Chelsey Dulaney

articles.title: .@MichaelRStrain notes that US manufacturing employment declined steadily both before and after "The China Shock," and cautions against the bipartisan turn to industrial policy and protectionism.
articles.author: Michael Strain

articles.title: Savings from the 2022 Inflation Reduction Act will fall well short of @USCBO estimates. The promised deficit reduction will be 60% lower, mostly due to the higher-than-forecast cost of prescription drug coverage under Medicare Part D.
articles.author: James Capretta

articles.title: .@joshrauh forecasts that “if the average interest rate the federal government paid on its debt crept up to just 4.4%,” interest payments would exceed 29.7% of federal revenue in 2034.  He argues the US is "playing with fire."
articles.author: Joshua Rauh

articles.title: The share of US firms with growth over 130%/year declined from 18% in 1985 to 12% in 2015, driven by a decline in new firm formation and slower growth among existing firms. @ngoldschlag @JHaltiwanger_UM @jdanielkim
articles.author: J. Daniel Kim, Joonkyu Choi, Nathan Goldschlag et al.

articles.title: Richard Rogerson and team infer 20% of the inequality in lifetime earnings among American men can be explained by differences in hours worked, and that 90% of the difference in hours worked is explained by worker preferences.
articles.author: Alexander Bick, Adam Blandin, and Richard Rogerson

articles.title: Slowing the uptake of new healthcare services by Medicare would mean "Medicare’s expenditures would shrink from 3.2% to 2.4% of GDP, rather than increasing to 5.4%." @biggsag and @CPopeHC suggest a supplemental premium to cover new services.
articles.author: Andrew Biggs and Chris Pope

articles.title: .@ProfDavidDeming and @LHSummers find higher “churn” in the labor market over 2010-2024 relative to 1990-2019. Growth in low-paid service jobs has “stalled.” Employment in STEM has risen by more than 50%, and retail sales jobs have declined by 25%.
articles.author: David Deming, Christopher Ong, and Lawrence Summers

articles.title: Raj Chetty @OppInsights finds that a white child born into the bottom household income quintile in 1992 had a 29.7% chance of remaining there, up from 24.9% in 1978, and an 11.9% chance of rising to the top quintile, down from 13.7% in 1978.
articles.author: Raj Chetty, Will Dobbie, Benjamin Goldman, Sonya Porter and Crystal Yang

articles.title: Over the last decade, US equities have delivered a 529% total compounded return in dollar terms, vs. 125% for the rest of the developed world. Given current elevated P/E ratios, US companies need about 7% EPS growth to earn a 4% risk premium over bonds.
articles.author: Atul Narayan and Alex Greene

articles.title: More than 4.5mm Americans aged 18-25 use marijuana daily or nearly daily. A @nytimes analysis finds 1/3 of American marijuana users have reported symptoms of cannabis use disorder, including 16.6% of users 18-25.
articles.author: Megan Twohey, Danielle Ivory, and Carson Kessler

articles.title: By the end of this year, the US economy will be 11% larger than it was prior to the pandemic while Canada's will be 6%. Canada’s GDP/person is a bit lower than Alabama, the fourth-poorest US state.
articles.author: Economist Staff

articles.title: China comprises 17% of global GDP but 29% of manufacturing and 13% of consumption while the US is 26% of GDP, 16% of manufacturing, and 26% of consumption. @michaelxpettis favors capital controls over tariffs to correct these imbalances.
articles.author: Michael Pettis and Erica Hogan

articles.title: Between 1979 and 2022 prime-age men in the bottom quintile saw their inflation-adjusted market income rise 10%, to an all-time high of $22,800/year. Their female counterparts saw their market income rise 63% to $20,300/year. @swinshi
articles.author: Scott Winship

articles.title: American equities make up 50% of the world’s market cap, up from 30% in 2009.
articles.author: Torsten Sløk

articles.title: A @pewresearch analysis finds the immigrant population of the US increased by 1.6mm in 2023 to a record 47.8mm. Their 14.3% share of the population is approaching the 14.8% peak of 1890.
articles.author: John Gramlich and Jeffrey Passel

articles.title: In the 2000 presidential election, of the 317 counties that received transfers equal to at least 25% of total personal income, 59% voted for the GOP candidate. In 2020, 88% of 1,986 counties voted GOP.
articles.author: Aaron Zitner, Jon Kamp, and Brian McGill

articles.title: .@JHaltiwanger_UM @UpdatedPriors document the continued high rate of new business formation by “likely employers” since 2020, and argue that “the pandemic and its aftermath featured a surge in genuine entrepreneurial employer business creation.”
articles.author: Ryan Decker and John Haltiwanger

articles.title: .@BennSteil cites investment in new manufacturing facilities and argues, “By 2033, 3.8 million new positions will open, yet only half of these are likely to be filled” due to retirement-linked withdrawals from the manufacturing labor force.
articles.author: Benn Steil and Elisabeth Harding

articles.title: Transfer payments made up 18% of all US personal income in 2022, up from 8% in 1970. Social Security/Medicare made up 56% of the increase from 1970 to 2022, and income maintenance programs/Medicaid accounted for 35%. @kenanfikri @BenGlasner
articles.author: Kenan Fikri, Sarah Eckhardt and Benjamin Glasner

articles.title: Real wages for non-managerial workers, who make up 80% of the private workforce, are above their pre-pandemic trend, while wages for all workers remain below the pre-pandemic trend. @arindube
articles.author: Arin Dube

articles.title: Across the West, the age curve of activity-limiting health conditions has flattened due to a rise in disability rates among people in their 20's and 30's. “But there is little change — or even a reduction — further up the age distribution.” @jburnmurdoch
articles.author: John Burn-Murdoch

articles.title: Crime statistics miss the rise in public disorder, which @CharlesFLehman describes as the "domination of public space for private purposes." He argues that police should focus their resources on phenomena like homelessness, litter, and graffiti.
articles.author: Charles Fain Lehman

articles.title: US entitlement and pension spending is crowding out government spending on public services; in the 1950s spending on public services was 25% of GDP, and entitlement/pension spending was about 3%. Today they are at par at 15%. @econcallum
articles.author: Callum Williams

articles.title: OpenAI CEO Sam Altman is pitching the Biden Administration on “the economic and national security benefits of building 5GW data centers in various US states.” Each facility would require power generation equivalent to five nuclear reactors.
articles.author: Shirin Ghaffary

articles.title: Last year 1.14mm people filled asylum claims in Europe and 2024 will likely see the same level. The majority who arrived in the Netherlands since 1999 and have left school “are unemployed, though the country suffers from extreme labor shortages.”
articles.author: Tom Fairless and Laurence Norman

articles.title: Mark Warshawsky and Giorgi Bokhua @AEIecon estimate that the US has a sustainable gross debt limit of 154% of GDP, “which is only 30 percentage points above the current level, projected to be reached by 2034.”
articles.author: Mark Warshawsky and Giorgi Bokhua

articles.title: In 2024 27% of Asians who took the SAT scored between 1400-1600; 7% of White, 2% of Hispanic, and 1% of Black test takers scored in that range. @cremieuxrecueil
articles.author: Cremieux

articles.title: .@FedGuy12 argues that the rise in wages as Japan's workforce shrinks suggests that aging demographics are likely to be inflationary. Unless offset by automation, this will also put upward pressure on interest rates as the BOJ confronts inflation.
articles.author: Joseph Wang

articles.title: .@pkedrosky argues that the historical relationship between labor markets and wages is being severed by automation. “Our economic frameworks are increasingly misaligned with the realities of an automated economy.”
articles.author: Paul Kedrosky

articles.title: US tech firms have shed about 137,000 jobs since January 2024 and software development job postings are down 30% from the start of 2020, as companies focus on revenue-generating products and services, and rely more heavily on global talent.
articles.author: Katherine Bindley and Joseph Pisani

articles.title: A 2-adult, 3-child US family with $20,000 of market income receives at least $61,000 in annual benefits and has $79,000 of disposable income. That same family earning a market income of $80,000 takes home only $11,000 more. @kevincorinth
articles.author: Kevin Corinth

articles.title: .@jburnmurdoch finds that across multiple developed countries “there are more young men than women in effect outside the economy for the first time in history,” neither employed, looking for work, or in education.
articles.author: John Burn-Murdoch

articles.title: From 2019 to 2021, income after transfers and taxes for the top quintile grew by 19% (largely from realized capital gains), the bottom quintile grew by 17% (primarily from temporary transfers), and the middle quintiles by 5%.
articles.author: Bilal Habib, Edward Harris, John McClelland, and Joseph Rosenberg

articles.title: .@DanielDiMartino notes higher-skilled immigrants reduce expected deficits, and proposes a policy of selective legalization, coupled with mass deportations to reduce Federal debt by about $1.9 trillion.
articles.author: Daniel Di Martino

articles.title: An EC study of European competitiveness finds that EU gross value-added per hour worked increased by 0.7%/year from 2000-19, vs. 1.2%/year in the US. “Europe is lagging in the breakthrough digital technologies that will drive growth in the future.”
articles.author: Mario Draghi and Staff

articles.title: China’s steel exports surged by 35% in 2023 vs. 2022, increasing by more than total US or Japanese production. In August, barely 1% of the 250 steel mills in China that reported their finances to the government turned a profit.
articles.author: Economist Staff

articles.title: .@martinwolf_ notes that Europe has fallen behind the US in technological innovation: in the last 50 years, no new EU-based company has achieved a market cap > €100B while the US has 5 new firms worth >$1T.
articles.author: Martin Wolf

articles.title: A Bloomberg analysis finds that in 2023, 12% of migrants seeking asylum and refugees went to swing states, and 72% of these went to counties that voted for Biden in 2020.
articles.author: Elena Mejía and Shawn Donnan

articles.title: .@JADHazell @jptguerreiro @ChenLian92 argue inflation is costlier to workers than the decline in real wages, as countering wage erosion requires costly conflict with employers. Workers would sacrifice 1.75% of their real wages to avoid conflict.
articles.author: Joao Guerreiro, Jonathon Hazell, Chen Lian et al.

articles.title: .@FT quotes data provider IT Juzi, “In 2018, at the height of VC investment, 51,302 start-ups were founded in China. By 2023, that figure had collapsed to 1,202 and is on track to be even lower this year.”
articles.author: Eleanor Olcott and Wang Xueqiao

articles.title: Real median US household income of $80,610 remains below its 2019 level of $81,210.
articles.author: Jon Kamp and Paul Overberg

articles.title: Stanford sociologist Michael Rosenfeld finds that declining social connections during COVID led to 13.3mm more single Americans in 2022 vs. pre-pandemic, delaying traditional adult milestones like getting married, having children, or buying a home.
articles.author: Ben Steverman

articles.title: Mark Schneider @AEIecon reviews the ROI of master’s degree programs and finds “vast differences among fields of study and among programs in the same field of study.” He argues that policymakers need to provide accurate ROI metrics to applicants.
articles.author: Mark Schneider

articles.title: .@JADHazell and @sj_hobler review inflation swap market reactions to the results of ‘21 Georgia Senate run-offs which allowed Democrats to pass legislation on a party-line vote. They infer that the resultant stimulus caused ~30% of 2021-22 inflation.
articles.author: Jonathon Hazell and Stephan Hobler

articles.title: .@ProfDavidDeming and @m_silliman find “the economic return to social skills in the US more than doubled” between the 1980s and the 2000s, and argue for a multi-dimensional concept of human capital.
articles.author: David Deming and Mikko Silliman

articles.title: A meta-analysis of international studies finds a 24% correlation between education achievement, as measured by grades, and “polygenic scores that are person-specific aggregates of inherited DNA differences.”
articles.author: Kirsty Wilding, Megan Wright, and Sophie von Stumm

articles.title: Bruce Meyer finds that when recipients of a variety of US government assistance programs complete Census Bureau surveys, they significantly underreport program participation. Black and Hispanic recipients show the highest rates of underreporting.
articles.author: Bruce Meyer, Nikolas Mittag and Derek Wu

articles.title: Since the end of 2020, net immigration has lifted the US population by 9 million people, contributing to annual population growth of 1.2%, the highest since the early 1990s. The bulk of the 6.5mm net illegal immigrants came across the southern border.
articles.author: Paul Kiernan

articles.title: The share of lending in the form of “private credit” has grown dramatically since the financial crisis. Private credit may now be a $1.5 trillion industry that is at least as large as the syndicated loans market. @jared_ellias and Elisabeth de Fontenay
articles.author: Jared Ellias and Elisabeth de Fontenay

articles.title: .@sffed analysts forecast that CPI shelter inflation will decline from 5.1% in July ‘24 to its pre-pandemic average of 3.3% in spring ‘25, based on trends in new units under construction and completed, net household formation, and rents on new leases.
articles.author: Òscar Jordà and Aren Yalcin
articles.pub: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco

articles.title: .@BLS_gov projects that jobs in the top wage quartile will grow by 8.9% per year from 2023-33, over 3X the rate for bottom-quartile jobs. @JedKolko notes this is a shift from the pre-pandemic period.
articles.author: Jed Kolko

articles.title: .@albertocavallo @SEAlvarezBlaser @_amackay and @Paolo_Mengano find that the total US markup of prices over production costs was stable over June 2018-June 2023. Increased production costs rather than markups explain soaring retail prices during 2022.
articles.author: Santiago Alvarez, Alberto Cavallo, Alexander MacKay and Paolo Mengano

articles.title: Noting that 80% of American jobs are outside of the S&P 500, Torsten Sløk argues that "privately owned companies continue to be the lifeblood of the US economy."
articles.author: Torsten Sløk

articles.title: According to World Bank data, manufacturing value added as a % of GDP in sub-Saharan Africa fell from 18% in 1981 to 11% in 2023. This series includes South Africa, Nigeria, and Ethiopia.
articles.author: David Pilling

articles.title: Sub-Saharan Africa will have 25% of the world's working-age population by 2050. Currently, the region accounts for 3% of global output. Assuming 2% productivity growth, Bridgewater expects this to double by 2050.
articles.author: Devon Long and Eka Zhao

articles.title: .@FT notes that “a number of places in Republican-dominated areas are eager for an influx of workers” to offset demographic decline. @BrookingsInst analysis finds that between 2010 and 2020 the US White population declined by 5.1mm.
articles.author: Eva Xiao and Taylor Nicole Rogers

articles.title: .@HannoLustig and @rgomezcram argue the pandemic was a period of “fiscal dominance” in which bondholders insulated taxpayers from increased spending by absorbing capital losses. From March 2020 to March 2022, the government’s real debt fell by 10%.
articles.author: Roberto Gomez-Cram, Howard Kung, and Hanno Lustig
articles.pub: Federal Reserve Bank of Kansas City

articles.title: Fiscal deficits at current levels will manifest in "substantial inflationary pressure, substantial indigestion in the Treasury markets with financial chaos, or substantial crowding out of what would be otherwise valuable private investment." @LHSummers
articles.author: Larry Summers, Bob Rubin, Greg Jensen, et al.

articles.title: When the race was Biden-Trump, women aged 18-29 favored Biden by 28pp while their male peers favored Trump by 11pp, a 39pp gender gap. That gap has risen to 51pp, with young women preferring Harris by 38pp and men favoring Trump by 13pp.
articles.author: Claire Cain Miller

articles.title: Noting that government deficits have exceeded total home sales by 16% for the 4th time in 5 years, Michael Solon argues chronic deficits, not constraints on building or the Fed, are ultimately responsible for the decline in housing affordability.
articles.author: Michael Solon

articles.title: Noting the growing gap between demand and supply in China, @M_C_Klein depicts Chinese producers as “increasingly reliant on dumping excess output into world markets."
articles.author: Matt Klein

articles.title: @XJaravel finds inflation rates differ by income quintiles. Accounting for inflation inequality reduces 1983-2019 real income growth for the bottom quintile from 12.5% to 2.1%, and from 19.1% to 12.0% for the next, with little change at the top.
articles.author: Xavier Jaravel

articles.title: .@PierpaBenigno and @GautiEggertsson argue that inflation rose sharply in 2022 due to labor market tightening well beyond the “Beveridge Threshold,” where job vacancies exceed unemployed workers.
articles.author: Pierpaolo Benigno and Gauti Eggertsson
articles.pub: Federal Reserve Bank of Kansas City

articles.title: Chinese export volumes adjusted for FX rates were up 10% since the end of 2021 relative to 1.5% for the rest of the world. @michaelxpettis notes of the export strategy, "The rest of the world wants the opposite. The world cannot accommodate it.”
articles.author: Lingling Wei and Jason Douglas

articles.title: A @NewYorkFed analysis of 33 countries over 1966-2020 finds 1 s.d. higher growth rate in bank credit increases the probability of real GDP growth below -2% in two years’ time by 2.5 percentage points, while nonbank credit has the opposite effect.
articles.author: Nina Boyarchenko and Leonardo Elias
articles.pub: Federal Reserve Bank of New York

articles.title: China lent $1T over the last two decades under its Belt & Road Initiative, but emerging markets may face a credit crunch due to a decline in China’s lending as its economic growth slows. @AEIecon
articles.author: Michael Beckley

articles.title: While noting a decline in homicides reported by the FBI, @foxjust argues the upcoming release of the 2023 National Crime Victimization Survey will be a better confirmation that the crime wave is in fact receding.
articles.author: Justin Fox

articles.title: .@ChadBown and team find that China’s tariff reductions in its WTO accession exceeded reciprocity norms, increasing real incomes but driving an incremental 4% decline in labor demand for tradable goods in the rest of the world.
articles.author: Chad Bown, Lorenzo Caliendo, Fernando Parro, Robert Staiger, and Alan Sykes

articles.title: Start-up failures are up 60% year-over-year in Q1 2024 and are running at 7x the 2019 rate according to @cartainc data. Only 9% of venture funds raised in 2021 have returned capital to investors, vs. 25% of 2017 funds in their third year of operation.
articles.author: George Hammond

articles.title: .@GeorgeWill argues, “Chicago’s population has declined for 9 consecutive years as education has cratered, crime has soared, and public services have withered because almost 1/2 of the city budget goes to debt service and government employees’ pensions.”
articles.author: George Will

articles.title: An @TheEconomist analysis of Gallup data estimates that in the absence of barriers to entry, 23mm college graduates would move to the US, 17mm to Canada, and 9mm to Australia. China would lose 14mm, and India 12mm.
articles.author: Economist Staff

articles.title: .@jmhorp finds that on average US wage earners are slightly better off than they were 4 years ago: CPI-adjusted real wages are up 0.3% since the start of the Biden Administration relative to a 6.6% increase during Trump's first term.
articles.author: Jeremy Horpedahl

articles.title: .@Brad_Setser argues that a new method China uses to account for foreign firms’ contract manufacturing in China for sale in China understates the current account surplus by $300B/year as of Q2 2024 and that the actual surplus is close to $700B.
articles.author: Brad Setser

articles.title: Maurice Obstfeld argues that a "demand switch from import-competing goods toward cheaper Chinese imports"  helps explain the China shock; he argues this would have occurred even if the US trade deficit had been smaller.
articles.author: Maurice Obstfeld

articles.title: The ADP software developer employment index was up 11% in Oct. 2019 vs. Jan. 2018, but by Jan 2024 it had fallen to 17% below 2018 levels. Hat tip to Brent Orrell @AEIecon.
articles.author: Jeff Nezaj

articles.title: At the start of 2024, the real wealth of Millennials (aged 27 to 44) was ~25% higher than Gen Xers and Baby Boomers at the same point in their life cycles, driven largely by $2.5 trilliongrowth in net housing wealth. @WSJ
articles.author: Joe Pinsker and Veronica Dagher

articles.title: .@FedGuy12 notes private credit has “blunted the impact of tightening monetary policy by actively lending to a wide range of businesses,” and that bank credit is “normalizing towards a pre-pandemic pace.”
articles.author: Joseph Wang

articles.title: Housing affordability has fallen to levels last seen in the 1980s, driven primarily by high home prices relative to median family income. Mortgage rates around 6.5% remain below the long-term average since 1971.
articles.author: Nicole Friedman and Alana Pipe

articles.title: Citing BCG data, Bloomberg estimates that Chips Act grants will increase the global share of US fabs to 14% in 2032 from 10% today. In the absence of the Chips Act, BCG estimates the US share would have declined to 8%.
articles.author: Mackenzie Hawkins

articles.title: The FT finds that 40% of the major manufacturing investments receiving tax credits, loans, and grants via the Inflation Reduction Act and Chips and Science Act "have been delayed for between two months and several years, or paused indefinitely."
articles.author: Amanda Chu, Alexandra White, and Rhea Basarkar

articles.title: Kevin Daly notes that r* tracks US/Global r*, while cross-country variation is driven by GDP per capita, inflation, and current account and government balances.
articles.author: Kevin Daly

articles.title: .@RBC analysts argue that slow investment is the biggest cause of Canada’s drop from the 6th most productive economy in the OECD in 1970 to the 18th as of 2022.
articles.author: Nathan Janzen, Rajeshni Naidu-Ghelani, and Darren Chow

articles.title: .@KurmannAndre and team find that the relative generosity of CARES Act unemployment insurance programs “held back the aggregate employment recovery by 4.7 percentage points between April and December 2020.”
articles.author: Andreas Hornstein, Marios Karabarbounis, Andre Kurmann, Etienne Lale and Lien Ta

articles.title: Maurice Obstfeld argues the Federal deficit is "a main driver of the trade deficit" and lowering spending would improve the US trade balance by reducing both government and private demand in a more efficient way than capital controls or tariffs.
articles.author: Maurice Obstfeld

articles.title: An experiment giving 1,000 individuals $1k per month for 3 years raised spending on housing as well as consumption, but also increased indebtedness, suggesting transfers may have little impact on the financial health of young, low-income households.
articles.author: Alexander Bartik, Elizabeth Rhodes, David Broockman, et al.

articles.title: .@jasonfurman notes a 19bps decline in the 10-year yield after Friday's job report, despite the FOMC not cutting. He suggests that a 50bps cut in September may be appropriate.
articles.author: Jason Furman

articles.title: .@davidautor et al. find that the labor-replacing impact of automation innovations has intensified in the past four decades, while the demand-increasing effects of augmentation innovations have not.
articles.author: David Autor, Caroline Chin, Anna Salomons, et al.

articles.title: Real median weekly earnings for non-college workers grew by 4.6% from Q4 2019 to Q1 2024, vs. 3.3% for their college peers, a reversal from the Great Recession “when real college wages consistently grew faster than non-college wages.” @ernietedeschi
articles.author: Ernie Tedeschi

articles.title: An additional senator or representative per mm residents predicted about $1,000 dollars in additional COVID aid per capita over 2020-2022, yielding incumbents an extra 4 ppt of the two-candidate vote share. @jeffreypclemens @payson_julia @stanveuger
articles.author: Jeffrey Clemens, Julia Payson, and Stan Veuger

articles.title: @drj_policy @AEI argues that from 1982–2021 antitrust enforcement standards “have become more pro-enforcement—exactly the opposite of the accepted wisdom.”
articles.author: Mark Jamison

articles.title: Long-term real rates over the last 7 centuries show a long downward trend and exhibit no positive correlation with growth in output or population. @krogoff @paul_schmelzing
articles.author: Kenneth Rogoff, Barbara Rossi, and Paul Schmelzing

articles.title: .@gabriel_zucman argues that Auten & Splinter’s work contains “empirical errors, conceptual errors, and questionable assumptions,” primarily the misallocation of partnership depreciation to sole proprietors rather than the owners of the partnerships.
articles.author: Thomas Piketty, Emmanuel Saez and Gabriel Zucman

articles.title: .@sc_cath @mjmill611 and @NatashaRSarin calculate that the market value of future Social Security benefits represented 49% of the wealth of the bottom 90% in 2019. Including Social Security, the wealth share of the top 1% had risen only 1.6pp since 1989.
articles.author: Sylvain Catherine, Max Miller, and Natasha Sarin

articles.title: Giving low income individuals $12,000/year for 3 years resulted in reduced market income of $1,500/year, due to a 2ppt reduction in labor force participation and 1.3-1.4 hour/week reduction in labor hours. @evavivalt @AlexBartik @dbroockman
articles.author: Eva Vivalt, Elizabeth Rhodes, Alexander Bartik, et al.

articles.title: .@KyleLHandley argues the "China Shock" has likely run its course; as of 2020 import penetration was well under the 2000-2010 and 2010-2018 trends.
articles.author: Kyle Handley

articles.title: Hendrik Bessembinder finds that from 1925 to 2023 51.6% of equities had a negative return; however, 17 stocks had cumulative returns greater than $50,000 per dollar invested, led by Altria Group with a return of $2.65mm for each dollar invested.
articles.author: Hendrik Bessembinder

articles.title: Using data from tax filers and W-2 recipients from 1998–2019, @AbigailWozniak, @john_voorheis, and @kevinrinz find that earnings for both Black men and Black women fell behind earnings for White men following the Great Recession.
articles.author: Illenin Kondo, Kevin Rinz, Natalie Gubbay, et al.

articles.title: Children who grew up in counties with higher employment rates had more economic mobility than peers raised in counties with lower rates, even if their parents weren’t employed, suggesting a relationship mediated by social interaction. @OppInsights
articles.author: Raj Chetty, Will Dobbie, Benjamin Goldman, et al.

articles.title: Using cross-state data they collected on Big Macs earned Per Hour (BMPH) to compute likely wages in the absence of minimum wage laws, Orley Ashenfelter and Štěpán Jurajda find minimum wages had a much smaller influence post-pandemic than in 2016.
articles.author: Orley Ashenfelter and Štěpán Jurajda

articles.title: In 2019, urban wages for workers with a high school degree or less failed to offset higher urban housing costs, reversing a long-term trend. @stanveuger suggests loosening restrictions on housing construction to "reignite the urban growth machine."
articles.author: Stan Veuger

articles.title: Martin Wolf argues the aging of rich countries with low fertility rates and the need for low-skilled in-person work will likely result in widespread "temporary contract work, without either family reunification or the possibility of citizenship."
articles.author: Martin Wolf

articles.title: A 2020 mandate that reduced the emission of sulfur dioxide from international shipping by about 80% likely doubled the rate of global warming by reducing the amount of light being reflected back into space.
articles.author: Tianle Yuan, Hua Song, Lazaros Oreopoulos, et al.

articles.title: Using information available to market participants in each period, Michael Kiley finds that the decline in 10-year Treasury rates since 1990 is attributed to a decline in the expected value of short-term interest rates, with no trend in the term premium.
articles.author: Michael Kiley

articles.title: .@SeanTrende @AEI notes that Republicans won 51.4% of 2022 two-party Congressional votes, and forecasts, “If Republicans match that showing in 2024, Trump will almost certainly return to the White House, with full control of the House and Senate.”
articles.author: Sean Trende

articles.title: A @sffed analysis finds that the recent spike in immigration from ~1mm/year in the 2010s to at least 3.3mm people in 2023 and 2024 drove about one-fifth of the improvement in the vacancy-to-unemployment ratio.
articles.author: Evgeniya Duzhak
articles.pub: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco

articles.title: According to Gallup data, the share of young men who identified with or leaned toward the Democratic Party has fallen from 51% in 2016 to 39% in 2023. @dcoxpolls
articles.author: Daniel Cox

articles.title: .@annastansbury finds 1st-gen college grads are 13% less likely to be tenured at a high-ranked university than PhD classmates whose parents held a non-PhD grad degree. Detailed measures of research productivity explain around a third of the “class gap.”
articles.author: Anna Stansbury and Kyra Rodriguez

articles.title: .@gchodorowreich @omzidar and Eric Zwich conclude that the 2017 Tax Cuts and Jobs Act raised tangible corporate investment by about 11%, but long-run GDP by less than 1% while reducing corporate tax revenue by 40%.
articles.author: Gabriel Chodorow-Reich, Owen Zidar and Eric Zwick

articles.title: In 2014 well over 60% of Chinese believed lack of ability and effort explained poverty; in 2023 these factors fell to 31-33%, topped by unequal opportunity at 38% and an unfair economic system at 36%. @mazzocco_ilaria @KennedyCSIS
articles.author: Ilaria Mazzocco and Scott Kennedy
articles.pub: Center for Strategic and International Studies

articles.title: Real net private investment per capita in 2022 was up 110% from 1993, led by a 460% increase in intellectual property, while residential investment failed to keep up with population growth. @zachary_bartsch
articles.author: Zachary Bartsch

articles.title: .@ernietedeschi creates a Low Wage Index, holding the labor force mix of sex, age, industry, occupation, and education constant from 1979, and finds that workers at the bottom 25th percentile saw earnings rise 20% from 1997-2007 and 25% from 2014-23.
articles.author: Ernie Tedeschi

articles.title: Market reaction to CBO scoring of legislative proposals with large negative cash flow projections implies an estimated 57¢ of each dollar in major fiscal expansions is unbacked by future surpluses and passed through to Treasury valuations. @HannoLustig
articles.author: Roberto Gomez Cram, Howard Kung, and Hanno Lustig

articles.title: The Wall Street Journal Editorial Board notes that the CBO’s June 2024 estimate of the cost of ObamaCare subsidies from 2022-31 has risen by $450B from the July 2021 estimate, with 20M people receiving subsidies, double the number in 2021.
articles.author: The Editorial Board

articles.title: .@kpomerleau argues that the expiration of the TCJA offers an opportunity to extend “the most pro-growth aspects” like the 100% bonus depreciation on a revenue-neutral basis by repealing green energy tax credits and business pass-through deductions.
articles.author: Kyle Pomerleau

articles.title: A @DallasFed analysis finds that “over the past year, the immigration wave contributed to the higher GDP growth by adding nearly 2 million newly employed workers…with very little effect on inflation.”
articles.author: Anton Cheremukhin, Sewon Hur, Ron Mau and Alexander Richter

articles.title: Labor productivity in the US manufacturing sector grew 3.4% annually between 1987-2007 but declined 0.5% annually between 2010-2022. Even the fastest-growing industries and the largest firms experienced a slowdown.
articles.author: Danial Lashkari and Jeremy Pearce
articles.pub: Federal Reserve Bank of New York

articles.title: Norwegian households with levels of financial wealth between 50-600k kroner, or $5-56k, who saw a 20% increase in their wealth from stock gains were 20% more likely to start a firm, suggesting financial constraints may impede entrepreneurship.
articles.author: Gabriel Chodorow-Reich, Plamen Nenov, Vitor Santos, et al.

articles.title: A “one size fits all” model of executive pay has become more common since 2006. This highlights the unintended consequences of the SEC’s rules for standardized pay disclosure in ‘06 and Say on Pay in ‘11, coupled with institutional investors’ influence.
articles.author: Felipe Cabezon

articles.title: A real housing price index in 30 US cities from 1890-2006 shows that rising prices are concentrated in the West Coast, Northeast, and South Coast. Across cities, there is a significant relationship between zoning and home price appreciation after 1980.
articles.author: Ronan Lyons, Allison Shertzer, Rowena Gray and David Agorastos

articles.title: .@john_iselin @danhreck propose an alternative estimate of unreported income and find that pre-tax income share of the top 1% rose by 0.52pp from 1962-2014. (labeled in the chart as “PSZ Macro.")
articles.author: John Iselin and Daniel Reck

articles.title: Foreigners have been the dominant net buyers of long-dated Treasuries over the 14 months ending April 2024, purchasing $700B, over 80% of total net issuance. @Fedguy12 warns that this would make a dollar devaluation "particularly dangerous."
articles.author: Joseph Wang

articles.title: .@michaelxpettis notes that “the manufacturing share of GDP is 3.4pp above the global average for surplus economies and 3.5pp below the global average for deficit economies,” and argues for capital controls to revive US manufacturing.
articles.author: Mike Pettis

articles.title: Between January 2020 and May 2024, the regular prices of cheaper US foods and beverages grew at 1.4 times the rate for more expensive ones, adding 8 ppts cumulative price growth relative to premium varieties. @albertocavallo
articles.author: Alberto Cavallo and Oleksiy Kryvtsov

articles.title: Despite the US foreign-born labor force growing from 28.7M in February ‘20 to 31.9M in April ‘24, a @DallasFed analysis finds that wage growth in immigrant-intensive industries has outpaced that of other industries.
articles.author: Pia Orrenius, Ana Pranger, Madeline Zavodny and Isabel Dhillon

articles.title: Data processing and media-related sectors with high technology investment and work-from-home potential saw annual productivity growth of nearly 10% from Q4 ‘19 to Q4 ‘23, while productivity in construction and wholesale trade fell by over 2%.
articles.author: Mai Chi Dao and Josef Platzer

articles.title: Japanese demand for US Treasuries is declining as domestic assets become more attractive, with 10-year JGB rates approaching 1.1%. At $1.2T Japan is the biggest foreign holder of US Treasuries.
articles.author: Torsten Sløk

articles.title: .@yfatihkarahan et al argue that 52% of the decline in US start-ups since the late 1970s is explained by a drop in labor supply growth linked to demographics. The findings are supported by cross-state startup and labor supply growth data.
articles.author: Fatih Karahan, Benjamin Pugsley and Ayşegül Şahin

articles.title: Imports valued at <$800 enter the US duty-free, and have grown from $9.2B in 2016 to $54.5B in 2023, 19% of US e-commerce spending, and are not reflected in Census trade data. These imports are largely direct-to-consumer shipments from China.
articles.author: Pablo Fajgelbaum and Amit Khandelwal

articles.title: Julia Coronado argues the US economy's outperformance relative to other advanced economies suggests “reverse hysteresis” where tight labor markets due to fiscal stimulus are driving increased investment and productivity.
articles.author: Julia Coronado

articles.title: Analysis led by Mark Warshawsky @AEIecon finds that CBO projections significantly underestimate rising healthcare costs, and proposes changing incentives to curb rising demand, and more rapid adoption of new technologies to improve productivity.
articles.author: Mark Warshawsky, John Mantus, and Gaobo Pang

articles.title: Adjusted for CPI inflation, the Q1 2024 Employment Cost Index of wages and benefits per hour is 3pp under its Q1 2021 level. @Jose_MariaRD @I_Am_NickBloom @Davis_StevenJ
articles.author: Jose Maria Barrero, Nick Bloom, Steven Davis, Kevin Foster, Brent Meyer and Emil Mihaylov

articles.title: Public company managers’ conference call comments about their cost of capital vary widely from its actual cost; 80% of the variance suggests managerial error. Those who perceive higher capital cost invest less, reducing TFP by 5%. @NielsGormsen
articles.author: Niels Joachim Gormsen and Kilian Huber

articles.title: According to a @cojobrien analysis of foreign students graduating from US universities from 2012-20, only 17% of BA recipients remained in the US, vs. 51% of Master’s and 76% of PhD recipients.
articles.author: Connor O’Brien

articles.title: As of 2016, 90% of US and Japanese households are equipped with air conditioning, compared with 60% in China, 10% in Europe, and 4% in India. @iea
articles.author: International Energy Agency
articles.pub: Brian Dean, John Dulac, Trevor Morgan and Uwe Remme

articles.title: The decrease in the gender pay gap in the US originates from the exit of age cohorts with high gender pay gaps, and the entry of recent cohorts with much lower pay gaps, due to declining opportunities for younger men, not large gains for younger women.
articles.author: Jaime Arellano-Bover, Nicola Bianchi, Salvatore Lattanzio et al.

articles.title: Analysis of firm and establishment-level data from Korea shows that software and labor are substitutes and that the rise of software accounts for two-thirds of the decline in Korean labor share of income between 1990 and 2018. @NBERpubs
articles.author: Sangmin Aum and Yongseok Shin

articles.title: .@RobertJShiller and @WGoetzmann find that subjective assessments consistently overestimate the risk of stock market collapse, and that emotional response to non-fundamental shocks explains at least some of the variation.
articles.author: William Goetzmann, Dasol Kim and Robert Shiller

articles.title: Strong growth since 1994 leaves US real median hourly wages up 36% since 1980. @BenGlasner @LettieriDC @ModeledBehavior
articles.author: Adam Ozimek, John Lettieri and Benjamin Glasner

articles.title: A new @MarcGoldwein @BudgetHawks analysis finds that Trump approved $2.9T of net non-Covid related borrowing, plus $1.9T for the Tax Cuts and Jobs Act. So far Biden has approved $2.2T of net borrowing, plus $2.1T for the American Rescue Plan.
articles.author: CRFB Staff
articles.pub: Committee for a Responsible Federal Budget

articles.title: A @stlouisfed analysis finds that since 2021 an increase in labor issues has spurred an additional $55B in investment, driven by firms that heavily employ routine manual tasks like assembly and warehouse packaging. @AakashKalyani @serdarozkanEN
articles.author: Mick Dueholm, Aakash Kalyani and Serdar Ozkan
articles.pub: Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis

articles.title: From 2008-2023 outstanding credit to the Chinese non-financial sector rose 148pp to over 283% of GDP. Over that same period, the corresponding measure for the US rose 15pp to 256% of GDP. @DerekScissors1
articles.author: Derek Scissors

articles.title: Census Bureau data shows that the foreign-born population of the US rose from 45mm in January 2021 to 51.6mm in March 2024, representing 15.6% of the population, surpassing the 1890 record of 14.8%.
articles.author: Steven Camarota

articles.title: US nonfarm business sector real output per hour grew at a 1.5% annual rate from Q4 2019 to Q1 2024, well under the 2.1% average from 1947-2024. @JimPethokoukis recommends that politicians “substantially step up investments in human capital.”
articles.author: James Pethokoukis

articles.title: An analysis from @SFFed finds that capital returns for publicly traded firms have fallen since 1950 while returns for privately held companies have increased. @ASollaci @EconTraina @CarterDavisFin
articles.author: Anton Bobrov, Carter Davis, Alexandre Sollac & James Traina
articles.pub: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco

articles.title: Since June 2022 the marginal buyer of Treasuries has shifted to domestic households, foreign hedge fund buyers, and insurance companies. @FederalReserve
articles.author: Lucy Cordes and Erin Ferris

articles.title: The pandemic-era rise in quit rates was strongest among female, nonwhite, non-college-educated workers aged < 35, many of whom left the workforce, facilitated by increases in wealth from stimulus checks. @philadelphiafed
articles.author: Ryan Michaels

articles.title: An analysis of war since 1870 finds that nearby non-belligerent countries suffered an 8% reduction in output six years after the onset of war, while distant non-belligerents saw a 4% rise in output, heavily influenced by increased military spending.
articles.author: Jonathan Federle, Andre Meier, Gernot Muller, et al.

articles.title: Updated @USCBO projections forecast the 2024 federal budget deficit will be almost $2T or 7.0% of US GDP, up from 6.2% in 2023.
articles.author: CBO Staff

articles.title: According to an IMF analysis, almost 1/3 of global investment flows since the pandemic have been into the US, up from 18% pre-pandemic.
articles.author: Enda Curran and Saleha Mohsin

articles.title: Responding to Maurice Obstfeld, @michaelxpettis argues that a capital inflow tax won’t raise US interest rates because capital inflows are “more likely to cause domestic savings to decline than to cause domestic investment to rise.”
articles.author: Michael Pettis

articles.title: The return of lower-paid workers who left the labor force during Covid as well as other workers’ willingness to accept lower pay for work-from-home jobs left average real wages in Q1 ‘24 down 3.5 to 4.4 ppts relative to 2006-19 trends.
articles.author: Steven Davis

articles.title: Maurice Obstfeld argues that attempting to address America's chronic trade deficits via a levy on foreign purchases of US assets would lead to higher interest rates and risk undermining the liquidity of US financial markets.
articles.author: Maurice Obstfeld

articles.title: Between 1980-2016, automation targeted tasks where workers earned an average wage that was 35% higher than their next best job opportunity. Automation accounted for 52% of the increase in between-group US inequality over the period. @DAcemogluMIT
articles.author: Daron Acemoglu and Pascual Restrepo

articles.title: Interest-bearing debt of China's ~12,000 local government financing vehicles (LGFVs) has grown from 13% of GDP in 2014 to 48% in 2023. @JonathonPSine finds their average borrowing cost of 5% far outpaces their 1% return on assets.
articles.author: Jonathan Sine

articles.title: Changes in sell-side analysts’ 3-5 year profit expectations of individual S&P 500 firms predict aggregate boom-bust cycles for both corporate investment and stock valuations.
articles.author: Pedro Bordalo,Nicola Gennaioli, Rafael La Porta, et al.

articles.title: The 2021 Child Tax Credit (CTC) expansion increased government benefits to low-income families but reduced employment only among mothers with relatively low levels of education. @dwschanz @MichaelRStrain
articles.author: Diane Whitmore Schanzenbach and Michael Strain

articles.title: Mark Warshawsky simulates the performance of government pensions based on the past ten-year history of bond and stock returns. At the 25th percentile of historical returns, few plans are fully funded, and many are below 50% funding. @AEIecon
articles.author: Mark Warshawsky

articles.title: An analysis of 17 Chinese industries from 2006-21 finds that a 1% increase in industrial robots per 10,000 workers (robot density) raises nominal labor productivity by 0.018%, with evidence of diminishing returns.
articles.author: Yantong Zhao, Rusmawati Said, Normaz Wana Ismail, et al.

articles.title: Between 1970 and 2022 median middle-income US households saw real income growth of 60%. Low-income households saw a 55% increase, excluding non-cash government transfers. @pewresearch
articles.author: Rakesh Kochhar

articles.title: Data from 39 countries show that countries with low bankruptcy efficiency experience a 3 ppt decline in GDP following a 5-year business credit boom, while in high-efficiency countries GDP barely declines.
articles.author: Martin Kornejew, Chen Lian, Yueran Ma, Pablo Ottonello and Diego Perez

articles.title: New technologies allow electric utilities to monitor transmission capacity in real-time, a system known as dynamic line rating. A pilot project in Minnesota showed this technology could increase existing transmission line capacity by about 40%.
articles.author: Scott Patterson

articles.title: The density of four-year colleges in 1980 is the most important single predictor of a region’s employment growth in high-skilled cognitive analytical “new job” categories between 1980 and 2010. @giov_peri @Gueyon_Kim
articles.author: Gueyon Kim, Cassandra Merritt, and Giovanni Peri

articles.title: Americans earned ~ $3.7T in annualized investment income in Q1 - up $770B from 2020. In the last quarter of 2023, wealth held in stocks, real estate, and other assets such as pensions reached the highest level ever recorded.
articles.author: David Uberti

articles.title: US jobs programs, transfers, and macroeconomic upturns tend to reduce property crime but not serious violent crime, so these programs have little impact on the total social costs of crime, which are driven by serious violence.
articles.author: Jens Ludwig and Kevin Schnepel

articles.title: US firms that won a low-skill H2B work visa lottery in 2021-22 and hired temporary foreign workers saw higher revenue and investment with no crowding out of temporary domestic workers. @m_clem @ethanglewis
articles.author: Michael Clemens and Ethan Lewis

articles.title: Last year the top 10 equities made up 27% of market capitalization but 69% of total economic profits; historically, increasing concentration has been associated with above-average S&P 500 returns. @mjmauboussin
articles.author: Michael Mauboussin and Dan Callahan

articles.title: Between 2019 and 2023 China's manufacturing surplus rose by 1pp of global GDP. @Brad_Setser notes "It is impossible for a global economy characterized by a large U.S. deficit on one side and a large Chinese surplus on the other to truly fragment."
articles.author: Brad Setser

articles.title: Randall Morck's research finds that the social return to R&D investment exceeds the private return by 4X and the social benefits of tech sector bubbles likely exceed the social costs of the subsequent crashes. Hat tip to @JimPethokoukis
articles.author: Randall Morck

articles.title: Evidence from German investors finds that educating investors about negative stock market returns during inflation causes them to reduce their return expectations and purchases of equities.
articles.author: Philip Schnorpfeil, Michael Weber, and Andreas Hackethal

articles.title: While the share of US residents who had moved in the past year fell to an all-time low of 7.8% in 2023, Americans ages 30-39 are still relatively mobile: in 2022, 49% were not born in the state where they currently live. @foxjust
articles.author: Justin Fox

articles.title: US AI-related capex was ~ $100B in 2023, about 0.4% of GDP. Bridgewater forecasts an investment arms race: “We see the makings of a surge in spending that could be large enough to shape the trajectory of the business cycle from here.”
articles.author: Greg Jensen and Josh Moriarty

articles.title: .@stlouisfed analysis finds Gen Xers had a higher median net worth at age 30 than Baby Boomers or Millennials, largely due to lower debt levels.
articles.author: Victoria Gregory and Kevin Bloodworth II
articles.pub: Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis

articles.title: European data from 2021 show that in contrast to the 2011 data, self-reported unhappiness and depression are highest among teens and decline sharply with age. @AlexanderBryson @D_Blanchflower
articles.author: David Blanchflower, Alex Bryson, Anthony Lepinteur, et al.

articles.title: Emerging economies are introducing import restrictions on Chinese goods, while accelerating a push for free trade elsewhere, as Chinese industrial production surges to 30% above January 2019 levels. @TheEconomist
articles.author: Economist Staff

articles.title: Under the CBO’s “Rising Debt” scenario, real GDP per person in 2054 would be almost $15,000 lower than the “Stable Debt” scenario, due to higher debt levels crowding out private investment and slowing growth. @MarcGoldwein
articles.author: CRFB Staff
articles.pub: Committee for a Responsible Federal Budget

articles.title: The rise in the work-from-home share accounts for more than half of the rise in overall interstate migration since 2019, and states with greater WFH opportunities experienced more out-migration.
articles.author: Alexander Bick, Adam Blandin, Karel Mertens, et al.
articles.pub: Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis Working Papers

articles.title: Using CPS data from 1991-2013, Boskin, Elnahal, and Zhang find that redistribution policy attenuates market income shifts most strongly at the middle-income quintile, consistent with the median voter theory.
articles.author: Michael Boskin, Kareem Elnahal, and Adam Zhang

articles.title: A new @USCBO analysis argues rising government debt may drive significant crowding out going forward: "In CBO’s assessment, every additional dollar of deficit-financed spending reduces private investment by 33 cents."
articles.author: Daniel Crown and Aaron Betz

articles.title: Chinese scientific research shows a strong "home bias" where researchers disproportionately cite work from their own country. An adjusted citation ranking places China fourth globally, behind the US, the UK, and Germany. @ClaudiaSteinwen @pierre_azoulay
articles.author: Shumin Qiu, Claudia Steinwender and Pierre Azoulay

articles.title: .@DAcemogluMIT and @baselinescene cite 19th-century textile automation to argue that AI may raise productivity but fail to increase employment and wages as it displaces workers and degrades the quality of remaining jobs.
articles.author: Daron Acemoglu and Simon Johnson

articles.title: Labor’s share of US GDP fell from 64% in 1950 to 59% in 2022, consistent with declines in other countries since 1980. Increasing deployment of capital-augmenting technology appears central to this trend.
articles.author: Loukas Karabarbounis
