articles.title: .@ernietedeschi argues that higher growth, higher debt levels, and greater political risk have most likely driven long-run R* higher than its pre-pandemic levels and higher than the 60bps the median FOMC participant currently projects.
articles.author: Ernie Tedeschi

articles.title: The private sector union weekly earnings premium reflects a 10% higher hourly wage and 5% more working hours, reflecting trade unions’ role in “ensuring workers receive the paid hours they desire.” @D_Blanchflower @AlexanderBryson
articles.author: David Blanchflower and Alex Bryson

articles.title: .@benbernanke and @ojblanchard1 find that pandemic-era inflation across ten countries was initially driven by transient price shocks, but as these effects receded, tight labor markets and wage inflation became more significant and persistent factors.
articles.author: Ben Bernanke and Olivier Blanchard

articles.title: A measure of individualism accounts for about 1/3 of the cross-country variation in work-from-home rates, with WFH most prevalent in the Anglosphere and least in Asia. @PabloZarate98 @I_Am_NickBloom @Jose_MariaRD @cevatgirayaksoy
articles.author: Pablo Zarate, Mathias Dolls, Steven Davis, et al.

articles.title: Evidence from CBO projections suggests the TCJA corporate tax cut increased business investment, driving economic growth. @kpomerleau and @DonFSchneider present two revenue-neutral options to finance a permanent extension of the TCJA .
articles.author: Kyle Pomerleau and Donald Schneider

articles.title: Robert Lighthizer argues that a universal tariff would better manage America's chronic deficits than either capital controls or Warren Buffett’s proposed import certificates. “Every country should be exporting in order to import.” @greg_ip
articles.author: Greg Ip

articles.title: The Department of Energy estimates that efficiency improvements such as upgraded transmission lines and better management of America's electrical grid could yield 100GW or 13% of current peak power demand.
articles.author: Economist Staff

articles.title: Large technology firms are driving investment in clean energy sources through “power-purchase agreements.” Adding 1GW of wind or solar costs ~ $1B. @IEA forecast that data center electrical demand will double between 2022-26.
articles.author: Economist Staff

articles.title: American manufacturing productivity has been flat as measured by output per hour worked since 2007; one driver has been a flatlining in American manufacturing capital intensity. @JosephPolitano
articles.author: Joey Politano

articles.title: Trade liberalization with China reduced employment and earnings of US workers initially in manufacturing by up to 22% over seven years. Workers not initially in manufacturing saw relative earnings gains due to access to lower-cost inputs. @tellotri
articles.author: Justin Pierce, Peter Schott, and Cristina Tello-Trillo

articles.title: Since 1973 median pay has risen 50% while average productivity rose 111%; @swinshi finds the divergence has been driven by “fanning out” in workers' productivity and slowing in the growth of men's wages as women entered the labor force.
articles.author: Scott Winship

articles.title: Globally fertility declines are outpacing the UN’s 2017 projections by 10-20%. Had the US total fertility rate remained at its 2007 level of 2.1 vs. dropping to 1.6 last year, an additional 10.6mm babies would have been born. @greg_ip
articles.author: Greg Ip and Janet Adamy

articles.title: Erik Nielsen, economics adviser at Italian bank UniCredit, says investment in the US has increased more than 8% since the end of 2019 and is still growing strongly at the start of 2024, while the Eurozone remained 4% below pre-Covid levels.
articles.author: Martin Arnold, Sam Fleming and Claire Jones

articles.title: The monthly average of US new business formation is 80% higher than during the decade before COVID, vs. 20% in Europe . Between 2020-23, US startups created more than 100% of net new jobs.
articles.author: Economist Staff

articles.title: Jesper Rangvid argues that monetarist theory would have predicted deflation from the recent contraction in the M2 money supply. Continuing inflation leaves him “skeptical, to say the least” about the theory.
articles.author: Jesper Rangvid

articles.title: An analysis of @BankofAmerica internal data indicates that the mean monthly customer payment for home and auto insurance is up 25% since 2019, and represented around 4.5% of household income in the 12 months to March 2024.
articles.author: David Michael Tinsley, Joe Wadford, et al.

articles.title: Grid-scale batteries can supply up to 20% of California’s electrical demand, equivalent to the output of 7 large nuclear reactors. US battery storage has grown 10X in the past three years to 16,000 megawatts and is expected to double again this year.
articles.author: Brad Plumer and Nadja Popovich

articles.title: .@toniwhited and @ivan_t_ivanov find that state tax cuts “make capital more productive and spur borrowing” by small private firms, as investment opportunities and lower credit spreads outweigh the reduced tax subsidy to borrowing.
articles.author: Ivan Ivanov, Luke Pettit, and Toni Whited

articles.title: Immigrants accounted for 15% of the 2020 US population, but 27% of new businesses formed in 2021-23. Differences in education and fields of study only partly explain immigrant firms’ high rates of patenting and innovation. @william_r_kerr @schodavadia
articles.author: Saheel Chodavadia, Sari Pekkala Kerr, William Kerr et al.

articles.title: .@VCurdia estimates that the contraction in credit in the aftermath of SVB’s failure may have added 0.4pp to unemployment through the end of this year and pushed down inflation slightly.
articles.author: Vasco Cúrdia
articles.pub: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco

articles.title: A @BudgetHawks analysis notes the Social Security "trust fund" will deplete its reserves by 2033, triggering a 21% benefit cut under current law. A 27% (i.e. 3.3pp) payroll tax increase could close the funding gap.
articles.author: CRFB Staff
articles.pub: Committee for a Responsible Federal Budget

articles.title: The Medicare Trustees report that the Medicare “trust fund” will be insolvent in 2036 with a 75-year shortfall of up to 1.2% of payroll. @MarcGoldwein @BudgetHawks
articles.author: CRFB Staff
articles.pub: Committee for a Responsible Federal Budget

articles.title: Torsten Sløk @apolloglobal argues the US economy’s continued strength reflects both reduced current interest rate sensitivity of household and corporate borrowing and multiple “demand tailwinds.”
articles.author: Torsten Sløk

articles.title: US financial assets benefit from a "Safe Harbor Premium" due to perceived lower risk. @ernietedeschi argues that US “shadow risk” increased 20-25 bps between 2016-20.
articles.author: Ernie Tedeschi

articles.title: .@Brad_Setser suggests the 2004 US-China “balance of financial terror” has evolved: “In a crisis over Taiwan, China would likely lose access to its reserves–and the G7 countries would see their supply of new cell phones disappear.”
articles.author: Brad Setser

articles.title: .@delong argues that the US is on track for a debt crisis unless the overall deficit is cut in half to 3% of GDP, running deficits only to service existing debt.
articles.author: Brad DeLong

articles.title: In 2018 the 400 richest Americans had a lower effective tax rate than working-class Americans. @gabriel_zucman argues for a global minimum tax, ensuring that the super-rich tax pay “a small portion — say, 2 percent — of their wealth each year.”
articles.author: Gabriel Zucman

articles.title: A @NewYorkFed note argues that overall market concentration across 169 manufacturing industries has remained flat from 1992 to 2012 as foreign competition has put less competitive US firms out of business, increasing domestic production concentration.
articles.author: Mary Amiti and Sebastian Heise
articles.pub: Federal Reserve Bank of New York

articles.title: The top-earning men in Finland and Norway score 1 standard deviation higher in cognitive ability than median earners. The ability curve is steepest at the top of the earnings range. @MarkoTervio @olerogeberg
articles.author: Bernt Bratsberg, Ole Rogeberg and Marko Terviö

articles.title: Between 2000 and 2019 a @giov_peri @ale_caiumi analysis finds immigration had a positive 1.7-2.6% impact on US non-college workers' wages, as high-skilled immigrant labor complemented low-skilled native labor.
articles.author: Alessandro Caiumi and Giovanni Peri

articles.title: The rich world is in the midst of an unprecedented migration boom, with foreign-born populations growing at 4%/year, double the 2010s rate. Lower-skilled immigrants are driving down GDP per capita in Australia, Britain, and Canada.
articles.author: Economist Staff

articles.title: A record 3.8% of @BankofAmerica customers received income from gig platforms in March, led by ridesharing, while delivery remains below late 2021 levels. This mirrors the pivot in consumer spending towards out-of-home services vs in-home goods.
articles.author: Joe Wadford and David Tinsley

articles.title: Torsten Sløk @apolloglobal notes the American economy is largely private: 80% of Americans work for firms outside of the S&P 500 and 87% of US firms with revenue greater than $100 million are private.
articles.author: Torsten Sløk

articles.title: Suicide rates for Americans in their 20s and 30s are now higher than among those in their 40s or 50s, reversing “the long-accepted statistic that more middle-aged Americans take their own lives than younger adults.” @jean_twenge
articles.author: Jean Twenge

articles.title: After adjusting for hidden expenditures, labor costs, and purchasing power parity, @MEaglen @AEI estimates that Chinese military spending in 2022 was $710.6B, nearly equal to the US's $742.2B.
articles.author: Mackenzie Eaglen

articles.title: .@MarcGoldwein @BudgetHawks estimates the cost of the Biden Administration's proposed student debt cancellations over the next decade at $0.9-1.4T, more than total Federal spending on higher education from 1962-2019.
articles.author: CRFB Staff
articles.pub: Committee for a Responsible Federal Budget

articles.title: The IMF argues that a well-designed tax on income from capital, at a lower rate than labor income, would be less distortive and more equitable than a wealth tax if countries seek to raise more revenue from the rich.
articles.author: Shafik Hebous, Alexander Klemm, Geerten Michielse and Carolina Osorio-Buitron

articles.title: In the 12 months ending March 2024, China ran a goods trade surplus of $844B, however only $411B has been repatriated. Two of the three drivers of this form of capital flight are unlikely to be reversed.  @martin_lynge
articles.author: Martin Lynge Rasmussen

articles.title: A @GoldmanSachs analysis argues US power demand will grow at 2.4%/year through 2030 versus 0% over the last decade. They forecast data centers will be 8% of power demand by 2030 from 3% currently.
articles.author: Carly Davenport, Brian Singer, Et al.

articles.title: .@jburnmurdoch notes that as of 2018 “In terms of the fiscal impact, immigrants pay more in than they get out in the US, UK, Australia and Ireland, but are net recipients in Belgium, France, Sweden and the Netherlands.”
articles.author: John Burn-Murdoch

articles.title: The highest-income 20% of metro areas, which drove roughly ⅔ of US job creation between 2011-21, have failed to keep up with rapid employment growth in the rest of the country and represented less than 50% of new jobs in 2023. @AugustBenzow
articles.author: August Benzow

articles.title: .@LaraLoewenstein and @hmontag3 find that by focusing on new tenants who represent only 13-25% of rentals, alternative rent inflation indices (e.g. Zillow, CoreLogic) overstate average rental inflation when rents are rising.
articles.author: Brian Adams, Lara Loewenstein, Hugh Montag and Randal Verbrugge

articles.title: The CEO of Norway's $1.6T oil fund notes that US companies have outpaced their European rivals in innovation and technology; “There’s a mindset issue in terms of acceptance of mistakes and risks…the Americans just work harder.”
articles.author: Richard Milne and Robin Wigglesworth

articles.title: Bor, @Cutler_econ, Glaeser, and @lj_ristovska find a strong negative correlation between the % of college graduates in an area and all-cause mortality, even after controlling for individual education.
articles.author: Jacob Bor, David Cutler, Edward Glaeser and Ljubica Ristovska

articles.title: Phil Gramm and Mike Solon defend the TCJA corporate tax cuts by highlighting that customers, employees, and investors ultimately pay corporate taxes.
articles.author: Phil Gramm and Mike Solon

articles.title: .@mjmauboussin and Dan Callahan argue that the ability of P/E and EV/EBITDA multiples to capture the underlying economics of a business has degraded as intangible investments in R&D and customer acquisition have become increasingly important.
articles.author: Michael Mauboussin and Dan Callahan

articles.title: Idaho’s Return to Work Bonus of up to $1,500 was associated with a 0.4pp decrease in the unemployment rate and a 2.4% increase in nonfarm payroll employment. @MichaelRStrain
articles.author: Michael Strain and Duncan Hobbs

articles.title: Bridgewater argues that investment of $3-5T annually between 2020-50, or 4% of global GDP, would be required to deploy mature emissions-reduction technologies, but this would yield only 40-50% of reductions required to achieve net zero goals.
articles.author: Daniel Hochman, Jeremy Ng, Alexis Teh, and JP Lucaci

articles.title: According to a Stanford analysis, there was $67.2B in private AI investment in the US last year, 8.7x the level in China.  Since 2013 there has been a cumulative $335.2B investment in AI by the US relative to $103.7B for China.
articles.author: Raymond Perrault, Jack Clark, et al.

articles.title: Norwegian tax records show that individuals born into the top 21% of the distribution or the bottom 42% tend to stay in these groups for life. Inter-generational mobility is primarily driven by movement within the middle of the distribution. @RichardAudoly
articles.author: Richard Audoly, Rory McGee, et al.
articles.pub: Federal Reserve Bank of New York

articles.title: The Bank of Korea is forecasting South Korean GDP growth of 2.1%/yr this decade, down from 6.4% from 1970-2022, as “Chinese companies that were once customers or suppliers have become rivals.” @FT
articles.author: Christian Davies

articles.title: Since 2010 the largest 500 Japanese stocks have increased their EPS by 5.8x, twice the rate as their US peers.  A @verdadcap analysis argues this outperformance was driven by the success of Corporate Japan's deleveraging.
articles.author: Nick Schmitz

articles.title: .@FedGuy12 notes the tech bubble played out with 10-year Treasury yields at 6.5%, and argues that higher rates won’t necessarily cause a selloff in US equity markets, given strong nominal growth and chronic fiscal deficits.
articles.author: Joseph Wang

articles.title: Torsten Sløk @apolloglobal notes that 87% of US firms with revenue greater than $100 million are private.
articles.author: Torsten Sløk

articles.title: .@tylercowen argues the @DAcemogluMIT estimate of AI’s productivity impact fails to account for new tasks that labor has not yet performed, gains from better science, or the departure of the least productive firms.
articles.author: Tyler Cowen

articles.title: .@DAcemogluMIT estimates that AI will have a modest 0.55% impact on total factor productivity over ten years, and may not have much effect on inequality.
articles.author: Daron Acemoglu

articles.title: New high-tech establishments were founded at a disproportionately high rate after 2019; the uptick in this sort of firm could imply future productivity gains. @UpdatedPriors @JHaltiwanger_UM
articles.author: Ryan Decker and John Haltiwanger

articles.title: Polling suggests Biden has increased his advantage over Trump with college-educated white voters from 9ppt in 2016 to 18ppt in 2024 while increasing his disadvantage among non-college white voters from 26ppt to 27ppt. @NateMoore1287
articles.author: Nate Moore

articles.title: US fatalities per mile driven are double that of other advanced economies, and the US road mortality rate is up 25% over the last decade; @jburnmurdoch suggests American attitudes toward alcohol and cell phone use are the driver.
articles.author: John Burn-Murdoch

articles.title: Between 2010 and 2020 over half of US counties, representing 25% of the population, saw their populations decline.  Rural America’s population decline is set to accelerate this decade as the birth rate declines and immigrants flow to major cities.
articles.author: Economist Staff

articles.title: Data on 38mm Italian and German workers in 3.7mm firms show that a large supply of older workers amidst slow growth has limited career progression for younger workers and led to an increasing wage gap between older and younger workers. @MatteoParadisi
articles.author: Nicola Bianchi and Matteo Paradisi

articles.title: The number of full-time "Gen Z" workers (born 1997-2012) in the US workforce will soon overtake the number of Baby boomers (1946-64.) Hourly wage growth among 16-24-year-olds is running at 13%,  the fastest “young person premium” on record.
articles.author: Economist Staff

articles.title: In advanced economies, private capital formation remains 40% below its pre-crisis trend, with slower economic growth explaining about half the decline.
articles.author: IMF Staff

articles.title: Based on a Dept. of Commerce survey of 1.2mm US businesses, only 6.6% of firms, representing 12% of employees, plan to use AI by Fall 2024. In the Information sector, 21.5% of firms with 25% of employees plan to use AI. @JHaltiwanger_UM
articles.author: Kathryn Bonney, Cory Breaux, Cathy Buffington, Emin Dinlersoz, Lucia Foster, Nathan Goldschlag, John Haltiwanger, Zachary Kroff and Keith Savage

articles.title: Geolocation data from cell phones shows that less than 5% of Americans attend weekly religious services, versus 22% who report they do. @Devin_G_Pope
articles.author: Devin Pope

articles.title: In a sample of ~140k homeless people, Meyer et al found that 40-50% aged 25-59 had earnings of $12-14k in 2018 dollars and 80%+ had benefits from SSI and related programs. For those with low permanent income, even modest events trigger homelessness.
articles.author: Bruce Meyer, Angela Wyse, Gillian Meyer, Alexa Grunwaldt and Derek Wu

articles.title: US firms hit by controls restricting exports to China saw negative stock returns that wiped out $130 billion in market capitalization and drops in bank lending, profitability, and employment. @mcrosign @linahan56358916 @andrefjsilva
articles.author: Matteo Crosignani, Lina Han, Marco Macchiavelli and André Silva
articles.pub: Federal Reserve Bank of New York

articles.title: Hospitalizations for intentional self-harm for American girls 10-14 increased 518% between 2007-2009 and 2021 and 91% for the 15-19 cohort. These increases imply a high degree of distress for young American women. @ZachMRausch
articles.author: Zach Rausch

articles.title: Data from public universities in Texas show students whose test scores marginally qualify for their target 4-year state college have higher educational attainment and eventually earn 10% more than counterparts whose scores marginally fail to qualify.
articles.author: Jack Mountjoy

articles.title: Investor overreaction to news events may reduce market volatility: “the sum total of different individual overreactions to multiple simultaneous shocks can dampen rather than amplify their combined market impact.” @Francesco_Bia @LudvigsonSydney
articles.author: Francesco Bianchi, Sydney Ludvigson and Sai Ma

articles.title: Existing power lines use steel cores surrounded by aluminum strands. Replacing these with carbon fiber-based cores, a process known as "advanced reconductoring," could double the capacity of current lines.
articles.author: Brad Plumer

articles.title: Felonious assaults on New York City's subways are up significantly since the pandemic. @foxjust suggests this “may have something to do with local criminal justice practices,” including the ending of cash bail.
articles.author: Justin Fox

articles.title: Jaremski and Schuster document that before the establishment of the FDIC, deposits fled to the safety of local insured postal banks, illustrating that lack of universal deposit coverage amplifies run risk. @ssprickschuster
articles.author: Matthew Jaremski and Steven Sprick Schuster

articles.title: A cross-country statistical study provides robust evidence that expropriation risk such as corruption negatively impacts innovation by reducing productivity-enhancing activities including R&D. @NBERpubs
articles.author: Jose-Miguel Benavente, Claudio Bravo-Ortega, Pablo Egaña-delSol and Bronwyn Hall

articles.title: Democratic losses with younger voters are being offset by a compositional shift in older voters as Baby Boomers replace more conservative cohorts aged 65+. @Nate_Cohn
articles.author: Nate Cohn

articles.title: Bank balance sheet lending has declined from 60% of total private lending in 1970 to 35% in 2023. Private credit is increasingly intermediated through arms-length transactions such as securitization. @NBERpubs
articles.author: Greg Buchak, Gregor Matvos, Tomasz Piskorski and Amit Seru

articles.title: By her mid-thirties, the most common number of children a US or UK woman has is 0. @jburnmurdoch argues there is no evidence that fiscal transfers lift fertility in advanced economies.
articles.author: John Burn-Murdoch

articles.title: The rise in U.S. patents is increasingly driven by non-creative or derivative patents, which have historically had little positive effect on productivity growth.
articles.author: Aakash Kalyani
articles.pub: Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis

articles.title: Though real rents remain at a record high, a 50-year peak in apartment completions is exerting downward pressure and has sharply reduced rental inflation. @JosephPolitano
articles.author: Joseph Politano

articles.title: Deposits - especially uninsured deposits that create run risk - have risen sharply, while banks with the most rapid growth in deposits have shifted assets away from lending towards longer-term MBS and Treasuries. @lauramnicolae
articles.author: Samuel Hanson, Victoria Ivanshina, Laura Nicolae, Jeremy Stein, Adi Sunderam and Daniel Tarullo

articles.title: Last year global venture capital funds raised one-third of 2021’s record total of $555B. Funds are increasingly using their dry powder to support existing investments as opposed to making new investments.
articles.author: George Hammond

articles.title: Michael Cembalest @jpmorgan notes McKinsey, BCG, and S&P Global all expect American electric power demand to grow 15-30% annually until 2030, with previously unanticipated power demand from AI a major driver.
articles.author: Michael Cembalest

articles.title: New survey research shows that people dislike inflation because  they believe that it is associated with declining real wages and falling living standards. @S_Stantcheva building on @RobertJShiller
articles.author: Stefanie Stantcheva

articles.title: Goldman Sachs forecasts an AI-driven investment cycle with $250B of annual hardware investment, equivalent to 9% of US business investment or roughly 1% of US GDP, through 2025.  They expect this capex cycle to peak at 2-2.5% of US GDP by 2032.
articles.author: Joseph Briggs and Devesh Kodnani

articles.title: Properly trained talent is the limiting constraint to growth; immigrants currently make up 20% of the domestic American workforce in strategic industries. @cojobrien @ModeledBehavior
articles.author: Connor O’Brien and Adam Ozimek

articles.title: .@krogoff @YaredPierre @hafrouzi argue that strengthened central bank independence and more credible public debt policies are required to offset inflationary pressures from trade-distorting geopolitical tensions and rising government deficits.
articles.author: Hassan Afrouzi, Marina Halac, Kenneth Rogoff and Pierre Yared

articles.title: Since the 1960s labor market fluctuations have become more uniform across states, helping to make interstate migration less important in cyclical adjustments.  @A_Fieldhouse @D_Munro_Econ @ChristofferKoch
articles.author: Andrew Fieldhouse, David Munro and Christoffer Koch

articles.title: .@AswathDamodaran estimates the current equity risk premium for the S&P 500 at 4.23%, the lowest level since 2008; the expected return on stocks is 8.6%.
articles.author: Aswath Damodaran

articles.title: Since the end of 2019, US GDP has grown by 8.2%, and immigration drove at least 1.6pp of that growth.  The overall US workforce grew by 2mm, with immigration offsetting a 1.2mm decline in the native-born workforce. @ernietedeschi
articles.author: Ernie Tedeschi

articles.title: After examining all US and Canadian private and public firms with >$10mm in sales, @verdadcap concludes that the relationship between a firm's market share and return on assets/equity is "virtually non-existent."
articles.author: Daniel Rasmussen

articles.title: 28% of American public school students missed at least 10% of the school year in 2022 up from 15% before the pandemic.  The change was most pronounced in low-achieving school districts.  @natmalkus @AEIecon
articles.author: Nat Malkus

articles.title: Many multinational companies are exporting less from China, sending fewer products there, and seeing declines in Chinese domestic revenues. Multinationals once accounted for more than half of exports out of China, but now make up less than 1/3. @WSJ
articles.author: Newley Purnell and Clarence Leong

articles.title: .@akorinek et al discuss two scenarios where AI leads to a wage collapse: if AI eventually automates all human tasks, or if capital investment fails to support automation that could potentially augment human productivity.
articles.author: Anton Korinek and Donghyun Suh

articles.title: Kiley and Miskin argue that curbing threats to financial stability is essential to price and economic stability, but that pursuit of additional objectives like climate change and inequality is not feasible for monetary policy.
articles.author: Michael Kiley and Frederic Mishkin

articles.title: .@andy_garin et al. show that men born before WWII in counties chosen for large wartime plant construction earned 2.5% more per year in adulthood relative to those born in comparable regions, with benefits mainly to children born to low-income parents.
articles.author: Andrew Garin and Jonathan Rothbaum

articles.title: The pandemic-era excess retirement surge was concentrated among non-college workers over age 55. Relative to the pre-pandemic trend about 2mm workers are missing. @Petrosky_Nadeau
articles.author: Brandon Miskanic, Nicolas Petrosky-Nadeau and Cindy Zhao
articles.pub: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco

articles.title: Increases in firm-level productivity disproportionately raise wages for top earners, and since 1980 have driven 40% of the rise in within-firm inequality as measured by the CEO/median pay ratio. @I_Am_NickBloom @WallskogMelanie @tellotri
articles.author: Melanie Wallskog, Nicholas Bloom, Scott Ohlmacher and Cristina Tello-Trillo

articles.title: There is early evidence that AI’s impact on labor markets is following the path of other forms of automation. @erikbryn @AdamThierer @DAcemogluMIT
articles.author: Erik Brynjolfsson, Adam Thierer, and Daron Acemoglu

articles.title: Illegal immigration may not be at a record high. @foxjust argues statistics are being skewed by a change in behavior as illegals seek out government authorities to take advantage of the asylum system.
articles.author: Justin Fox

articles.title: For every 1pp mortgage rate increase above an American homeowner's current rate, the probability of a sale decreases 18%. This “lock-in” effect drove a 5.7% increase in home prices between Q2 ‘22 and Q4 ‘24.
articles.author: Ross Batzer, Jonah Coste, William Doerner and Michael Seiler

articles.title: A paper from @AEIecon Mark Warshawsky, John Mantus and Gaobo Pang uses an economic model rather than extrapolating historical trends and projects that debt-to-GDP will be 132% in 2032, compared to CBO’s 2023 projection of 115%.
articles.author: Mark Warshawsky, John Mantus and Gaobo Pang

articles.title: Large labor markets are more liquid and improve matching. A 1% increase in market size results in an increase in the 1-year re-employment probability following a firm closure by .14 and .023  for high school and college graduates respectively. @MoisesYi3
articles.author: Enrico Moretti and Moises Yi

articles.title: Slower economic growth, not rising inequality, is the primary factor behind declining absolute mobility, according to Scott Winship's analysis of Chetty's data.
articles.author: Scott Winship

articles.title: .@FedGuy12 argues that the recent surge of at least 3.3mm immigrants is putting upward pressure on housing costs which make up 30% of the CPI, creating a challenge to the 2% target.
articles.author: Joseph Wang

articles.title: The more time students spent in pandemic-related remote learning, the more they underperformed.  The mean drop in math scores for 3rd through 8th grades in 2022 for mostly in-person was .35 years behind and for mostly remote .57 years behind. @NYTimes
articles.author: Sarah Mervosh, Claire Cain Miller, and Francesca Paris

articles.title: A recent @NBERpubs paper confirms that global poverty and inequality declined dramatically between 1980 and 2019 and that “within-country inequality is falling on average, and has been largely constant since the 1990s” @XSalaimartin
articles.author: Maxim Pinkovskiy, Xavier Sala-i-Martin, Kasey Chatterji-Len, and William Nober

articles.title: .@FedGuy12 endorses a proposed adjustment to the Basel III leverage calculations that would increase demand for Treasuries from US banks as future issuances surge.
articles.author: Joseph Wang

articles.title: Calomiris and Jaremski review ten famous financial crises from ancient Rome through the present and find four common factors: politically motivated subsidies, changes in investor preferences towards risk, unsophisticated investors, and fraud.
articles.author: Charles Calomiris and Matthew Jaremski

articles.title: As energy prices have normalized Germany's trade surplus is back, reaching a nominal record, though in real terms German exports continue to contract. @GeneralTheorist
articles.author: Chris Marsh

articles.title: Industries with the largest increase in markups between 1980 and 2016 saw smaller declines in dynamism. Larger increases in markups are not associated with larger declines in dynamism at the industry level.
articles.author: Brian Albrecht and Ryan Decker

articles.title: Jesper Rangvid shows that high stock market returns over the last 40 years have been driven by falling interest rates.  With rates now rising, he expects real returns of only around 2% annually over the next decade.
articles.author: Jesper Rangvid

articles.title: A @GoldmanSachs analysis notes while market concentration is high, valuations are lower than in recent bubble periods and US superstar firms are much more profitable and have stronger balance sheets than they did during the tech bubble.
articles.author: Peter Oppenheimer, Guillaume Jaisson, Sharon Bell, Marcus von Scheele and Lilia Peytavin

articles.title: China's post-pandemic manufacturing goods surplus is now  2% of global GDP and shows no sign of shrinking, driving countries to seek protection from Chinese overcapacity. @Brad_Setser @Mike_Weilandt @BaurVolk
articles.author: Brad Setser, Volkmar Baur and Michael Weilandt

articles.title: Phil Gramm and John Early note that the "Gender Pay Gap" is largely driven by female preference for fewer hours worked and lower-paying professions.
articles.author: Phil Gramm and John Early

articles.title: Wage returns to cognitive skill have declined for white men who took the Armed Forces Qualifying Test (AFQT) in 1997 relative to 1979, driven primarily by a decline in scores at the bottom of the skill distribution.
articles.author: Judith Hellerstein, Sai Luo and Sergio Urzúa

articles.title: A new analysis estimates that the recent surge in immigration lifted real GDP growth by 0.1 percentage point in 2022 and 2023 relative to a baseline without the surge and likely will do the same in 2024. @WendyEdelberg @taraelizwatson
articles.author: Wendy Edelberg and Tara Watson

articles.title: According to new IRS data, during 2019-21 California's economy created millionaires faster than they fled: its share of US taxpayers with adjusted gross income of $1M or higher rose almost 1pp from 17% to 17.9%. @foxjust
articles.author: Justin Fox

articles.title: During 2003-19, the mean EBIT margin of US public companies increased by 11.5% vs. 1984-2002, driven by a 47.4% increase in foreign profitability for S&P 500 firms, while domestic profitability remained flat.
articles.author: Bullipe Chintha, Ravi Jagannathan and Sri Sridhar

articles.title: According to IMF data China generated 28% of global savings in 2023 just under the 33% share the US and EU generated. @Brad_Setser notes these savings are driving a trade surplus of 4% of GDP, a post-crisis high.
articles.author: Martin Wolf

articles.title: .@gchodorowreich @omzidar Eric Zwick and Matthew Smith find firms that benefited from the corporate tax cuts in the 2017 Tax Cuts and Jobs Act responded with a 20% increase in investment relative to firms that were not impacted.
articles.author: Gabriel Chodorow-Reich, Matthew Smith, Owen Zidar and Eric Zwick

articles.title: According to a @stlouisfed analysis, the 2022 wealth of older millennials (born between 1980-89) was 37% above expectations based on prior generations at the same age, largely driven by real estate gains.
articles.author: Ana Hernández Kent and Lowell Ricketts
articles.pub: Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis

articles.title: While excess Chinese capacity is contributing to lower import prices, the US, EU, and Japan are seeking to avoid another “China Shock” with subsidies and tariffs to buttress their economies from a flood of cheap Chinese goods. @WSJ
articles.author: Jason Douglas

articles.title: Of the almost $1T in Federal pandemic-era fiscal aid to states, 38% went to general government expenditures (excluding healthcare, education, and infrastructure) and 7% on employee pension funding. @stanveuger @joshrauh @jeffreypclemens @og_giesecke
articles.author: Stan Veuger, Jeffrey Clemens, Oliver Giesecke and Joshua Rauh

articles.title: An Economist analysis argues "The surge in net profits is really an artifact of lower taxes and interest bills. Measures of underlying profits have grown less impressively." This suggests lower returns going forward.
articles.author: Economist Staff

articles.title: Between 2009 and 2021 Russel 3000 firms didn’t respond to low interest rates and a lower weighted average cost of capital by raising their investment in capex, M&A, and investment SG&A relative to 1996-2008. @mjmauboussin
articles.author: Michael Mauboussin and Dan Callahan

articles.title: Data compiled by Robert Dugger and @ChicagoFed researchers Robert Barsky and Matthew Easton suggest that the global savings glut plateaued just after the 2007-09 financial crisis.
articles.author: Rich Miller

articles.title: Science is not getting less disruptive over time; an earlier finding that the pace of disruptiveness has slowed was the result of data errors.
articles.author: Vincent Holst, Andres Algaba, Floriano Tori, Sylvia Wenmackers and Vincent Ginis

articles.title: .@Birdyword @econcallum argue rumors of Sino-US economic decoupling are overstated citing a rise in China’s exports of intermediate goods and discrepancies between the two countries’ trade data.
articles.author: Mike Bird and Callum Williams

articles.title: According to a @Lymanstoneky analysis about one-third of today’s 18 and 19 year old young Americans will not have married by the time they turn 45 in 2050.
articles.author: Lyman Stone

articles.title: According to a @sffed analysis, real household wealth, including unrealized capital gains, is back in line with pre-pandemic projections as of Q3 2023. @ah_shapiro
articles.author: Hamza Abdelrahman, Luiz Edgard Oliveira, and Adam Shapiro
articles.pub: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco

articles.title: US consumer sentiment is significantly lower than expected based on unemployment and inflation. Alternative measures of inflation that include borrowing costs can account for almost three quarters of this gap. @MA_Bolhuis @juddcramer @LHSummers
articles.author: Marijn Bolhuis, Judd Cramer, Karl Oskar Schulz and Lawrence Summers

articles.title: Removing artificial constraints to building lowers housing costs argues @jburnmurdoch: “Homes in Texan cities are cheap and their populations soaring because the state has made urban development easy.”
articles.author: John Burn-Murdoch

articles.title: As long as the chronic surplus countries balance their trade with American assets the US has "no choice" but to run deficits.  The most likely response to beggar-thy-neighbor trade policies will be additional tariffs or capital controls. @michaelxpettis
articles.author: Michael Pettis

articles.title: As of December 2023, real wages for the US middle quintile are 6.6% higher than they were in 2019 and above the level implied by the 2015-2019 trend. The US is the only G7 country that experienced substantial real wage gains in recent years. @ArinduB
articles.author: Arindrajit Dube

articles.title: The Nikkei 225 is back at its 1989 peak after 34 years. During that period Japan’s real per capita GDP fell to the lowest of its peers in the G7, and the mean age in Japan rose from 37.2 to 48.9.
articles.author: Kana Inagaki and Leo Lewis

articles.title: In 2021 344 unicorns were minted; in 2023 only 45 new firms met that threshold. There are 700 unicorns with a valuation of $2.4T that have yet to IPO or be acquired.
articles.author: Economist Staff

articles.title: In Sweden and Denmark, a 1pp increase in the mean wealth tax reduced the stock of rich taxpayers by 2%. When an entrepreneur subject to the wealth tax emigrates, employment in their businesses drops by 33%, and tax payments by 50%. @MathildeMunoz
articles.author: Katrine Jakobsen, Henrik Kleven, Jonas Kolsrud, Camille Landais and Mathilde Muñoz

articles.title: Millennials' inflation-adjusted per capita wealth closely mirrors that of boomers, yet the economic landscape has shifted, making their financial success bittersweet.
articles.author: John Burn-Murdoch

articles.title: Excess savings from deficit-financed fiscal transfers influence the economy for up to 5 years as they trickle up to individuals with lower marginal propensities to consume.
articles.author: Adrien Auclert, Matthew Rognlie and Ludwig Straub

articles.title: Americans spent 11.3% of their disposable income on food in 2022, the highest level since 1991, driven by a rise in both restaurant and grocery prices.
articles.author: Jesse Newman and Heather Haddon

articles.title: Criminal behavior follows a power law distribution, where a small fraction of offenders are responsible for a large portion of crimes.
articles.author: Inquisitive Bird

articles.title: The EU's GDP/working age population has remained ~32% lower than the US's since 1995, as rising working-age employment has been offset by declining GDP/person employed. @ChrisGiles_
articles.author: Chris Giles

articles.title: US productivity growth has averaged 3.9% over the past three quarters, over 3X the 2009-2019 rate. The Fed's long-term base case productivity growth remains at 1.5%.
articles.author: Steve Matthews

articles.title: Students at Ivy-Plus colleges with 99th percentile SAT/ACT scores earned a first-year college GPA 0.43 points higher than students who scored at the 75th percentile, ~ 1200 SAT. @John_N_Friedman
articles.author: John Friedman, Bruce Sacerdote, and Michele Tine

articles.title: After a 2012 increase in marginal tax rates of up to 3% for high-income California households 0.8% of the impacted tax base left the state in 2013 and others restructured income, eroding 61% of "windfall revenues" within the first 2 years. @joshrauh
articles.author: Joshua Rauh and Ryan Shyu

articles.title: .@jmhorp argues the rise in both market and post-tax/transfer income by generation documented by @jefflarrimore @kevincorinth is not driven by the growth of dual-income families.
articles.author: Jeremy Horpedahl

articles.title: David Tinsley @BankofAmerica finds for the first time in US history the West’s population share is declining and suggests that relative housing affordability is a key factor.
articles.author: David Tinsley

articles.title: .@jefflarrimore @kevincorinth find that Millennials between the ages of 36-40 have 18% higher real median household incomes (net of taxes and transfers) than Generation X at the same age range and that Millennial’ market incomes were 14% higher.
articles.author: Kevin Corinth and Jeff Larrimore

articles.title: Female prime-age LFP has exceeded its pre-pandemic peak hitting an all-time high last year, and male prime-age LFP returned to its pre-pandemic level. A @sffed analysis argues LFP gains are "winding down" which implies sustained tight labor markets.
articles.author: Robert Valletta
articles.pub: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco

articles.title: The decline in internal migration in the US has been driven by increasing housing prices; potential wage gains from migration are being offset by housing-related factors.
articles.author: William Olney and Owen Thompson

articles.title: Tariffs implemented during the 2018-2019 trade war were “at best a wash, and may have been mildly negative” in terms of employment, but increased political support for Trump in the 2020 election. @davidautor
articles.author: David Autor, Anne Beck, David Dorn, and Gordon Hanson

articles.title: .@TheEconomist notes that illegal immigration is as at a record under President Biden: nearly 250,000 crossed the border in November alone, compared to an average of 100,000/month in the 1980s through to the early 2000s.
articles.author: Economist Staff

articles.title: .@jburnmurdoch highlights a growing ideological gap between women and men under the age of 30: US women are now 30 percentage points more liberal than their male counterparts, with similar gaps in Germany and the UK.
articles.author: John Burn Murdoch

articles.title: The spread of information about drugs from person to person, and the ease and safety of acquiring drugs account for 84-92% of the rise in opioid deaths between 1990-2018, and drive the persistence of the epidemic. @Cutler_econ @travis_donahoe
articles.author: David Cutler and Travis Donahoe

articles.title: Rising electrical consumption from data centers, in part driven by AI demand, will be almost 6% of US electricity demand by 2026 according to @IEA forecast.
articles.author: Eren Çam, Zoe Hungerford, Niklas Schoch, Francys Pinto Miranda and Carlos David Yáñez de León

articles.title: As deficits surge towards 10% of GDP, @Brian_Riedl argues there is limited space to increase revenue by taxing the rich, which at most could raise an additional 2% of GDP over the long term.
articles.author: Brian Riedl

articles.title: There were 1.54mm housing completions in 2023, the highest level since 2007. While housing starts were down 9% y/y in 2023, @calculatedrisk forecasts that completions will increase this year.
articles.author: Bill McBride

articles.title: Comparing illegal immigration encounters at the south-western border under Trump and Biden the Economist argues "This is a clear win for Mr. Trump."
articles.author: Economist Staff

articles.title: Meta is spending $9B on 350,000 NvidiaH100 GPUs and buying another 250,000 H100 equivalents this year as the AI-driven capex cycle ramps up.
articles.author: Tae Kim

articles.title: The pandemic-era entrepreneurship boom has driven an increase in the share of workers employed by firms less than 5 years old to 11.3%, the highest since 2010.  @JosephPolitano
articles.author: Joseph Politano

articles.title: An @InnovateEconomy analysis of @uscensusbureau data finds that new business applications from firms that are likely employers hit 1.8mm in 2023, a new record high.
articles.author: Kenan Fikri and Daniel Newman

articles.title: Native-born employment is 6mm jobs under the pre-pandemic trend while foreign-born employment is back at trend.  Netting out 1mm Covid deaths Torsten Sløk @apolloglobal finds 5mm native-born workers are "missing."
articles.author: Torsten Sløk

articles.title: Over 10,000 research papers were retracted in 2023 globally according to @Nature, up from 400 in 2010.  The retractions are due to both increased scrutiny but also the rise of "paper writing mills" or low-quality journals.
articles.author: Anemona Hartocollis

articles.title: US electricity demand is booming after years of stagnation, driven by new manufacturing capacity and  data centers, prompting warnings over the stability of the power grid.
articles.author: Jamie Smyth and Eva Xiao

articles.title: Nicholas Eberstadt notes that the labor force participation rate for prime-age women is up 5pp since the pandemic to an all-time high, while the rate for prime-age men was up by 1pp to a level that matches the trough of the 1983 recession. @AEIecon
articles.author: Nicholas Eberstadt

articles.title: A @budgethawks analysis finds rising new spending relative to GDP drove about 2/3 of the growth in annual budget deficits since 2001 while declining revenue explains 1/3. Absent these policy choices debt would have been fully paid off.
articles.author: Marc Goldwein
articles.pub: Committee for a Responsible Federal Budget

articles.title: .@JohnHCochrane revisits 2008-2020 and argues the Fiscal theory of the price level has been more consistent with inflation’s behavior than Keynesians’ warnings about deflation spirals or monetarist’s predictions of hyperinflation.
articles.author: John Cochrane

articles.title: .@delong noting the 30X increase from 1982 to 2023 in the ratio of top wealth to median family income argues “the chances are 99% that there are big things wrong in the numbers in Auten/Splinter” that show stable after-tax top income shares since 1960.
articles.author: Brad DeLong

articles.title: Torsten Sløk @apolloglobal notes that since 2010 private credit assets under management (AUM) have increased by $800B, more than the $500B growth in high-yield debt.
articles.author: Torsten Sløk, Jyoti Agarwal, and Rajvi Shah

articles.title: Bloomberg estimates that a Sino-American War over Taiwan would reduce global GDP by 10.2% or $10T almost 2x the impact of the Covid-19 pandemic. US GDP would drop 6.7% and the PRC would face a 16.7% loss.
articles.author: Jennifer Welch, Jenny Leonard, Maeva Cousin, Gerard DiPippo, and Tom Orlik

articles.title: While Nvidia is increasing capex by 30% and Microsoft by 20%, excluding AI leaders, S&P 500 firms are planning on lifting capex by 2.5% in 2024 defying projections of an broader AI-driven capex boom. @TheEconomist
articles.author: Economist Staff

articles.title: Between 2010 and 2022 so-called deaths of despair “rose by 5.5% per year in counties with lots of high-school-educated whites, but by 7% in the most diverse, educated ones.” @TheEconomist
articles.author: Economist Staff

articles.title: Noting that test scores are predictive of academic and professional outcomes @DLeonhardt argues that standardized tests are the most meritocratic talent identification method. Raj Chetty notes, "The SAT is picking up fundamentals."
articles.author: David Leonhardt

articles.title: Under the Biden administration, more than 2.3mm migrants have been released into the United States. More than 6mm were taken into custody and 4mm migrants were expelled to Mexico or their home countries.
articles.author: Maria Sacchetti and Nick Miroff

articles.title: Fundraising by US venture capital firms declined 60% y/y in 2023 to $67B, the lowest level since 2017.
articles.author: George Hammond

articles.title: Since the start of the pandemic real economic output in Texas has increased $230B, close to the $270B growth in the larger California economy. @JosephPolitano
articles.author: Joseph Politano

articles.title: The disconnect between strong employment numbers and consumer sentiment has likely been driven by real income growth being under its 1980-2019 average since Q1 2022; however real income growth has started to accelerate. @stlouisfed
articles.author: Charles Gascon and Joseph Martorana
articles.pub: Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis

articles.title: Both PSZ and AS show the same trend in US redistribution: a dramatic increase over the past 60 years driven by transfers to the bottom half of the US population. @WeisbachD
articles.author: Thomas Coleman and David Weisbach

articles.title: .@caroartc and @Viquibarone find that greater exposure to the opioid epidemic has continuously increased the GOP vote share starting around 2000.
articles.author: Carolina Arteaga and Victoria Barone

articles.title: French workers who joined the technology sector from 1994-2002 earned 6% lower wages 15 years out relative to similar workers starting in other sectors, presumably driven by faster skill obsolescence. @johanhombert @AdrienMatray
articles.author: Johan Hombert and Adrien Matray

articles.title: Concerns about climate change's impact on the US Southwest are overstated: Arizona uses 3% less water than it did in 1957 despite the population growing 555% argues @tomzoellner.
articles.author: Tom Zoellner

articles.title: Based on cross-sectional data from 93 countries, low-income individuals’ redistributive preferences are more predictive of redistribution policy than elite preferences. @alain_cohn @RFisman
articles.author: Michel Marechal, Alain Cohn, Jeffrey Yusof, and Raymond Fisman

articles.title: David Splinter argues that @WilliamGale2 John Sabelhaus @samthorpe__ critique of AS misrepresents some of the results and AS’s headline finding that the US's long-run top 1% after-tax income share being relatively flat stands.
articles.author: David Splinter

articles.title: While "excess savings" have largely been drawn down US saving and spending rates have normalized relative to income. Relative to the pre-pandemic period "Americans are richer than before and have much lower debt than would be expected." @M_C_Klein
articles.author: Matt Klein

articles.title: .@verdadcap created a value metric on a blend of price vs. cash flow, earnings, book value, and sales, and found that half of equities in the “most expensive” quartile were in the same quartile the following year, while 30% dropped to the next quartile.
articles.author: Chris Satterthwaite

articles.title: Weighing in on the AS/PSZ debate @WilliamGale2 John Sabelhaus @samthorpe__ argue that "the preponderance of evidence suggests that income inequality has increased" in the US and other advanced economies.
articles.author: William Gale, John Sabelhaus, and John Sabelhaus

articles.title: David Splinter responds to Saez @PikettyWIL @gabriel_zucman comments: "Our headline finding of relatively flat long-run top 1% after-tax income shares is robust."
articles.author: David Splinter

articles.title: 87% of foreign money that flowed into China’s stock market in 2023 has already left.
articles.author: Hudson Lockett and Cheng Leng

articles.title: .@ojblanchard1 argues that reconciling higher spending needed to fight global warming and stabilizing debt levels will require fiscal consolidation yielding “some positive level of the ratio of primary balances to GDP.”
articles.author: Olivier Blanchard

articles.title: The S&P 500 was up 24% in 2023, with 52% of the increase in market capitalization driven by the Magnificent 7. As of August, 70% of S&P gains had been driven by the Magnificent 7.
articles.author: Jesper Rangvid

articles.title: According to an @ITIFdc analysis of 10 strategic industries, including pharmaceuticals, transportation equipment, and IT, China is the leading producer in 7 of 10 industries and has 25% of global output vs. 21% for the US.
articles.author: Robert Atkinson and Ian Tufts

articles.title: Saez @PikettyWIL @gabriel_zucman respond to Auten and Splinter's critique of their inequality findings.  They claim AS underallocated a large share of untaxed business and capital income to the top 1%, singificantly understating the rise of inequality.
articles.author: Thomas Piketty, Emmanuel Saez, and Gabriel Zucman

articles.title: Maurice Obstfeld argues that the drivers of declining interest rates since 1980, such as low productivity growth and high safe asset demand relative to supply, have not been offset enough to drive a "big and durable rise in global real interest rates."
articles.author: Maurice Obstfeld

articles.title: 71% of American teenagers aged 13-17 visit Alphabet’s YouTube daily, and 58% visit ByteDance’s TikTok daily, with 17% of respondents saying they were on TikTok “Almost Constantly.”
articles.author: Monica Anderson, Michelle Faverio, and Jeffrey Gottfried

articles.title: .@verdadcap @verdadcap_quant argue that private equity returns are primarily driven by the small cap asset class returns showing that random samples of the cheapest 5% and the most profitable 5% levered micro-cap stocks produce similar returns to private equity.
articles.author: Brian Chingono and Dan Rasmussen

articles.title: Citing Gerald Auten and David Splinter’s recent work that found after taxes and transfers the income of the bottom half of the distribution has increased by two-thirds, Mark Warshawsky @AEIecon argues the case for further redistribution is weak.
articles.author: Mark Warshawsky

articles.title: A @WSJ interview with Jensen Huang, founder of @Nvidia, highlights why motivating properly trained talent to bear risk is foundational to growth. Failure is by far the most likely outcome of high-impact entrepreneurial efforts.
articles.author: Ben Cohen

articles.title: Noting Americans are less likely to want to address inequality via redistribution, @jburnmurdoch wonders if "seeing inequality can equate to seeing opportunity, the American dream makes US society more tolerant of large disparities."
articles.author: John Burn-Murdoch

articles.title: After exposure to a narrative that “the economy is rigged,” survey participants in Australia, France, Germany, Switzerland, and the United Kingdom showed increased support for redistribution, but no effect in the US. @jung_jaehee_
articles.author: Pepper Culpepper, Ryan Shandler, Jae-Hee Jung, and Taeku Lee

articles.title: China's "slow motion financial crisis" wasn't that slow, as highlighted by a recent Barclays report.  In the aftermath of Evergrande half of China's major developers have defaulted on at least $140bn of dollar bonds.
articles.author: Bryce Elder

articles.title: There is evidence the opioid epidemic was a supply shock: states with higher levels of imports, and more opportunities for smuggling, saw higher levels of fentanyl deaths between 2017 and 2020. @benconomics
articles.author: Timothy Moore, William Olney, and Benjamin Hansen

articles.title: Bridgewater forecasts that AI productivity will have a “Model T Effect” where consumers buy more of previously-unaffordable cognitive services, and shift spending to other forms of consumption.
articles.author: Greg Jensen, Lauren Simon, and Josh Moriarty

articles.title: As prices rise, lower-income households are disproportionately impacted as they generally buy products with lower prices and margins which are more sensitive to inflationary episodes. @ksangani8
articles.author: Kunal Sangani

articles.title: The EU is increasingly concerned about Chinese industrial overcapacity and the growing Sino-EU trade deficit, which has doubled in two years to just under $400B.
articles.author: Joe Leahy, Henry Foy, Andy Bounds, and Andy Lin

articles.title: .@PIIE estimates that at least $100B in FDI flowed out of China in 2023, as foreign firms are no longer reinvesting earnings, and are also selling their Chinese operations to local firms and repatriating the proceeds.
articles.author: Nicholas Lardy

articles.title: Breaking down the 2022 PISA math results by subpopulation, @cremieuxrecueil finds that US Asians and whites topped the charts, scoring on par with top international performers.
articles.author: Crémieux

articles.title: Between 2019 and 2021 government spending on social benefits rose by 47% but the official poverty rate rose from 10.5% to 11.6%. Phil Gramm and John Early note that 88 social benefits are excluded from poverty measures.
articles.author: Phil Gramm and John Early

articles.title: Mexico has outpaced China and Canada as the US's largest trading partner.
articles.author: Christine Murray

articles.title: Chris Satterthwaite @verdadcap argues the decline in US small-cap gross profit/assets has been driven by the recent IPOs of unprofitable firms during the SPAC boom of 2021.
articles.author: Chris Satterthwaite

articles.title: .@delong cites evidence that wealth inequality has increased markedly since 1982, and argues that, since wealth and income fluctuate together, it's implausible that top-income shares have not risen.
articles.author: Brad DeLong

articles.title: .@paulkrugman notes that Average Hourly Earnings have outpaced CPI since 2019, and argues "most workers’ wages have risen significantly more" than inflation.
articles.author: Paul Krugman

articles.title: Victoria Udalova notes that, according to the Bureau of Economic Analysis, the share of American GDP spent on health care declined for the second straight year to 17.1% in 2022, the lowest level since 2014.
articles.author: Victoria Udalova

articles.title: Intergenerational income mobility in US is greater than in the 19th and early 20th century, as previous estimates overestimated historical mobility due to bad samples and measurement error.
articles.author: Zachary Ward

articles.title: The US won the War on Poverty on LBJ’s terms, cutting the absolute full-income poverty rate from 19.5% in 1963 to 1.6% in 2019. During these years the share of working-age adults who earned < half of their income from market sources more than doubled.
articles.author: Richard Burkhauser, Kevin Corinth, James Elwell, and Jeff Larrimore

articles.title: While Americans are now seeing real wage gains vs. the start of 2020, the price level is up 19.3%, about as much as the cumulative rise in prices between 2010-20.
articles.author: Reade Pickert and Jennah Haque

articles.title: Globally, demand for water is up 40% over the past forty years and will increase another 25% by 2050.  Since 1970 water supply has halved. @BankofAmerica
articles.author: Vanessa Cook

articles.title: The decline in trade as a percent of global GDP in the aftermath of the global financial crisis has largely been driven by China, as China's imports from the world have lagged both China's GDP growth and the growth in global trade. @arvindsubraman
articles.author: Arvind Subramanian, Martin Kessler, and Emanuele Properzi

articles.title: Han Feizi argues China's deepening pool of human capital will offset demographic drag. 40% of China’s college graduates are STEM majors.  Given current graduation rates, by 2043 China will have more STEM-educated workers than the rest of the world.
articles.author: Han Feizi

articles.title: Immigration restrictions on skilled immigration during Trump's first term drove an uptick in skilled immigration to Canada.  This inflow was associated with Canadian firms expanding in both domestic and foreign markets. @AgosBrinatti
articles.author: Agostina Brinatti and Xing Guo

articles.title: .@Brad_Setser finds “With reasonable adjustments, China's ‘true’ current account surplus might be $300B larger than China officially reports,” or around $800B.
articles.author: Brad Setser

articles.title: Japan achieved GDP growth per working-age adult of 31.9% between 1998 and 2019, slightly faster than the US at 29.5%. @King_ofSweden
articles.author: Jesus Fernandez-Villaverde, Gustavo Ventura, and Wen Yao

articles.title: China's surplus is growing relative to GDP. @M_C_Klein notes the negative employment consequences of this has been offset by US government spending which has sustained domestic American demand and shored up private sector balance sheets.
articles.author: Matt Klein

articles.title: 10.5mm unauthorized immigrants lived in the US in 2021, under the 2007 peak of 12.2mm. This represented about 3% of the total U.S. population and 22% of the foreign-born population, among the lowest shares since the 1990s.
articles.author: Jeffrey Passel and Jens Manuel Krogstad

articles.title: The New York Times editorial board notes that pandemic era education policy "may prove to be the most damaging disruption in the history of American education." They highlight an ongoing problem with chronic absenteeism.
articles.author: New York Times Editorial Board

articles.title: Microsoft is spending at least $50bn annually on data centers going forward to power its AI push. @dylan522p notes this is “the largest infrastructure buildout that humanity has ever seen.”
articles.author: Dylan Patel and Myron Xie

articles.title: .@GeneralTheorist forecasts that, absent fiscal consolidation, federal debt will reach 113% of GDP by 2030, and that gross financing needs will exceed $11 trillion every year from 2024-2030.
articles.author: Chris Marsh

articles.title: Citing that 40% of Russell 2000 firms have negative earnings, Torsten Sløk @apolloglobal argues that “if the economy enters a recession, a lot of middle-market companies will be vulnerable to the combination of high rates and slowing growth.”
articles.author: Torsten Sløk

articles.title: US voting behavior is becoming less racially polarized, as young black men swing from Biden to Trump. @jburnmurdoch @StackStrat
articles.author: John Burn-Murdoch

articles.title: The Treasury market will become increasingly stressed as the US runs record peacetime deficits at the same time the Fed is letting its portfolio run down $60B/month. @Brad_Setser notes foreign buyers are unlikely to step in at the pace supply is rising.
articles.author: Chelsey Dulaney and Megumi Fujikawa

articles.title: An @NBERpubs study finds that, unlike labor-saving technologies that drive earnings declines and job losses for all affected workers, labor-augmenting technologies disproportionately impact white-collar, older, and higher-paid workers. @DimitrisPapan20
articles.author: Leonid Kogan, Dimitris Papanikolaou, Lawrence Schmidt and Bryan Seegmiller

articles.title: New research from @ArnaudDyevre finds a 1% decline in public R&D spillovers caused a 0.17% decline in productivity growth in the United States.  Notably, the decline in public R&D can explain a third of the deceleration in TFP since 1995.
articles.author: Arnaud Dyevre

articles.title: Updated work by @davidautor documents that, since the pandemic, real hourly wage gains for the bottom 10% have reversed nearly 40% of the cumulative increase in inequality since 1980. @nberpubs
articles.author: David Autor, Arindrajit Dube, and Annie McGrew

articles.title: .@verdadcap uses Gross Profits/Assets as a proxy for quality, and notes that there has been a sharp deterioration in quality for US small caps.  Chris Satterthwaite writes that foreign small caps have similar quality but lower price than US small caps.
articles.author: Chris Satterthwaite

articles.title: Analysis by Loukas Karabarbounis finds that labor’s share of US national income declined 5pp btw 1929-2022, and labor’s share globally has declined 6pp since 1980. He proposes capital-augmenting technology as the most likely driver of these declines.
articles.author: Loukas Karabarbounis

articles.title: Wage growth for workers in the bottom quartile dropped to 5.9% in October from 7.2% in January, according to data from the Federal Reserve Bank of Atlanta.
articles.author: Amara Omeokwe

articles.title: Real hourly compensation is above 2019 levels but below the 2015-19 trend line.  @JosephPolitano
articles.author: Joseph Politano

articles.title: Productivity in America’s manufacturing sector has been in decline since 2011.  Fourteen out of nineteen manufacturing sub-sectors, from machinery to textiles, saw declines during the 2010s.
articles.author: Economist Staff

articles.title: An @AEI survey finds Gen Z is socially isolated relative to prior generations with only 56% of them reporting having had a romantic partner as a teenager, compared with 69% of Millennials and 78% of Baby Boomers.
articles.author: Daniel Cox, Kelsey Eyre Hammond, and Kyle Gray

articles.title: Discretionary spending is not driving federal deficits: defense spending is at its lowest share of GDP since World War 2, and @Brian_Riedl projects discretionary spending will likely soon start falling as a % of GDP.
articles.author: Brian Riedl

articles.title: Gerald Auten and David Splinter have updated their income inequality estimate and find that the after-tax income share of the top 1% in the US has been steady since the 1960s, disproving Piketty-Saez-Zucman’s claims otherwise.
articles.author: Gerald Auten and David Splinter

articles.title: China accounts for 18% of global GDP, 13% of global consumption, and 32% of global investment.  If China grows at 4-5% and maintains its current ratio of investment to consumption, its share of investment would rise to 37%. @michaelxpettis
articles.author: Michael Pettis

articles.title: .@ojblanchard1 writes that the rapid and unexpected move up in rates means that advanced economies will need to move towards primary balance quickly to avoid an explosion of debt-to-GDP ratios.
articles.author: Olivier Blanchard

articles.title: Robert Barro and @Francesco_Bia find that 40-50% of pandemic-era OECD government financing came from the effect of unexpected inflation on the real value of public debt, consistent with the fiscal theory of the price level.
articles.author: Robert Barro and Francesco Bianchi

articles.title: Foreign firms repatriated at least $160B of their Chinese earnings over the past 6 quarters. Historically, foreign firms have reinvested their Chinese earnings.
articles.author: Jason Douglas and Weilun Soon

articles.title: $1.3T of speculative-grade corporate borrowing is facing refinancing over the next three years, at rates more than double their 2021 lows. Moody’s predicts the US default rate will peak at 5.4% in January but could soar as high as 14%.
articles.author: Harriet Clarfelt

articles.title: Trump is leading Biden in five of six swing states driven by a decline in Biden support among young non-white voters. @Nate_Cohn
articles.author: Nate Cohn

articles.title: A @washingtonpost analysis finds 34.6% of American women 25-44 have never had a child, up 13pp from 1980. Single-child families have stayed steady at ~ 20% over that time period, while families with 4 or more children declined significantly.
articles.author: Andrew Van Dam

articles.title: Torsten Sløk @apolloglobal notes that over the last decade, the foreign ownership of US government bonds has fallen from 33% to 23%.
articles.author: Torsten Sløk

articles.title: A new @stlouisfed piece contends that the Japanese government takes on both duration and currency risk as it borrows short to invest in long-duration assets including foreign equities, domestic equities, and foreign debt. @HannoLustig
articles.author: YiLi Chien, Harold Cole and Hanno Lustig
articles.pub: Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis

articles.title: China is offsetting reduced exports to the US and Europe with exports to the rest of the world, especially Brazil, Russia, India, and South Africa.
articles.author: Jason Douglas and Tom Fairless

articles.title: In response to higher wages associated with the pandemic, the US restaurant sector has significantly improved productivity: from a 1.2%/year rate from 2014-20, productivity jumped 21% in 2021 and has retained most of those gains. @foxjust
articles.author: Justin Fox

articles.title: Noting for the past 15 years R < G,  @MarcGoldwein  argues that in a world where R > G, at current debt stocks and primary deficits, the US will face a debt spiral.
articles.author: Marc Goldwein
articles.pub: Committee for a Responsible Federal Budget

articles.title: Since 1960, older Americans have increased their consumption relative to younger Americans. @petercoy, citing arguments by @Kotlikoff, writes that this has likely lowered domestic investment.
articles.author: Peter Coy

articles.title: Enrico Moretti, Michel Serafinelli and @GagliarL show that, among cities worldwide that were hit by shocks that caused a decline in manufacturing, those that had a high share of college-educated workers recovered faster.
articles.author: Luisa Gagliardi, Enrico Moretti and Michel Serafinelli

articles.title: US college graduates earn 40% more than their British counterparts despite comparable skill levels.  @jburnmurdoch argues that this is driven by “much higher and more lucrative demand” for talent in the US economy relative to the UK.
articles.author: John Burn-Murdoch

articles.title: Denmark is seeking to better integrate non-Danes into Danish society by demolishing social housing in neighborhoods with low income, low education, high unemployment, and high criminal convictions where at least half of the population is non-Western.
articles.author: Emma Bubola

articles.title: Analysis by the UN projects that by 2050, 35% of the world’s population aged 15-24 will live in Africa, up from 23% in 2023.
articles.author: Declan Walsh

articles.title: Overall GDP per hour worked in Europe is about 82% of US levels. German productivity per hour worked is on par with the US, and lower per capita GDP is “entirely due to fewer hours worked” according to @TimothyTTaylor.
articles.author: Tim Taylor

articles.title: Assets in closed-end private debt funds have risen from $0.5 trillion at the end of 2015 to $1.6 trillion in March of 2023.
articles.author: Katharine Hidalgo

articles.title: Driven by housing appreciation, real median family net worth hit a record high in 2022. @JosephPolitano
articles.author: Joseph Politano

articles.title: Carol Ryan @wsj reports that the cost of homeownership exceeds the cost of renting by 52%, as compared to 33% in the second quarter of 2006.
articles.author: Carol Ryan

articles.title: Between 2012-21, the total shareholder return for the S&P 500 was 16.6%, with 44% of the return driven by earnings per share growth, and multiple expansion contributing 42%. @mjmauboussin
articles.author: Michael Mauboussin and Dan Callahan

articles.title: Citing Christopher Jencks, @DLeonhardt notes the cost of America's current immigration policy is disproportionately borne by low-skilled American workers.
articles.author: David Leonhardt

articles.title: .@OppInsights data shows that 31% of the children of the top 1% of income score 1300 or above on the SAT, as compared to 2.4% of children of the bottom 20%.
articles.author: Claire Cain Miller

articles.title: Accounting for spillovers from other states, @stanveuger and @jeffreypclemens find that $900B federal pandemic aid translated to $878,000 of spending to create or preserve one state or local job for one year. @AEIecon
articles.author: Stan Veuger, Beatrice Lee, Jeffrey Clemens, and John Kearns

articles.title: A composition shift in illegal immigration over the past two years, from single men to family units, is putting stress on the border control system as they are harder to deport.
articles.author: Andrew Mollica, Alicia Caldwell, Michelle Hackman and Santiago Pérez

articles.title: According to IMF estimates, eurozone cumulative deficits will fall to 3.4% of GDP in 2023, relative to 6.3% in the US.  Aside from Italy, the crisis-era PIGS are running even lower deficits, ranging from 1.6% in Greece to 0.2% in Portugal.
articles.author: Paul Hannon

articles.title: Lower-income workers have seen real wage gains since the pre-pandemic period; however, higher-wage workers have seen low or zero real growth depending on the measure. @ChloeNEast @WendyEdelberg
articles.author: Chloe East, Wendy Edelberg, and Noadia Steinmetz-Silber

articles.title: Firms that benefited from the corporate tax cuts in the 2017 Tax Cuts and Jobs Act responded with increased investment relative to firms that were not impacted, according to @gchodorowreich @omzidar Eric Zwick and Matthew Smith.
articles.author: Gabriel Chodorow-Reich, Matthew Smith, Owen Zidar and Eric Zwick

articles.title: According to @damienics and @hzsong, China is heading toward a protracted debt crisis which will see 40% of local government financing vehicles default with total losses of $5T or ~30% of China's GDP. @MacroPoloChina
articles.author: Damien Ma and Houze Song

articles.title: Torsten Sløk @apolloglobal argues that the “Magnificent Seven” stocks that have led the S+P higher have become “more and more overvalued” given their high P-E ratio (45) and the recent run-up in rates.
articles.author: Torsten Sløk

articles.title: Michael Cembalest @jpmorgan notes NYC is above median on many measures of urban recovery since 2019, but nonetheless ranks only above Chicago/Detroit/San Francisco because of structural problems related to business conditions and fiscal health.
articles.author: Michael Cembalest

articles.title: .@GoldmanSachs estimates interest expenses for small businesses will be up 1pp by 2024 from 5.8% of revenues in 2021 to around 7% in 2024.  Despite that, the overall private sector is forecast to run a financial surplus of 4% of GDP.
articles.author: Manuel Abecasis and Spencer Hill

articles.title: The top 2% of male earners in the United States see their annual earnings pull away from the median male workers starting at age 25; these workers see strong earnings growth until age 55. @serdarozkanEN
articles.author: Serdar Ozkan
articles.pub: Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis

articles.title: A new @nberpubs analysis documents that the natural interest rate moves in a very wide range of 400-500 basis points across advanced economies.  There is a strong global factor in r*, such that natural rates tend to move together across countries.
articles.author: Josh Davis, Cristian Fuenzalida, Leon Huetsch, Benjamin Mills, and Alan Taylor

articles.title: According to @BankofAmerica microtransaction data gig work is rising. 3% of the banks' customers earned money from gig work in August, up from 2.7% in April and 2% prior to the pandemic.
articles.author: Anna Zhou

articles.title: John Fernald @sffed finds that the trajectory of productivity growth post-pandemic is consistent with its pre-pandemic productivity track since 2004.
articles.author: John Fernald and Huiyu Li
articles.pub: Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco

articles.title: Lower life expectancy for America’s poor is largely driven by opioids and gun deaths. Among the 10% of Americans who die youngest, the mean age of death is 36.
articles.author: John Burn-Murdoch

articles.title: The Defense Department manages schools for about 66,000 students and achieves proficiency outcomes far higher than national averages.  DoD schools performed better than any US state in 2022, in part because they avoided pandemic declines. @nytimes
articles.author: Sarah Mervosh

articles.title: .@MTabarrok argues that the decline in research productivity documented by @chadjonesecon @I_Am_NickBloom @johnvanreenen might be driven by misallocation of resources.
articles.author: Maxwell Tabarrok

articles.title: The American working-age population, and thus our potential labor force, is shrinking relative to the economy. Population aging is the main driver, as 4mm baby boomers, or 2% of the working-age population, turn 65 each year. @foxjust
articles.author: Justin Fox

articles.title: Evidence from Belgian btw 2002-14 suggests that large firms generate significant TFP spillovers to domestic suppliers.  Firms that start a serious relationship with a superstar firm increase their TFP by 8% three or more years after the relationship is formed.
articles.author: Mary Amiti, Cédric Duprez, Jozef Konings and John Van Reenen

articles.title: The average score on the ACT dropped to a 30-year low after being down .3 points in 2023, the sixth consecutive year of decline. @WSJ
articles.author: Joseph Pisani

articles.title: .@paulkrugman argues “high interest rates will almost surely crowd out private investment, hurting our long-term prospects," but doesn’t see paths for either federal spending reductions or “a tax increase that would make a large dent in the deficit.”
articles.author: Paul Krugman

articles.title: Among 400,000 people who graduated from Ivy League schools between 1970 and 2021, athletes earned 3.4% more than non-athletes over the course of their careers. @NateeA1
articles.author: Natee Amornsiripanitch, Paul Gompers, George Hu, et al.

articles.title: 46,020 Americans were killed in traffic accidents in the US in 2022, up 18% from pre-pandemic levels. This was driven primarily by rising Black per-capita fatality rates, which were 26% higher than for whites last year. @foxjust
articles.author: Justin Fox

articles.title: A @BudgetModel analysis argues the United States has two decades to undertake structural reforms to keep debt below 200% of GDP, a level which will be associated with a government default.
articles.author: Jagadeesh Gokhale and Kent Smetters

articles.title: .@swinshi notes a recent American Compass survey finds 96% of American workers are working full time or working the number of hours they prefer.  This suggests a labor market that is effectively matching workers to their preferences.
articles.author: Scott Winship

articles.title: Caroline Hoxby argues that Anne Case and Angus Deaton's recent findings on the divergence btw Americans with a BA and those without is largely driven by a compositional shift that has occurred as more Americans have graduated college.
articles.author: Caroline Hoxby

articles.title: Revised @BEA_News numbers show Americans saved $1.1T less over the last six years than previous estimates.
articles.author: Rich Miller and Reade Pickert

articles.title: .@LHSummers issues a warning that America's fiscal trajectory is leaving it little slack for meeting contingencies, "military or non-military."
articles.author: Larry Summers

articles.title: After accounting for Chinese inputs into industrial imports from other countries, US exposure to China in 2018 was 4x the headline level. @BaldwinRE @freeman_reb @Angel__Theo
articles.author: Richard Baldwin, Rebecca Freeman, and Angelos Theodorakopoulos

articles.title: US net investment has declined from its 1950-80 average of 10% of GDP to 5%.  @TimothyTTaylor argues that the cause is increased investment in information technology that depreciates more quickly than plant and equipment.
articles.author: Timothy Taylor

articles.title: In a study of nearly 10,000 US schools, @CEDR_US, @emily_r_morton, and @ajmceachin find that remote instruction during the pandemic was a primary driver of widening math achievement gaps in high-poverty districts. @HarvardCEPR
articles.author: Dan Goldhaber, Thomas Kane, Andrew McEachin, et al.
